▪ Sanskritisation & de-ritualisation: Srinivas — mobility by emulating twice-born norms; caste associations turn into interest groups.
▪ Structural deprivation: SC/ST over-represented among landless, MPI- poor — Ambedkar's 'graded inequality' .
▪ Caste in the market: Ex: caste capital in business networks; reservation debates in private sector.
▪ Atrocities: Ex: SC/ST (PoA) Act invoked yearly; NCRB atrocity data.
▪ New assertion: Dalit-Bahujan consciousness, sub-quota churn — Ex: SC sub-classification verdict (2024) permitting sub-quotas.
▪ Reproduced identity: Dipankar Gupta — caste as discrete plural hierarchies , not one ladder.
▪ Census salience: demand for a caste census to recalibrate affirmative action.

Way Forward: de-link caste from stigma while retaining data for justice · strengthen atrocity-law enforcement & rehabilitation of manual scavengers · promote inter-dining/inter-marriage via education · evidence-based recalibration of reservation.

Conclusion: Caste has not died but mutated — shedding ritual skin while acquiring political and economic muscle; the task is to dismantle its hierarchy of stigma without erasing the data justice still needs.

＋ 2026 ammo (T2): Census 2027 will enumerate caste (first since 1931; digital, "open-column" method field-tested) — shift your line from "demand for caste census" to "design & safeguards": data privacy (Census Act 1948), sub-categorisation (Rohini Commission), pressure on the Indra Sawhney 50% ceiling.
T3 · Communalism — Colonial Roots & Contemporary Politics (covers PYQ Q75; PYQ 2023/2017) [15m]

Intro (concept): Communalism = the belief that a religious community shares common secular (political-economic) interests antagonistic to another — a modern, political ideology , not mere religiosity ( Bipan Chandra ).

Intro (historical): W.C. Smith and Bipan Chandra trace organised communalism to colonial 'divide-and-rule' — separate electorates (1909), census enumeration, and the two-nation theory hardening fluid identities.
H1 — Roots & drivers:
H2 — Manifestations & antidotes:
▪ Colonial construction: Ex: Morley-Minto separate electorates 1909; communal award 1932.
▪ From riots to everyday othering: Ashutosh Varshney — inter-communal civic engagement dampens violence.
▪ Religiosity → communalism: T.K. Oommen / PYQ 2017 — faith politicised when leaders convert belief into a mobilising 'us vs them'.
▪ Ghettoisation & mistrust: Ex: Sachar Committee (2006) documented Muslim spatial-economic exclusion.
▪ Relative deprivation & elite competition: Paul Brass — instrumentalist 'riot-production' by political entrepreneurs.
▪ Secular constitutional design: Ex: Places of Worship Act 1991; Art 25–28.
▪ Socio-economic insecurity: unemployment, rumour, social media amplification. Ex: fake-news-triggered flashpoints.
▪ Composite culture as counter: syncretic traditions, inter-faith networks.
▪ Institutional weakness: partisan policing, delayed justice embolden riots.
▪ Rule-of-law response: Ex: Sri Krishna Commission (Mumbai 1992-93); model communal-harmony codes.

Way Forward: strengthen impartial policing & swift justice · build associational bridges ( Varshney ) · counter disinformation · address the deprivation that feeds insecurity · protect secular statutes.

Conclusion: Communalism is a colonial inheritance kept alive by contemporary politics — its cure lies less in condemning faith than in dense civic bonds, even-handed institutions, and inclusive development.
T4 · Indian Secularism & the Places of Worship Act (covers PYQ Q17/Q50; PYQ 2022/2020/2019/2018) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · Places of Worship case live

Intro (comparative): Western secularism = wall of separation (US First Amendment; French laïcité ) and state non-interference; Indian secularism = 'principled distance' ( Rajeev Bhargava ) — equal engagement with all faiths, permitting reform.
Intro (concept): Indian secularism is 'sarva dharma sambhava' — equal respect, not equal indifference; the state may intervene to reform (temple entry, triple talaq) while guaranteeing Art 25–28 .
H1 — The Indian model & its distinctiveness:
H2 — Places of Worship Act & strains:
▪ Principled distance: Bhargava — context-sensitive engagement, unlike rigid separation. Ex: state manages temples, funds Haj/pilgrim boards.
▪ Freezing character: Places of Worship Act 1991 fixes religious character as on 15 Aug 1947 (Ayodhya excepted) to secure harmony.
▪ Reformist secularism: interference to end social evils. Ex: Sabarimala (2018); Triple Talaq Act 2019.
▪ Live litigation: SC special-bench hearings through 2025-26; survey suits at multiple sites test the Act.
▪ Basic-structure status: Ex: S.R. Bommai (1994) made secularism unamendable.
▪ Challenges in the name of secularism: cultural-practice friction ( PYQ 2019 ). Ex: Sabarimala backlash.
▪ Positive minority rights: Art 29–30 cultural-educational safeguards.
▪ Majoritarian vs minority anxiety: perceived asymmetry in reform.
▪ Tolerance–assimilation–pluralism as its making ( PYQ 2022 ).
▪ Judicial balancing: Ex: Ayodhya (2019) invoked the Act's constitutional- commitment value.

Way Forward: uphold the 1991 Act's harmony rationale · calibrate reform through dialogue not imposition · insulate secular statutes from majoritarian pressure · deepen 'principled distance' with even-handedness.

Conclusion: India's secularism is a home-grown, engaged model — neither anti-religion nor pro-any-religion; safeguarding statutes like the Places of Worship Act is essential to keeping that equilibrium of equal respect.
＋ 2026 ammo (T4): Places of Worship Act still sub judice mid-2026 — SC special bench seized; lower-court survey suits continue; write "litigation live", never a decided outcome.
T5 · Uniform Civil Code — Diversity vs Gender Justice (covers PYQ Q51; new (UCC debate live)) [15m] ★ near-certain · Art 44 + Uttarakhand UCC

Intro (concept): Art 44 (DPSP) urges a Uniform Civil Code — one secular code of marriage, divorce, succession — pitting personal-law pluralism against individual equality (Art 14/15) and gender justice.
Intro (event): Uttarakhand's UCC (2024, operational 2025) — the first state code — reopened the perennial debate the SC has flagged from Shah Bano (1985) to Sarla Mudgal and Shayara Bano (2017) .
H1 — The case for a UCC:
H2 — The case for caution:
▪ Gender justice: ends discriminatory personal-law provisions. Ex: Shah Bano; triple-talaq abolition 2019.
▪ Diversity & minority rights: feared threat to Art 25–30 cultural autonomy.
▪ Equality & fraternity: one nation-one civil law strengthens integration. Ex: Art 44 constituent-assembly vision.
▪ Consent over coercion: Ex: 21st Law Commission (2018) — 'not necessary at this stage', prefer reform-within.
▪ Secular citizenship: decouples civil status from religion.
▪ Tribal & regional exceptions: Ex: Sched. VI, customary laws of NE, HUF tax status.
▪ Legal simplicity: ends forum-shopping across personal laws. Ex: Sarla Mudgal (1995).
▪ Codify-then-uniformise: reform each personal law before merger.
▪ Constitutional signal: Ex: SC's repeated nudges to Parliament.
▪ Gender justice ≠ uniformity alone: patriarchy cuts across all communities.

Way Forward: pursue reform-within personal laws first · optional/opt-in model & wide consultation · protect tribal customary law · centre gender justice as the north-star, not majoritarian uniformity.

Conclusion: A UCC that advances equality without erasing diversity must be built by consensus and reform, not imposition — the goal is justice for women within a plural polity, not homogenisation of it.
T6 · Regionalism, Language & the Federal Fabric (covers PYQ Q76; PYQ 2024/2017/2016) [15m]

Intro (definition): Regionalism = assertion of a region's distinct identity/interests — positive (sub-national pride, federal bargaining) or negative (secession, 'sons-of-the-soil'); its basis is uneven development ( PYQ 2016 ).

Intro (historical): The States Reorganisation Act 1956 reconciled linguistic diversity with unity; regions as cultural units rather than states ( PYQ 2017 ) shows identity often overflows administrative lines.
H1 — Bases & drivers of regionalism:
H2 — Strengthens or weakens the union?:
▪ Development imbalance: unequal benefits breed grievance ( PYQ 2016/2024 ). Ex: demands for Telangana, Vidarbha, Bundelkhand.
▪ Federal deepening: channels identity democratically. Ex: linguistic states stabilised, not fragmented, India.
▪ Language & culture: linguistic pride. Ex: anti-Hindi agitations; three- language-formula friction.
▪ Cooperative federalism: Ex: NITI Aayog, Inter-State Council, GST Council.
▪ Ethnic sub-nationalism: Ex: North-East autonomy demands; FNTA/Frontier Nagaland 2026.
▪ Risk of fissures: when unmet, tips into secession/violence.
▪ Sons-of-the-soil: nativism vs migrants. Ex: domicile-based job reservation debates.
▪ Delimitation anxiety: North–South representation fear post-2026 census.
▪ Resource federalism: river-water & fiscal-devolution disputes.
▪ Balancing device: Ex: Sarkaria/Punchhi Commissions on Centre-State equilibrium.

Way Forward: equitable regional development (Aspirational Districts) · genuine fiscal federalism · accommodate identity via Art 371/Sched.VI · resolve delimitation with consensus to allay Southern fears.
Conclusion: Regionalism is the federal safety-valve of a continental democracy — corrosive when development is denied, but a source of vitality when identity is accommodated within a fair union.
T4A · Secularisation, Religiosity & Religious Revival — NEW process card (pairs with T4) [10/15m]

Intro (concept): Secularisation = the process by which religion loses social significance (Bryan Wilson); in India it is paradoxical — rituals shorten and beliefs privatise even as public religiosity, pilgrimage and religious markets boom.

Intro (thinker): M.N. Srinivas saw the "secularisation of caste"; T.N. Madan ("Secularism in its Place") and Ashis Nandy argue imported secularism misfits a believing society; Peter Berger recanted secularisation theory — the world is "as furiously religious as ever".
H1 — Evidence of secularisation:
H2 — Evidence of revival & why both coexist:
▪ Ritual economising: shortened ceremonies, priest-on-app, self-respect marriages recognised by courts.
▪ Public religiosity booms: mega-pilgrimages (Kumbh economics), corridor projects, temple economy & religious tourism.
▪ Differentiation: education, law, medicine progressively de-linked from religious authority (Weberian rationalisation).
▪ Godmen & deras: charisma routinised into organisations (Weber) — filling welfare & identity gaps the state leaves.
▪ Privatisation of belief: faith as personal spirituality among urban youth; interfaith workplaces.
▪ Digital religiosity: online darshan, devotional apps, festival streaming — technology amplifies, not erases, faith (T19).
▪ Caste secularised: from ritual status to interest-group politics (Srinivas; T2).
▪ Identity assertion: religiosity as community identity under competitive politics (T3) — Durkheim: religion as social solidarity.
Way Forward: open any such answer by distinguishing secularisation (social process) from secularism (state principle, T4) · argue coexistence — rationalisation of means, revival of meaning.

Conclusion: India is not becoming irreligious but differently religious — faith is privatising, digitising and politicising at once; the sociological task is to explain the coexistence, not to pick a winner.
SECTION B · ROLE OF WOMEN & GENDER — WORK, EMPOWERMENT, FAMILY
T7 · Women's Work — LFPR, Care Economy & Gig (covers PYQ Q16/Q47/Q48; PYQ 2024/2021) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · fresh data

Intro (data): Female LFPR has climbed sharply ( PLFS 2023-24 ) yet women do the bulk of unpaid care — Time Use Survey : women ~ 289–363 min/day unpaid domestic/care work vs men's fraction — a 'care penalty' .

Intro (concept): The 'feminisation-U' and the double burden : rising participation is largely distress-driven and self-employed/agri, while the care economy remains invisible in GDP ( Nancy Folbre , Devaki Jain ).
H1 — The paradox & barriers:
H2 — Gig economy: opportunity & risk:
▪ Unpaid-care skew: disproportionate domestic burden depresses paid work. Ex: Economic Survey time-use data.
▪ Flexible entry: platform work eases the mobility/time constraint ( PYQ 2021 gig+women ). Ex: women ~28% of gig workforce (LEAD).
▪ Low economic participation: India 131/146 on WEF Global Gender Gap 2025 , weak on economic sub-index.
▪ Algorithmic precarity: no social security, rating-based insecurity, 'she- cession' risk.
▪ Occupational segregation & pay gap: informal, low-wage 'pink-collar' concentration.
▪ Empowerment via SHGs & DBT: Ex: NRLM / Lakhpati Didi; DBT to women's Jan-Dhan accounts.
▪ Safety & mobility: Ex: Justice Verma Committee (2013) on workplace safety.
▪ Recognising care: measuring & sharing unpaid work; crèche mandates.
▪ Motherhood penalty: exit at marriage/childbirth; missing childcare.
▪ Formalisation: Ex: e-Shram registration; Code on Social Security 2020 covers gig/platform workers.
Way Forward: count & redistribute unpaid care (public childcare, paternity leave) · portable social security for gig workers · close the pay gap & safe transport · skilling for higher-value work.

Conclusion: Women's rising visibility in the labour market is hollow without valuing the care work that subsidises it — recognising, reducing and redistributing unpaid care is the real frontier of women's economic empowerment.

＋ 2026 ammo (T7): upgrade 3R → ILO 5R: Recognise → Reduce → Redistribute → Reward → Represent; Time Use Survey 2024: women ~289 min/day unpaid domestic work vs men ~88; WEF Gender Gap 2025: 131/146 (check 2026 edition).
FLOW — care-economy answer skeleton (hand-repeatable):
Unpaid care (TUS 289 vs 88 min) → depresses FLFPR → double burden / motherhood penalty
→ POLICY 5R: Recognise (GDP-satellite count) → Reduce (creches, water, LPG) → Redistribute
(paternity leave) → Reward (paid care jobs) → Represent (care-worker voice) → FLFPR ↑ → dividend
T8 · Gender Equality, Equity & Women's Empowerment (covers PYQ Q49; PYQ 2024/2019) [10/15m] ★ cross-confirmed · concepts asked 2024

Intro (definition): Gender equality = same rights/opportunities; gender equity = fairness accounting for historical disadvantage (differentiated means); empowerment = expanding women's agency and choice ( Naila Kabeer — resources, agency, achievements).
Intro (thinker): Amartya Sen — development as freedom and women's agency ; 'missing women' frames gender as central to, not peripheral to, human development.
H1 — Why gender-lens in policy design:
H2 — Persisting deficits & barriers:
▪ Equity ≠ equality: neutral schemes reproduce disadvantage — need gender budgeting (since 2005-06). Ex: Gender Budget Statement.
▪ Leadership gap: women underrepresented in boards, judiciary, bureaucracy ( PYQ 2024 ). Ex: <15% women in Parliament pre-quota.
▪ Agency & capabilities: Sen/Kabeer — empowerment as expanded choice, not welfare handouts.
▪ Patriarchy & son-preference: skewed sex ratio at birth 929 ( NFHS-5 ); Ex: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao.
▪ Multiplier effect: Ex: educating/empowering women lowers TFR, raises child health (NFHS-5).
▪ Structural violence: dowry, domestic violence. Ex: PWDVA 2005; NCRB data.
▪ Representation: Ex: Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (106th CAA, 2023) — 33% women's reservation in legislatures.
▪ Political empowerment paradox: 'sarpanch-pati' proxy despite 73rd- Amdt quotas.
▪ Constitutional mandate: Art 15(3) enabling special provisions.
▪ Committee lens: Ex: High-Level Committee on Status of Women (Pam Rajput, 2015) on continuing inequality.
Way Forward: mainstream gender budgeting & gender-disaggregated data · translate reservation into real agency (anti-proxy safeguards) · tackle son- preference & violence · fund care infrastructure.
Conclusion: Empowerment is not welfare delivered to women but agency exercised by them — a state that designs for equity, not mere equality, converts constitutional promise into lived freedom.
T9 · The Women's Question — Reform & National Movement (covers PYQ Q37 crossover; PYQ 2017) [15m]

Intro (historical): The women's question arose within the 19th-c. social-reform movement ( PYQ 2017 ) — Rammohan Roy (sati abolition 1829), Vidyasagar (widow-remarriage 1856), Phule–Savitribai (girls' schooling), Ranade , Syed Ahmad Khan .
Intro (thinker): Partha Chatterjee — nationalism resolved the women's question by splitting 'ghar/bahir' (home/world) : women as custodians of an inner spiritual domain, delaying full emancipation.
H1 — Reform-era issues & debates:
H2 — Women in the freedom struggle:
▪ Social evils targeted: sati, child marriage, widow celibacy, purdah, denial of education. Ex: Age of Consent Act 1891.
▪ Mass entry via Gandhi: Ex: Salt Satyagraha, picketing, Quit India — Kasturba, Sarojini Naidu.
▪ Reform vs revival: liberal reformers vs revivalists invoking scriptural sanction.
▪ Organised feminism: Ex: AIWC (1927), Women's Indian Association.
▪ Legislative reform: Ex: Widow Remarriage Act 1856; Sharda Act 1929.
▪ Radical participation: Ex: Kalpana Dutt, Pritilata Waddedar, Aruna Asaf Ali, Rani Gaidinliu.
▪ Women's own agency: Pandita Ramabai, Tarabai Shinde ('Stri Purush Tulana') — early feminist voices.
▪ From reform to rights: franchise & equality demands feed into the Constitution (Art 14/15/15(3)).
▪ Colonial ambivalence: reform used to legitimise the 'civilising mission'.
▪ Continuity: reform-era groundwork enabled post-independence codification (Hindu Code Bills).
Way Forward: recover women's own agency in history-writing · link reform legacy to present gender-justice statutes · use the movement's memory to legitimise equal citizenship.

Conclusion: The women's question began as a reformist project about women and matured into a movement by women — a trajectory from being objects of reform to subjects of rights that still frames India's gender agenda.
T10 · Marriage, Family & Kinship in Transition (covers PYQ Q73/Q80; PYQ 2023/2022) [10/15m]

Intro (concept): The Indian family is shifting from joint to nuclear/'functionally-joint' forms; marriage moves from sacrament (sanskara) to companionate contract ( PYQ 2023 ) under urbanisation, education and women's earning.
Intro (thinker): I.P. Desai & A.M. Shah on household structure; M.S. Gore on urbanisation's impact — kinship networks persist even as co-residence shrinks ( 'network family' ).
H1 — Drivers of change:
H2 — Continuities & strains:
▪ Women's economic independence: delayed marriage, mate choice, dual- earner homes. Ex: rising age at marriage (NFHS-5).
▪ Endogamy persists: arranged marriage, caste/religious boundaries stay strong.
▪ Urbanisation & migration: nuclearisation, WFH reshaping roles ( PYQ 2022 ).
▪ Elder care gap: nuclearisation strains support for the aged (links ageing card).
▪ Individualism & consumer culture: companionate ideal, live-in relationships, LGBTQ+ recognition ( Navtej Johar 2018 ).
▪ Isolation & anomie: Durkheim — weakened bonds; rising young-women suicide ( PYQ 2023 ).
▪ Technology in intimacy: dating apps, digital socialisation ( PYQ 2023 mobile/child ).
▪ Patriarchal residue: unequal domestic labour even in nuclear homes.
▪ Legal shifts: Ex: divorce reform debates; maintenance rights (Shah Bano lineage).
▪ Kinship resilience: Ex: festivals, remittances, digital family ties sustain networks.
Way Forward: family-friendly workplaces & elder-care systems · counsel against isolation/anomie · legal recognition of diverse family forms · balance autonomy with support functions.

Conclusion: The family is not disintegrating but reorganising — from co-resident joint households to dispersed kinship networks; policy must cushion the functions (care, security) that the shrinking household can no longer fully perform.
SECTION C · POPULATION, DEMOGRAPHY & ASSOCIATED ISSUES
T11 · Demographic Transition, Dividend & 'Winter' (covers PYQ Q14/Q15/Q43; PYQ 2024) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · HLC on Demography
Intro (data): India's TFR has fallen to ~2.0 — below the 2.1 replacement level — even as it is the world's most populous nation; a demographic dividend window projected to peak around 2041 .

Intro (concept): Demographic transition theory (high→low birth/death) meets the 'demographic winter' ( PYQ 2024 ) — sub-replacement fertility, ageing, shrinking workforce — a paradox of youth-surplus-now, ageing-soon .
H1 — The dividend & how to capture it:
H2 — Fertility divergence & the 'winter':
▪ Window & size: working-age share peaking ~2041; a one-time bonus if skilled/employed.
▪ North–South divide: Southern states already sub-replacement; North still transitioning — Ex: delimitation/representation anxiety (Q43).
▪ Skilling & jobs: Ex: Skill India, PLI-led manufacturing, apprenticeship push.
▪ Ageing onset: India may 'grow old before it grows rich' ( ORF ).
▪ Health & education base: Ex: NEP 2020; Ayushman Bharat to build human capital.
▪ Policy churn: High-Level Committee on Demographic Changes (2025-26) [verify] examining fertility/migration.
▪ FLFP unlock: raising women's participation could add materially to GDP ( McKinsey/Goldman estimates ).
▪ Migration valve: internal migration rebalances labour but strains cities (links urbanisation card).
▪ Risk of 'wasted dividend': jobless growth → demographic liability .
▪ From winter to design: pronatalist vs productivity focus — invest in productivity dividend , not just numbers.

Way Forward: front-load skilling, health & jobs before the window shuts · unlock FLFP · plan for ageing now · handle North-South delimitation with consensus · convert demographic to productivity dividend.

Conclusion: India's demography is a narrowing window, not a standing asset — the dividend materialises only with jobs, skills and women's participation; squander it, and the 'winter' of ageing arrives before prosperity does.

＋ 2026 ammo (T11): India ~146 cr, most populous (UN WPP); TFR ~1.9–2.0 (below replacement); NEW federal edge — 16th FC gives 10% weight to GDP contribution, sharpening the North–South "efficiency vs equity" debate that delimitation will inflame.
T12 · Ageing Society & Social Security (covers PYQ Q44; new) [10/15m]

Intro (data): India's 60+ population is rising fast — projected to roughly double to ~ 20% by 2050 ( Economic Survey / UNFPA India Ageing Report ) — 'ageing is no longer only a rich-country challenge' ( PYQ 2044 framing ).
Intro (concept): The 'greying' of a still-developing economy brings a shrinking support ratio, feminisation of ageing (widowed elderly women), and rural old-age vulnerability.
H1 — Challenges of an ageing India:
H2 — Adequacy of the response:
▪ Income insecurity: thin pension coverage; most elderly in informal sector. Ex: NSAP/IGNOAPS meagre pensions.
▪ Legal-scheme scaffold: Ex: MWPSC Act 2007; Atal Vayo Abhyudaya Yojana (AVYAY); SACRED portal.
▪ Health burden: rising NCDs, geriatric-care gap. Ex: NPHCE programme.
▪ Pension architecture: Ex: APY, NPS — low coverage in informal sector.
▪ Feminisation of ageing: widowed, dependent older women.
▪ Care economy for elderly: geriatric wards, elder day-care, home-care skilling.
▪ Family shift: nuclearisation erodes traditional elder care (links family card).
▪ Active-ageing model: re-employment, silver economy.
▪ Elder abuse & isolation: Ex: Maintenance & Welfare of Parents Act 2007.
▪ Data & planning: Ex: LASI (Longitudinal Ageing Study in India).
Way Forward: universalise a decent old-age pension floor · build geriatric & home-care systems · promote active/silver economy · strengthen family- support with state back-up.

Conclusion: India must age with dignity while still developing — pre-empting the ageing wave with pensions, geriatric care and an active-ageing vision, so longevity becomes a social achievement, not a fiscal shock.
T13 · Population Policy — Fertility, Education & Choice (covers PYQ Q74; PYQ 2019) [10m]

Intro (thinker): 'Development is the best contraceptive' (Karan Singh, Bucharest 1974) — fertility falls with women's education, autonomy and child survival , not coercion ( PYQ 2019 'empower women to control population' ).

Intro (data): The education–marriage-age–fertility chain ( PYQ Q74 ): each extra year of girls' schooling raises marriage age and lowers TFR — Ex: Kerala's high female literacy → early sub-replacement fertility.
H1 — Determinants of fertility decline:
H2 — Policy approach & cautions:
▪ Women's education & autonomy: delays marriage, expands choice. Ex: Kerala/Tamil Nadu model.
▪ Rights-based, not coercive: Ex: NPP 2000; lessons of 1975-77 emergency sterilisation.
▪ Child survival: falling IMR reduces 'insurance' births. Ex: NFHS-5 IMR decline.
▪ Two-child-norm debate: risks sex-selection & marginalising the poor.
▪ Contraceptive access: Ex: Mission Parivar Vikas; basket of choices.
▪ Regional targeting: high-TFR pockets need health-education investment, not penalties.
▪ Economic development & urbanisation: rising cost of children.
▪ Empowerment multiplier: Ex: SHGs, DBT raise women's bargaining power.
▪ Social security: reduces old-age-support motive for large families.
▪ Male responsibility: shift contraceptive burden, engage men.
Way Forward: invest in girls' education, health & jobs · rights-based family planning · avoid coercive/discriminatory norms · target high-fertility districts with services.
Conclusion: Population is best 'controlled' by expanding women's freedoms, not curbing them — education, health and autonomy are the demographic policy that works, humanely and durably.
SECTION D · POVERTY, DEVELOPMENTAL ISSUES & SOCIAL EMPOWERMENT
T14 · Poverty, Deprivation & Human Development (covers PYQ Q77; PYQ 2020/2018/2016) [15m]

Intro (thinker): Amartya Sen — poverty is capability deprivation , not mere low income; 'growth does not automatically translate into human development' ( PYQ Q77 ) needs public action on health/education.

Intro (concept): 'Liberating the poor from the process of deprivation' ( PYQ 2016 ) — structural, multidimensional poverty ( Alkire-Foster MPI ), not just the headcount below a Tendulkar/Rangarajan line.
H1 — Nature & measurement of poverty:
H2 — Growth vs human development:
▪ Multidimensional lens: Ex: NITI Aayog National MPI — health, education, living standards; ~135 mn exited MPI-poverty (claimed).
▪ Jobless growth: GDP rise without commensurate HDI gains. Ex: India's HDI rank lag.
▪ Income lines & debate: Ex: Tendulkar vs Rangarajan committee thresholds.
▪ Public provisioning: Ex: PDS/NFSA, MGNREGA, Ayushman Bharat as capability-builders.
▪ Deprivation as process: Sen — entitlement failure, not scarcity alone.
▪ Inequality: rising wealth concentration blunts poverty reduction.
▪ Social exclusion: caste/tribe/gender overlap with poverty (Béteille's marginality).
▪ Human-capital deficit: learning poverty, malnutrition ( Global Hunger Index concerns).
▪ COVID shock: pandemic deepened class inequality ( PYQ 2020 ).
▪ Targeting & leakage: Ex: DBT/JAM plugging delivery gaps.
Way Forward: shift from income-transfer to capability-building (health, learning, nutrition) · reduce inequality · quality jobs · direct benefits with last-mile inclusion.

Conclusion: Poverty is a denial of capabilities, and growth alone does not repair it — only public investment in human development and dismantling the structures of deprivation truly 'liberate the poor'.
T15 · Affirmative Action & Social Empowerment (covers PYQ Q78; PYQ 2024) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · affirmative-action asked 2024
Intro (committee): Reservation flows from Art 15(4)/16(4) and the Mandal Commission (1980, 27% OBC) , upheld in Indra Sawhney (1992) with the 50% ceiling and creamy-layer exclusion.

Intro (concept): 'Despite affirmative action, underprivileged sections don't get full benefits' ( PYQ 2024 ) — a gap between formal entitlement and substantive empowerment ( Marc Galanter, 'Competing Equalities' ).
H1 — Rationale & instruments:
H2 — Why benefits don't fully reach:
▪ Reparative justice: compensating historical exclusion. Ex: Ambedkar's constitutional safeguards.
▪ Elite capture / creamy layer: advantaged sub-groups corner gains.
▪ Representation: SC/ST/OBC in legislatures, services, education. Ex: Art 330/332; 105th CAA (OBC lists).
▪ Implementation deficit: backlog vacancies, poor enforcement.
▪ Sub-classification: Ex: SC sub-quota verdict (2024) to reach the least- advantaged within groups.
▪ Beyond jobs/seats: need land, credit, dignity — reservation ≠ empowerment.
▪ Beyond quotas: Ex: SCSP/TSP funds, scholarships, EWS 10% (103rd CAA).
▪ Data gaps: absence of caste-census data blunts targeting.
▪ Empowerment agencies: Ex: NCSC/NCST/NCBC; PM-JANMAN for PVTGs.
▪ Intersectionality: Dalit/tribal women face compounded disadvantage.

Way Forward: recalibrate via evidence (caste data, sub-classification) · pair quotas with capability-building (education, credit, land) · fill backlog vacancies · protect the least-advantaged within groups.

Conclusion: Affirmative action opened doors but substantive empowerment needs more than a reserved seat — assets, capabilities and dignity must accompany representation for constitutional justice to be realised on the ground.
＋ 2026 ammo (T15): caste-census confirmation (Census 2027) converts "data gaps" bullet into an implementation question — evidence-based recalibration is now live policy, not demand.
T16 · Employment Guarantee & Social Security Net (covers PYQ Q19; new) [15m]

Intro (event): India's rural employment guarantee — MGNREGA (2005) as a rights-based demand-driven safety-net — has been replaced by the VB-G RAM G Act, 2025 (in force 1 July 2026) to fix wage-delay and asset-quality gaps.
Intro (concept): Employment guarantee embodies the 'right to work' and acts as an automatic stabiliser — Ex: it cushioned reverse-migration shock during COVID.
H1 — Achievements of the guarantee model:
H2 — Shortcomings & the reform agenda:
▪ Demand-driven safety net: legally enforceable wage employment. Ex: MGNREGA's 100-day guarantee.
▪ Wage delays & fund crunch: eroded credibility; Aadhaar-based payment glitches.
▪ Rural distress cushion: Ex: peak demand during COVID reverse migration.
▪ Leakage & ghost muster-rolls: Ex: social-audit & DBT reforms.
▪ Women & inclusion: high female/SC-ST participation, statutory wage parity.
▪ Poor asset durability & planning: need convergence with other schemes.
▪ Asset creation: water conservation, rural roads (mixed quality).
▪ Urban gap: no comparable urban employment guarantee.
▪ Bargaining floor: raises rural reservation wages.
▪ Successor design [verify]: consolidating livelihoods, skilling & durable assets.
Way Forward: guarantee timely wages & adequate funding · strengthen social audit · improve asset quality & convergence · consider an urban employment-guarantee · link to skilling/livelihoods.
Conclusion: A credible right-to-work guarantee is India's largest anti-poverty stabiliser — its promise depends on timely wages, honest audits and durable assets, whatever the statute is named.

＋ CRITICAL 2026 UPDATE (this card): the successor law is now fact — VB-G RAM G Act, 2025 (Viksit Bharat – Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin)): passed 18 Dec 2025, assent 21 Dec, in force 1 July 2026, repealing MGNREGA. Key design: 125 days guarantee (vs 100) · 4 priority areas — water security, rural infrastructure, livelihood infrastructure, extreme-weather mitigation · up to 60-day agricultural pause · funding 60:40 Centre-State (90:10 NE) · mission outlay ~₹1.51 lakh cr. Critique lines: federal burden-shift (Kerala/TN objections), centralised allocation power, fear that a demand-driven *right* becomes a budget-capped *mission* (UN poverty experts’ concern). Keep this card’s conclusion — judge the successor on MGNREGA’s own tests: timely wages, demand-responsiveness, social audit, women’s participation.
SECTION E · URBANISATION — MIGRATION, SLUMS & CITY MANAGEMENT
T17 · Urbanisation — Migration, Slums & Inclusive Cities (covers PYQ Q45/Q46/Q72/Q24; PYQ 2025/2023) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · smart-city + migration
Intro (data): India is ~35% urban and urbanising fast (projected ~40% by 2030); cities generate ~ 60%+ of GDP yet host vast slum populations (~65 mn, Census 2011) — growth with exclusion .
Intro (thinker): Louis Wirth ('Urbanism as a Way of Life') on urban anomie; does urbanisation segregate/marginalise the poor? ( PYQ 2023 ) — the metropolis reproduces spatial inequality.
H1 — Migration & urban distress:
H2 — Toward inclusive urbanisation:
▪ Push-pull migration: agrarian distress pushes, city wages pull. Ex: extension of 2024 migration PYQ (Q45).
▪ Smart Cities & the poor: 'Smart Cities haven't served the urban poor' ( PYQ Q46/2025 ) — area-based bias.
▪ Source-destination impact: remittances vs 'left-behind' villages; overcrowded destinations.
▪ Distributive justice: basic services, tenure security for slum-dwellers.
▪ Informality & precarity: migrants in informal sector, no social security. Ex: e-Shram; One Nation One Ration Card.
▪ City governance: Ex: 74th Amendment under-empowered ULBs; weak municipal finance.
▪ Slums & housing deficit: Ex: PMAY-U; Rajiv Awas / in-situ upgrading.
▪ Planning capacity: Ex: AMRUT 2.0, Master-plan/land-use reform.
▪ Urban flooding & fragility: Ex: unplanned growth, choked drainage (Q24 crossover).
▪ Migrant-inclusive city: portable entitlements, night shelters, rental housing.
Way Forward: empower & finance ULBs (74th Amdt spirit) · portable social security for migrants · in-situ slum upgrading with tenure · climate-resilient, inclusive master-planning.

Conclusion: Indian urbanisation delivers growth but exports its costs to the poor — segregated slums, precarious migrants, brittle infrastructure; only inclusive, well-governed cities can turn agglomeration into shared prosperity and distributive justice.
SECTION F · EFFECTS OF GLOBALISATION & TECHNOLOGY ON SOCIETY
T18 · Globalisation & Indian Society (covers PYQ Q79; PYQ 2025/2020/2018/2016) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · asked almost yearly

Intro (concept): Globalisation integrates markets, culture and information — producing cultural homogenisation and its opposite: strengthened cultural specificities/glocalisation ( PYQ 2018 ), not merely 'aggressive consumer culture' ( PYQ 2025 ).
Intro (thinker): Roland Robertson — 'glocalisation' ; Appadurai's '-scapes' (ethno/media/finance) — flows both flatten and revive local identity, a differential rural–urban impact ( PYQ Q79 ).
H1 — Homogenising & consumerist effects:
H2 — Revival & assimilation (glocalisation):
▪ Consumer culture: brands, malls, fast-food ( PYQ 2025 fast-food ). Ex: changing diets, obesity/health concerns.
▪ Cultural specificities strengthened: Ex: yoga, Ayurveda, regional cinema go global (PYQ 2018).
▪ Cultural flattening: McDonaldisation ( Ritzer ); English-media dominance; 'losing local identity' ( PYQ 2019 ).
▪ Selective assimilation: Yogendra Singh — modernisation absorbed within tradition.
▪ Economic disruption: Ex: differential impact on agriculture, small industry, informal labour (Q79).
▪ Hybrid identities: 'glocal' youth culture; festivals reinvented.
▪ Inequality: globalisation's gains skewed to skilled/urban.
▪ Soft power: Ex: International Yoga Day, Indian cuisine, cinema exports.
▪ Diaspora & flows: remittances, reverse cultural exchange.
▪ Diversity under strain? ( PYQ 2020 ) — pluralism both threatened and revitalised.

Way Forward: harness globalisation's opportunity while protecting cultural roots & vulnerable sectors · cushion agrarian/informal disruption · leverage soft power · regulate consumerist harms (health, environment).

Conclusion: Globalisation is neither pure homogeniser nor pure liberator — India both consumes global culture and re-exports its own; the task is to ride its opportunities while shielding the local identities and livelihoods it unsettles.
T19 · Technology, AI & Social Media in Society (covers PYQ Q20/Q80; PYQ 2023/2021) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · AI + digital-life fresh

Intro (event): AI is reshaping work and social life , but 'its benefits are not gender-neutral' ( PYQ Q20 ) — a widening gender digital divide ( 'Decoded: Women & Future of Digital Work', 2025 ); Ex: India AI mission/summit context.

Intro (concept): Technology is a double-edged social force : it democratises voice and access yet enables surveillance, addiction, misinformation and the erosion of face-to-face bonds ( PYQ Q80 / 2023 mobile-childhood ).
H1 — Empowering & connective effects:
H2 — Disruptive & divisive effects:
▪ Digital inclusion: Ex: DPI/India Stack — Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker widen access.
▪ Gender digital divide: lower female device/internet access; AI trained on biased data ( Q20 ).
▪ Voice & mobilisation: social media amplifies marginalised voices, civic action.
▪ Misinformation & polarisation: virality fuels communal rumour, echo chambers.
▪ Livelihoods: gig/platform work, e-commerce, remote work (WFH, PYQ 2022 ).
▪ Addiction & socialisation loss: 'child-cuddling replaced by mobiles' ( PYQ 2023 ); screen-time, mental health.
▪ Knowledge access: ed-tech, tele-medicine bridge rural gaps.
▪ Surveillance & privacy: Ex: DPDP Act 2023 data-protection debate.
▪ Financial empowerment: Ex: DBT to women's accounts; JAM trinity.
▪ Job displacement: automation threatens routine/informal work.
Way Forward: close the gender & rural digital divide · digital-literacy & safety · debias AI and regulate platforms · protect privacy (DPDP) · balance connectivity with real-world bonds.

Conclusion: Technology mirrors and magnifies society — liberating and dividing in the same stroke ; steering it toward inclusion, especially for women and the digitally excluded, is the defining social challenge of India's AI age.
T19A · Youth in India — Aspiration, Employment, Mental Health & Loneliness — NEW emerging card [10/15m]
Intro (data): ~65% of Indians are under 35 — the demographic-dividend generation (T11) — yet graduate unemployment (~29%, PLFS) reveals an aspiration–opportunity mismatch at the heart of young India.

Intro (concept): Durkheim’s anomie, updated — exam pressure (Kota syndrome), gig precarity, social-media comparison and shrinking community produce a documented mental-health and loneliness burden (WHO now treats social connection as a health determinant).
H1 — Stressors on the young:
H2 — Social consequences & responses:
▪ Education–employment mismatch: degrees without employability; the coaching-industrial complex; govt-job queue psychology.
▪ Mental-health burden: NMHS ~10.6% adult mental morbidity; ~1.7 lakh suicides/yr (NCRB), ~8% students; 70–90% treatment gap → Tele-MANAS.
▪ Precarity: gig/contract work without security (T7); "waithood" — delayed marriage, housing, adulthood (T10).
▪ Loneliness & digital substitution: screen-mediated ties replace face-to-face bonds (PYQ 2023 mobile-childhood; T19).
▪ Comparison culture: curated social-media lives → status anxiety (relative deprivation).
▪ Policy scaffold: Mental Healthcare Act 2017 (suicide decriminalised), Tele-MANAS 24×7, NEP-2020 skilling turn, sports & fitness missions.
▪ Migration & family stretch: young migrants alone in cities (T17); "left-behind" parents (T12).
▪ Way of hope: youth-led civic energy — climate activism, start-ups, sports success as new mobility scripts.

Way Forward: treat mental health as public health (school counsellors, primary-care integration) · fix the skilling-jobs pipeline before the dividend window closes (T11) · design cities & campuses for connection, not isolation.
Conclusion: A young India anxious and alone is a dividend squandered — converting demographic weight into wellbeing needs jobs, mental-health care and rebuilt community in equal measure.
SECTION G · TRIBAL SOCIETY — DEVELOPMENT, RIGHTS & DISPLACEMENT
T20 · Tribal Development — Displacement, FRA & PVTGs (covers PYQ Q18/Q53/Q54/Q35; PYQ 2025/2022/2021/2016) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed · tribal asked 4 of last 5 yrs

Intro (thinker): The old debate — Verrier Elwin's isolation ('national parks') vs G.S. Ghurye's assimilation ('backward Hindus') — is resolved by Nehru's Panchsheel of integration without imposition ; tribal development turns on displacement vs rehabilitation ( PYQ 2025 ).
Intro (committee): Xaxa Committee (2014) documented tribal deprivation & displacement; 705 STs, 75 PVTGs need a differentiated approach ( PYQ Q18/2022 'single category?' ).
H1 — The two axes: displacement & rights:
H2 — Rehabilitation & self-governance:
▪ Development-induced displacement: dams/mines/projects uproot tribals disproportionately. Ex: Great Nicobar project vs Shompen (Q53).
▪ Differentiated development: PVTGs need distinct approach vs general STs. Ex: PM-JANMAN habitat/basic-services focus.
▪ Land alienation: despite Sched. V & PESA safeguards. Ex: erosion of protective tenancy laws.
▪ Self-rule: Ex: PESA 1996 — gram sabha consent; Sched. VI autonomous councils (NE).
▪ Forest rights: Forest Rights Act 2006 recognises individual & community forest rights — yet poorly implemented ( PYQ Q54 ).
▪ Tribal knowledge systems: Ex: uniqueness vs mainstream (PYQ 2021) — sustainable, community-based.
▪ PVTG vulnerability: Ex: only ~10/75 PVTGs granted habitat rights; PM- JANMAN (₹24,104 cr).
▪ Rehabilitation deficit: poor R&R, cash-for-land traps. Ex: LARR Act 2013 consent/SIA provisions.
▪ Historic resistance: Ex: Santhal, Munda (Birsa), Bhil uprisings (PYQ Q35 crossover).
▪ When 'single category'?: only for constitutional safeguards/affirmative action, not culturally ( PYQ 2022 ).

Way Forward: implement FRA/PESA in letter & spirit · free, prior, informed consent before projects · PVTG-specific habitat rights (PM-JANMAN) · valorise tribal knowledge · humane rehabilitation over displacement.

Conclusion: Tribal India stands between displacement and dignity — genuine development means securing forest and habitat rights, honouring self- governance, and treating rehabilitation not as compensation but as a constitutional promise of Antyodaya .
FLOW-MEMORY SPINE — recite in order to trigger every card
NEW CARDS woven into the spine: T4A Secularisation-vs-Revival (Block A, after Secularism/UCC/Regionalism) · T19A Youth-MentalHealth-Loneliness (Block F, after Technology/AI).

A · Society & Diversity: Unity-in-Diversity → Caste (static/fluid) → Communalism → Secularism/Places-of-Worship → UCC → Regionalism. B · Women & Family: Women's Work (LFPR/care/gig) → Gender Equality-Equity-Empowerment → Women's Question & National Movement → Marriage/Family/Kinship.

C · Population: Demographic Transition/Dividend/Winter → Ageing → Population Policy (education-fertility). D · Poverty & Empowerment: Poverty/Human-Development → Affirmative Action → Employment Guarantee. E · Urban: Urbanisation-Migration-Slums-Smart-Cities. F · Global/Tech: Globalisation → Technology/AI/Social-Media. G · Tribal: Tribal Development-Displacement-FRA-PVTG. → 20 cards, 7 blocks.

CONCEPT / DOCTRINE BANK — sprinkle for sociological clarity Sanskritisation · Westernisation · Dominant caste ( Srinivas ) · purity–pollution / Homo Hierarchicus ( Dumont ) · caste-class-power ( Béteille ) · graded inequality / annihilation of caste ( Ambedkar ) · politics of caste ( Rajni Kothari ) · glocalisation ( Robertson ) · -scapes ( Appadurai ) · McDonaldisation ( Ritzer ) · capability deprivation / development as freedom ( Sen ) · empowerment = resources+agency+achievements ( Kabeer ) · principled distance / sarva-dharma- sambhava (secularism, Bhargava ) · ghar–bahir / inner-outer domain ( Partha Chatterjee ) · demographic transition · demographic dividend/winter · modernisation of tradition ( Yogendra Singh ) · mistaken modernity ( Dipankar Gupta ) · Panchsheel of tribal integration ( Nehru ) vs isolation ( Elwin ) / assimilation ( Ghurye ) · urbanism as a way of life ( Wirth ) · anomie ( Durkheim ) · relative deprivation · glocal hybridity · feminisation of ageing · care economy · social capital / civic engagement ( Varshney ).

UNIVERSAL LINKERS / QUOTES — droppable into any intro/conclusion Nehru: 'unity in diversity' — India as a palimpsest. · Ambedkar: 'annihilation of caste' ; democracy as 'a mode of associated living'. · Sen: poverty is capability deprivation , development is freedom . · Gandhi: Antyodaya — the last person first; 'India lives in her villages'. · Srinivas: mobility through Sanskritisation . · Karan Singh (1974): 'development is the best contraceptive' . · Kofi Annan: 'gender equality is more than a goal in itself'. · 'Glocal' — think global, act local ( Robertson ). · Bhargava: Indian secularism as principled distance . · Constituent Assembly: the Constitution as a document of social revolution ( Granville Austin ).

VERIFY-BEFORE-EXAM — current items that drift; confirm close to 21 Aug 2026 FLFPR / Time-Use figures (PLFS 2023-24 & latest; unpaid-care minutes) · WEF Gender Gap rank (131/146 in 2025 — check 2026 edition) · TFR (~2.0; NFHS/latest SRS) · PM-JANMAN outlay ₹24,104 cr & PVTG habitat-rights count (~10/75) · MGNREGA-successor / 'Viksit Bharat Rozgar-Ajeevika' Act (2025) — CONFIRM enactment, name & features · High-Level Committee on Demographic Changes (2025-26) — CONFIRM status/report · Places of Worship Act — latest SC bench outcome · Uttarakhand UCC operational status & other states · Great Nicobar vs Shompen litigation status · Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam delimitation-linked rollout timeline · National MPI poverty-exit numbers · elderly-population % (UNFPA India Ageing Report) · caste-census decision. (Anchors dated to mid-2026; re-verify any datum you quote.)
RESOLVED — MGNREGA successor
VB-G RAM G Act, 2025 — name, enactment (Dec 2025) and features confirmed; in force 1 Jul 2026 (see T16 update).
RESOLVED — HLC / Census
Census 2027 notified: digital + caste enumeration; reference 1 Mar 2027 (1 Oct 2026 snow-bound) — delimitation & women’s-quota sequencing follows it.
RESOLVED — gender data
WEF 2025: 131/146 (2026 edition pending); TUS 2024: 289 vs 88 min unpaid work; female LFPR ~41.7% (PLFS 2023-24).
STILL OPEN
Places of Worship final verdict · Great Nicobar/Shompen litigation · National MPI next round · UNFPA elderly % update — re-verify near 21 Aug 2026.

title: GS2 · Governance & Social Justice
lines: GS-2 GOVERNANCE & SOCIAL JUSTICE — MASTER FLOW-MEMORY CHEAT SHEET (Mains 2026) 38 consolidated topics covering all 59 banked questions · facts current to mid-2026 Per topic: Intro (green, ready line) → Body-heading tables (keyword points + example/data) → ＋ Ammo (indigo, high-scoring extras) → Way-fwd (red) →
Concl (navy line)
DRILL: read only the topic + column headings, then rebuild each point with its case/scheme/data. Appendix = reusable arsenal for any answer.
1 · GOVERNMENT POLICIES & INTERVENTIONS
1. Four Labour Codes [covers Q1]

Intro — 2nd National Commission on Labour (2002) → 29 central laws fused into 4 Codes (Wages 2019; Industrial Relations, Social Security, OSH 2020); notified in force 21 Nov 2025 — the largest labour reform since Independence, seeking a shift from job-security to income-and- social-security.
Ease of doing business
Worker social security
Critique
▪ Single registration, licence & return; one uniform definition of "wages" cuts litigation across statutes
▪ Statutory national floor wage + universal minimum wage (Art 43 living wage goal)
▪ Higher strike-notice & 51% union-recognition thresholds dilute collective bargaining (ILO Conv. 87/98)
▪ Fixed-term employment legalised → flexibility with pro-rata gratuity/benefits
▪ Gig/platform workers covered first time ; aggregator 1–2% turnover → Social Security Fund
▪ "Inspector-cum-facilitator" + self-certification weaken enforcement
▪ Threshold for standing orders/lay-off-closure raised to 300 workers — eases MSME compliance
▪ ESIC/EPFO widened to unorganised, plantation, fixed-term; e-Shram portability
▪ Bulk of detail left to State rules → federal unevenness, race-to-bottom risk
＋ Ammo — ILO "Decent Work" & SDG-8; contrast rigid pre-reform regime (Industrial Disputes Act 1947, ch. V-B) with post-reform flexicurity; Economic Survey flags 90% informality as the real challenge.
Way fwd — Uniform time-bound State rules; fund the SS schemes; preserve bargaining rights; grievance machinery for gig workers.
Concl — The Codes deliver genuine formalisation only if they privilege neither capital-cutting nor compliance-cutting — flexibility with security.
2. JAM–DBT & Welfare Delivery [covers Q2]

Intro — The Economic Survey called JAM (Jan Dhan–Aadhaar–Mobile) a plumbing revolution; DBT (since 2013, Aadhaar Act 2016) now runs across 300+ schemes , re-architecting welfare from leaky subsidies to targeted transfers.
Transformative gains
Persisting challenges
▪ Leakage plugged — cumulative savings >₹3.48 lakh cr (DBT Mission); PAHAL = world’s largest DBT (Guinness)
▪ Exclusion errors — biometric/seeding failure denied ration → Jharkhand (Santoshi Kumari, 2017) starvation death
▪ Inclusion — PMJDY 53 cr accounts (55%+ women); Aadhaar 138 cr; UPI rails
▪ Last-mile — sparse Banking-Correspondent network, low rural bank density, patchy connectivity
▪ Real-time, transparent, ghost/duplicate-beneficiary elimination; MGNREGA wage-timeliness ↑
▪ Privacy — Puttaswamy II (2018) upheld Aadhaar but struck §57 (private mandatory use)
＋ Ammo — JAM enabled Economic Survey 2016-17’s UBI debate; DBT ≠ service-delivery quality; contrast in-kind PDS vs cash (per Bihar cash-transfer pilots); "last-mile" is the frontier, not coverage.
Way fwd — Robust grievance redress; offline/manual fallback; financial & digital literacy; strengthen BC ecosystem.
Concl — DBT fixes the pipes, but the water must reach the last hut — inclusion, not efficiency alone, is the test of welfare .
3. Police Reform [covers Q3]

Intro — Prakash Singh v Union of India (2006) issued 7 binding directives to insulate the police from the political executive; two decades on, implementation stays largely "on paper", the colonial Police Act, 1861 endures.
Reform blueprint
Why it fails
▪ Prakash Singh 7 directives — State Security Commission, DGP fixed 2-yr tenure , Police Establishment Board, Police Complaints Authority , separate investigation from law-&-order
▪ Political interference — arbitrary transfers; States dilute/ignore directives
▪ Model Police Act 2006 (Soli Sorabjee); 2nd ARC 5th Report "Public Order"
▪ Police a State subject (Entry 2, List II); federal friction; chronic under- funding
▪ SMART police (PM, 2014); CCTNS/ICJS; modern forensics (NFSU)
▪ Vacancies (~5 lakh); ratio ~152/lakh vs UN-norm 222 ; custodial violence ( D.K. Basu, 1997 ; NCRB)

＋ Ammo — Malimath Committee (2003, criminal-justice); Justice Verma Committee (2013, women’s safety); Vineet Narain (1998) on investigative autonomy; contrast community-policing models (Kerala Janamaithri, Rajasthan).
Way fwd — Enforce Prakash Singh + Model Police Act; insulate postings; community policing; forensic & tech upgrade; separate law-order and investigation wings.
Concl — An autonomous, accountable, citizen-friendly police is the frontline of the rule of law — SMART policing must move from slogan to statute.
4. Cyber Security Governance [covers Q4]
Intro — With 100+ cr internet users and a digitising economy, cyberspace is the "fifth domain of warfare"; yet India’s National Cyber Security Policy still dates to 2013 and lacks an umbrella law.
Architecture
Gaps & threats
▪ CERT-In (nodal; 6-hr breach reporting , 2022 directions); NCIIPC (critical infra); I4C + 1930 helpline (cybercrime); National Cyber Security Coordinator
▪ Outdated 2013 policy; no unified strategy/law ; fragmented mandates (civil-military-sectoral)
▪ DPDP Act 2023; IT Act 2000 + §69/70; proposed Digital India Act ; Cyber Swachhta Kendra Architecture
▪ Skilled-workforce deficit; hardware/semiconductor & API import dependence
▪ Attacks — AIIMS-Delhi ransomware (2022) , power-grid probing, Gaps & threats deepfakes, UPI/financial fraud

＋ Ammo — Lt Gen Rajesh Pant’s draft National Cyber Security Strategy (pending); Budapest Convention debate; compare EU NIS2 / GDPR, Estonia’s cyber-resilience post-2007; "data is the new oil, cyber the new frontier".
Way fwd — Enact the new Cyber Security Strategy; whole-of-nation PPP; cyber-hygiene & skilling; critical-infra audits; international cooperation & attribution capacity.
Concl — Digital sovereignty demands proactive, whole-of-nation cyber-resilience , not reactive firefighting after each breach.
2 · E-GOVERNANCE — APPLICATIONS, MODELS, LIMITS
5. Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) [covers Q5, Q8]

Intro — The G20 New Delhi Declaration (2023) endorsed India Stack as a global template; DPI (identity + payments + data-exchange on open protocols) reframes e-governance from a service portal into a foundational societal utility .
Advances inclusion
Risks of exclusion
▪ Financial inclusion via JAM/UPI; DBT savings >₹3.48 lakh cr
▪ Digital divide — rural teledensity gap (TRAI); biometric failure denies PDS (Jharkhand)
▪ Population-scale delivery — CoWIN 220 cr doses ; DigiLocker 30 cr+ users; FASTag
▪ Privacy/surveillance — Puttaswamy (2017) ; DPDP not yet fully operational
▪ ONDC unbundles e-commerce; Account Aggregator/DEPA & OCEN enable MSME credit
▪ Private capture, vendor lock-in; opaque automated denials without human recourse

＋ Ammo — MOSIP exported to Philippines, Morocco, Ethiopia, Sri Lanka; contrast Estonia’s X-Road; NITI/RBI on "techade"; "DPI as a public good, not a private moat"; risk of a two-speed digital citizenry.
Way fwd — Inclusive-by-design; offline/assisted fallback; operationalise DPDP; interoperability & grievance redress; guard against private monopolies.
Concl — DPI’s legitimacy rests on remaining a public good, not a gatekeeper — the constitutional pledge is to "leave no one behind".
6. Artificial Intelligence in Governance [covers Q6, Q7]

Intro — NITI Aayog’s "Responsible AI for All" (2021) frames AI as a governance tool bound by Article 14 (non-arbitrariness) and Article 21 (due process) — efficiency that must not eclipse constitutional fairness.
Potential
Risks
Safeguards
▪ Predictive welfare targeting, disaster early- warning, GST fraud analytics
▪ Opacity — " black-box " denials violate the duty to give reasons (Art 14)
▪ Explainability + algorithmic impact audit before deployment
▪ Bhashini breaks the language barrier; e- courts/judgment translation (SUVAS)
▪ Bias — skewed training data entrench disadvantage; facial-recognition error rates
▪ Human-in-the-loop for adverse decisions; data-justice & bias testing
▪ IndiaAI Mission (₹10,372 cr, 2024) ; agri- advisory, telemedicine triage
▪ Digital exclusion + diffused accountability ("problem of many hands"); deepfakes & disinfo
▪ DPDP + proposed Digital India Act; independent AI oversight
＋ Ammo — Compare EU AI Act (2024) risk-tiers, OECD AI Principles, US Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights; Justice Srikrishna on automated processing; "move fast" must not mean "break rights".
Way fwd — Statutory footing (Digital India Act); mandatory audits; human oversight; representative datasets; grievance rights against algorithmic decisions.
Concl — Responsible AI must move from principle to statute — the citizen, not the code, stays sovereign.
7. Grievance Redressal [covers Q9]

Intro — 2nd ARC’s "Citizen-Centric Administration" (12th Report, 2009) held responsiveness to be the truest test of good governance; Article 41-adjacent service-delivery is now increasingly technology-mediated.
Technology strengthens
Limits
▪ CPGRAMS — online tracking, time-bound disposal; auto-escalation & feedback call-back
▪ Digital exclusion of poor/rural/elderly citizens
▪ Sevottam service-quality framework; Citizens’ Charters set measurable standards
▪ " Resolved " ≠ redressed — disposal-count over closure-quality
▪ Grievance analytics → root-cause systemic reform (data-driven governance)
▪ Charters non-justiciable ; no Union Right-to-Public-Services law (only States, e.g. MP 2010)

＋ Ammo — Nagarik/RTPS Acts of MP, Bihar, Rajasthan, Karnataka (Sakala); UK Citizen’s Charter (1991) origin; contrast rules-based CPGRAMS 7.0 with earlier apathy; "administration for the citizen, not the file".
Way fwd — Statutory time-bound service delivery with penalties ; assisted-access (CSC/help-desk); social audit; independent ombudsman.
Concl — Trust is built by genuine closure, not dashboards — redressal must empower, not tokenise, the citizen.
8. DPDP Act 2023 / Data Protection [covers Q10]
Intro — Flowing from Puttaswamy (2017) — privacy a fundamental right under Art 21 — the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 is India’s first cross-sectoral privacy law; Rules notified 2025 .
Architecture
Concerns
▪ Data Principal & Data Fiduciary ; consent, purpose & storage limitation; notice; Consent Managers
▪ Wide State exemptions (§17) — privacy vs surveillance imbalance
▪ Data Protection Board of India (adjudication); Significant Data Fiduciaries; penalties up to ₹250 cr
▪ §44(3) dilutes RTI [amends §8(1)(j)] — transparency casualty
▪ Rights — access, correction, erasure, grievance; deemed consent for legit uses
▪ DPB not independent (govt-appointed, removable); no data-localisation mandate; no right vs automated decisions

＋ Ammo — B.N. Srikrishna Committee (2018) blueprint; contrast EU GDPR (independent DPAs, data-localisation); proportionality test (legality- necessity-proportionality) from Puttaswamy; "privacy is the price of dignity in the digital age".
Way fwd — Independent, collegium-appointed regulator; narrow & judicially-reviewable exemptions; restore RTI balance; data-breach & children-data safeguards.
Concl — A thriving data economy and privacy can coexist only under an independent, rights-first framework.
3 · TRANSPARENCY & ACCOUNTABILITY (RTI)
9. RTI Act 2005 & the Privacy Tension [covers Q11, Q12, Q13]

Intro — RTI Act 2005 operationalises Art 19(1)(a) — the right to know first read into the Constitution in State of UP v Raj Narain (1975) & S.P. Gupta (1981) ; at ~20 years it faces its sternest test from the DPDP Act.
Achievements
Erosion / challenges
▪ Empowered citizen — exposed 2G, Commonwealth, PDS/MGNREGA leakages; electoral-bonds transparency (SC, 2024)
▪ DPDP §44(3) removes the public-interest override in §8(1)(j) — shields "personal information"
▪ §4 proactive (suo-motu) disclosure ; ~40–60 lakh applications/yr — world’s most-used transparency law
▪ Info-Commission vacancies & backlog; RTI (Amendment) Act 2019 cut CIC tenure/salary autonomy
▪ Deterrent effect; strengthens participatory democracy & anti-corruption
▪ Attacks on RTI activists (need Whistle Blowers Protection Act 2014 operationalised); frivolous-use debate
＋ Ammo — Anjali Bhardwaj v UoI (2019) on vacancies; Kofi Annan — "Information is liberating"; contrast pre-2005 Official Secrets Act 1923 opacity; RTI as "oxygen of democracy".
Way fwd — Restore the public-interest/harm-balancing test; fill Information Commissions transparently; protect whistle-blowers; digitise §4 disclosures.
Concl — Sunshine and privacy are twin democratic goods — the answer is calibration, not the subordination of one to the other.
4 · ROLE OF CIVIL SERVICES IN A DEMOCRACY
10. Mission Karmayogi [covers Q14]

Intro — The National Programme for Civil Services Capacity Building (NPCSCB, 2020) aims to shift officials from a rule-based to a role-based , competency-driven (FRAC) model — the "steel-frame" reimagined for a service-delivery era.
Promise
Structural limits
▪ iGOT Karmayogi on-demand digital learning; Capacity Building Commission as apex
▪ Recruitment silos & generalism; lateral entry contested (2024 ad withdrawn)
▪ FRAC (Framework of Roles, Activities, Competencies); citizen-centric, outcome & behaviour focus
▪ Weak performance-accountability; secure tenure without meaningful appraisal
▪ Continuous lifelong learning vs one-time induction training
▪ Political interface — arbitrary transfers ( T.S.R. Subramanian v UoI, 2013 : fixed tenure, Civil Services Board)

＋ Ammo — 2nd ARC (10th Report "Personnel Administration"); Hota & Baswan Committees; compare Singapore’s meritocratic civil service, UK’s Next Steps agencies; Weberian bureaucracy vs New Public Management.
Way fwd — Pair training with performance-linked appraisal, fixed tenure, and calibrated lateral entry; mid-career domain specialisation.
Concl — Capacity-building reforms the human , but only structural reform can renovate the steel-frame itself.
＋ 2026 ammo (topic 10): add SPARROW (online APAR) & Karmayogi Competency Model (behavioural + functional + domain) as concrete reform artefacts; "rule-based to role-based" is the exam phrase.
11. Lateral Entry [covers Q15]

Intro — Introduced in 2018 at Joint-Secretary/Director level to inject domain expertise; the 2024 UPSC recruitment of 45 posts was withdrawn over reservation concerns — reigniting the generalist-vs-specialist debate.
Merits
Challenges
▪ Domain expertise & fresh perspective (backed by NITI Aayog, 2nd ARC)
▪ Reservation dilution — single-post recruitment escapes SC/ST/OBC quota (13-point roster)
▪ Ends generalist monopoly; injects competition & private-sector best practice
▪ Lack of field/grassroots & cutting-edge experience; short tenure & accountability gaps
▪ Bridges specialist talent-gaps in a complex, sectoral economy
▪ Insider resistance & morale; conflict-of-interest and revolving-door risk
＋ Ammo — Compare US "spoils/political appointees" vs UK permanent secretaries; NITI’s "Strategy for New India @75"; contract vs cadre; "expertise must not come at the cost of equity".
Way fwd — Transparent UPSC-run selection; reservation compliance; clearly-defined specialist domains; a calibrated generalist-specialist mix.
Concl — Expertise and the steel-frame can be complements, not rivals — provided entry is fair, finite and accountable.
12. Civil Services (CSE) Reform [covers Q16]
Intro — A century after the first exam (1926 Federal PSC → UPSC, Art 315), the Baswan Committee (2016) reaffirmed merit while urging modern selection aligned to 21st-century governance.
Debates
Reform ideas
▪ Age limit & number of attempts ( 2nd ARC: lower entry age ); optional- subject scoring inequity; CSAT language-fairness & rural-urban divide
▪ Aptitude, ethics & domain focus; specialisation post-selection; probation-cum-training & mid-career reviews; reduce exam-cycle length
＋ Ammo — Kothari (1976), Satish Chandra, Nigavekar, Alagh & Hota Committees; Yugandhar (training); compare Singapore/France (ENA→INSP); "select for character and competence, not endurance".
Way fwd — Refine rather than dismantle; competency-based selection; strengthen LBSNAA training; align with Mission Karmayogi’s role- based model.
Concl — The steel-frame’s credibility rests on selecting for character and competence , not the endurance of cramming.
5 · REGULATORY BODIES & INSTITUTIONAL DESIGN
13. Regulatory Autonomy [covers Q17, Q18]

Intro — Regulators are "arm’s-length" guardians of complex, information-asymmetric sectors (SEBI, TRAI, CCI, RBI, IRDAI); autonomy is their lifeblood, and the new Data Protection Board is a live test of it.
Why autonomy matters
Threats
▪ Impartial adjudication where the State itself is a stakeholder/competitor
▪ Executive-controlled appointments & removal (DPB); dependence on govt grants
▪ Investor & citizen confidence; predictable, rule-based (not discretionary) regulation
▪ Turf overlap — TRAI vs CCI in digital markets; sectoral vs competition regulators
▪ Expertise-led, technically-sound decisions insulated from electoral cycles
▪ Excessive delegated power without accountability; tribunalisation ( Rojer Mathew, 2019 )

＋ Ammo — FSLRC/Justice B.N. Srikrishna (2013) on regulatory governance; separation of executive-legislative-judicial functions within regulators; RIA (Regulatory Impact Assessment); "regulate the market, and regulate the regulator".
Way fwd — Collegium-style appointments; ring-fenced funding; statutorily-defined powers; parliamentary oversight; performance review & appeals.
Concl — A regulator without independence merely relocates arbitrariness — insulate it to legitimise it.
14. National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) [covers Q19]
Intro — Established under the Protection of Human Rights Act 1993 (aligned to the Paris Principles, 1991 ), the NHRC is the "watchdog of human rights" — widely seen as a watchdog without teeth .
Constraints
Reforms needed
▪ Recommendatory powers only ; 1-year limitation bar on complaints
▪ Binding/enforceable directions; independent investigation wing
▪ No direct probe of armed forces (only seeks report, §19); no own investigative cadre — leans on the same police it scrutinises
▪ Armed-forces jurisdiction; remove/relax limitation period
▪ GANHRI deferred its ‘A’-status accreditation (2023) over autonomy & pluralism concerns
▪ Timely, plural & diverse appointments (women, minorities, civil society); financial autonomy
＋ Ammo — 2nd ARC; compare Kenya/South Africa NHRIs; overlap with NCW/NCSC/NCPCR (multiplicity); D.K. Basu (1997) & custodial-death focus; "rights on paper vs remedies in practice".
Way fwd — Amend PHRA for binding powers, own cadre & armed-forces reach; secure Paris-Principles compliance; timely appointments.
Concl — Human-rights protection needs teeth, not just a voice — empower the guardian to actually guard.
6 · DEVELOPMENT PROCESSES (NGOs, SHGs, CIVIL SOCIETY)
15. Self-Help Groups (SHGs) [covers Q20, Q21]

Intro — The SHG–Bank Linkage Programme (NABARD, 1992) seeded the world’s largest microfinance movement ; under DAY-NRLM (2011) SHGs have evolved from thrift-credit collectives into engines of rural transformation.
Empowerment
Constraints
▪ Credit without the moneylender; 10 cr+ women in 91 lakh SHGs (NRLM)
▪ Small loan sizes; weak market & value-chain linkage; low credit- absorption
▪ Enterprise — Lakhpati Didi (target 3 cr) ; Bank/Bima Sakhi; Drone Didi; Kudumbashree (Kerala)
▪ Skill & digital-literacy gaps; thin penetration in poorest, most- patriarchal regions
▪ Social capital, agency, political participation; COVID frontline (masks, awareness)
▪ Elite capture; over-indebtedness; dependence on facilitators/CRPs

＋ Ammo — Compare Grameen Bank/Muhammad Yunus (Bangladesh) ; SEWA (Ela Bhatt); Deendayal Antyodaya "reach the last"; graduate SHGs → FPOs/producer collectives ; "microfinance built agency; enterprise builds prosperity".
Way fwd — Value-chain, digital-market & skilling linkage; higher credit limits; FPO federation; convergence with One-District-One-Product.
Concl — Micro-credit built agency ; enterprise scaling must now build a self-reliant rural economy (Atmanirbhar Bharat).
16. NGOs / FCRA / Civil Society [covers Q22, Q23, Q24]

Intro — NGOs are the "third sector" — partners in delivery (Art 43-adjacent) yet increasingly under the scanner of the FCRA (1976, replaced 2010, amended 2020) ; civil society occupies a contested space between partnership and scrutiny.
Case for regulation
Case for autonomy
▪ National sovereignty/security; anti-money-laundering & FATF compliance
▪ Compliance chokehold — 20% admin-expense cap , mandatory SBI-New Delhi account, sub-granting ban
▪ Noel Harper v UoI (2022) & INSAF v UoI (2020) upheld FCRA provisions
▪ Mass FCRA cancellations (thousands) shrink civic space; chills rights/environment advocacy
▪ Transparency via NGO-DARPAN (NITI); accountability for public/foreign funds
▪ Discretionary renewal → arbitrariness; service-delivery in health/education hit

＋ Ammo — Vineet Narain (1998) accountability lineage; compare "shrinking civic space" (CIVICUS Monitor); NGOs as "watchdog & delivery arm"; distinguish national-security cases from dissent; "regulate for probity, not for silence".
Way fwd — Proportionate, transparent, time-bound regulation; independent review of cancellations; separate genuine security threat from advocacy.
Concl — A democracy needs a free third sector — accountable, but not asphyxiated.
7 · DECENTRALISATION & LOCAL GOVERNANCE
17. Panchayati Raj & Decentralised Planning [covers Q25, Q26, Q27]

Intro — The 73rd Amendment (1992) — Part IX, 11th Schedule (29 subjects), Art 243G — envisaged PRIs as institutions of self-government ; real power rests on the " 3 Fs ": funds, functions, functionaries.
Gains
Deficits
▪ Political devolution — 34 lakh reps; 14 lakh+ elected women (many States 50%)
▪ Fiscal dependence — thin own-source revenue ; State Finance Commissions (Art 243-I) delayed/ignored
▪ Gram Sabha participatory planning; Kerala People’s Plan Campaign (1996) as gold standard
▪ Functionary gap; incomplete activity-mapping of the 29 subjects; untied vs tied funds
▪ SVAMITVA (property cards), eGramSwaraj, GPDP; SDG-localisation
▪ Parallel bodies/parastatals; proxy rule (" Sarpanch Pati "); capacity deficits

＋ Ammo — 2nd ARC 6th Report "Local Governance"; Balwant Rai Mehta (1957) & Ashok Mehta (1978) lineage; 15th FC ₹4.36 lakh cr to local bodies; compare Brazil’s participatory budgeting (Porto Alegre); "democracy’s grassroots".
Way fwd — Empower SFCs; activity-map with matching staff & funds; buoyant own-revenue; capacity-building (RGSA); curb parallel bodies.
Concl — PRIs won political voice but not fiscal power — the unfinished 73rd Amendment still awaits completion.

＋ VERIFIED 16th-FC (topic 17): local-body grants ₹9.47 lakh cr total — RLB ₹4.4 lakh cr (80% basic / 20% performance-linked); entry conditions: audited accounts online + timely SFCs — quote as the new fiscal-decentralisation datum (replaces 15th-FC ₹4.36 L cr).
18. Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) [covers Q28, Q29]

Intro — The 74th Amendment (1992) — Part IXA, 12th Schedule (18 functions), Art 243W — created ULBs; yet Indian cities remain "governed but not self-governing" amid the world’s largest urban transition.
Crisis of capacity
Reforms
▪ Fiscal — municipal own-revenue <1% of GDP (RBI Report on Municipal Finances, 2024); weak property tax
▪ Directly-elected, empowered mayors (fixed 5-yr term) & municipal cadre
▪ Parastatals/development authorities & smart-city SPVs bypass elected ULBs ; ceremonial mayors
▪ Property-tax reform, municipal bonds , value-capture financing, 15th FC grants
▪ SFCs delayed; staff & data deficits; Smart Cities Mission concluded (2024) — legacy question
▪ Full activity-mapping of 18 functions; ward committees & Area Sabhas (participation)
＋ Ammo — ISHUP/AMRUT/PMAY-U ecosystem; 2nd ARC; compare metropolitan governance (London GLA, Tokyo); "India will add ~40 cr urban dwellers by 2050 "; cities as engines of 70% GDP.
Way fwd — Fiscal + functional + functionary devolution; empowered mayors; municipal bonds; professional cadre; citizen participation.
Concl — As India urbanises, empowered cities are not optional — they are the engines of Viksit Bharat @2047.
8 · WELFARE SCHEMES FOR VULNERABLE SECTIONS

＋ VERIFIED 16th-FC (topic 18): ULB grants ₹3.6 lakh cr incl. urbanisation premium & infrastructure top-ups, conditional on property-tax floor-growth & accounts — the strongest-ever FC push on own-source revenue.
19. Gig & Platform Workers [covers Q30, Q31]

Intro — The Code on Social Security 2020 defined "gig" [§2(35)] and "platform" workers [§2(61)] — a historic first recognition (Art 41/43 social-security goals) still largely awaiting on-ground realisation.
Significance
Challenges
▪ Aggregators to contribute 1–2% of turnover (cap 5% of payouts) → Social Security Fund
▪ Registration/awareness gap; schemes "enabling", not entitlement (un- notified)
▪ AB-PMJAY extended to gig workers (Budget 2025-26); e-Shram + UAN portability
▪ No employer–employee status → no minimum wage, PF, or job security
▪ State laws — Rajasthan Platform-Based Gig Workers Act 2023 , Karnataka 2025 (welfare boards, fee)
▪ Algorithmic control — opaque deactivation, fluctuating pay, no grievance mechanism

＋ Ammo — NITI Aayog (2022): 77 lakh (2020-21) → 2.35 cr by 2029-30 ; compare Spain’s "Rider Law" (2021) (employment presumption), EU Platform Work Directive (2024) , UK Uber v Aslam (2021) worker status; "dignity of labour in the algorithmic economy".
Way fwd — Operationalise the SS Fund; mandate algorithmic transparency & portability; a Universal Social Security Account ; model welfare- board law.
Concl — Recognition is the foundation ; funded, enforceable security is the building — dignity of work demands both.
19A. VB-G RAM G Act 2025 — the New Rural Employment Guarantee — NEW card (near-certain 2026 zone) [covers MGNREGA successor]

Intro — Parliament replaced MGNREGA with the Viksit Bharat – Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin) [VB-G RAM G] Act, 2025 (passed 18 Dec 2025; in force 1 July 2026) — the biggest welfare-architecture rewrite since NFSA 2013: from a standalone wage guarantee to a mission fusing employment with livelihoods and climate resilience.
What changes (design)
What to debate (critique)
▪ 125 days guaranteed unskilled work per rural household (vs 100); outlay ~₹1.51 lakh cr.
▪ Federal burden-shift: States now fund 40% (NE 10%) — Kerala/TN objections; allocation power centralised.
▪ Four priority areas: water security, rural infrastructure, livelihood infrastructure, extreme-weather mitigation — durable-asset & convergence focus.
▪ Right vs mission: will a demand-driven legal right become a budget-capped scheme? (UN poverty experts’ warning; MGNREGA’s ch. on "legally enforceable" demand).
▪ Up to 60-day agricultural pause to protect farm-labour supply; Aadhaar/ABPS payment rails continue.
▪ Transition risks: job-card migration, wage-delay carryover, social-audit continuity, works-in-progress limbo.

＋ Ammo — evaluate on MGNREGA’s own tests: timely wages, demand-responsiveness, women’s ~55-59% participation, social audit (§17 legacy); cite COVID reverse-migration cushion as the proven counterfactual; Jean Drèze’s critique vs govt’s asset-quality defence.
Way fwd — notify State rules fast & fund the 40% share realistically; statutory timelines for wages; protect social audit; publish a transition dashboard.
Concl — A guarantee is judged at the muster roll, not in the Gazette — the new Act must prove the extra 25 days are real days, not notional ones.
20. Social Security Architecture / UBI [covers Q32]

Intro — With ~90% of the workforce informal , the Economic Survey 2016-17 floated a JAM-enabled Universal Basic Income as "conceptually appealing"; India’s patchwork of 500+ schemes invites rationalisation.
Case for UBI
Case against
▪ Administrative simplicity; plugs leakage; dignity, autonomy & unconditionality
▪ Large fiscal cost (Survey est. ~4–5% of GDP for meaningful transfer)
▪ JAM makes delivery feasible; cushions automation/gig precarity & climate shocks
▪ Work-disincentive & inflation risk; may displace merit goods (health/education)
▪ Targeting vs universality dilemma; political irreversibility
＋ Ammo — Finland UBI pilot (2017-18); GiveDirectly (Kenya) RCTs; Rythu Bandhu/KALIA/PM-KISAN as quasi-income support; Amartya Sen vs Jean Drèze debate; "a floor of dignity, not a ceiling on effort".
Way fwd — Consolidate fragmented schemes; a universal social-security floor via Code on Social Security + e-Shram; start with quasi-UBI for the poorest.
Concl — The goal is a universal social-security floor — the route (UBI vs targeted) must fit India’s fiscal reality.
21. Elderly Welfare & Ageing (Ayushman 70+) [covers Q33, Q34]

Intro — India’s 60+ will double to ~20.8% by 2050 (UNFPA India Ageing Report 2023) ; the Oct-2024 extension of AB-PMJAY to all 70+ (income-blind) is a landmark step toward universal, life-course health coverage.
Gains
Gaps
▪ Income-blind ₹5 lakh cover — Ayushman Vay Vandana Card (~6 cr seniors)
▪ Health spend ~2% GDP (NHP target 2.5%); acute geriatric-specialist & infra deficit
▪ Maintenance & Welfare of Parents & Senior Citizens Act 2007 ; NPHCE; National Policy for Older Persons (1999)
▪ Feminisation of ageing (women outlive, less pension); old-age income insecurity
▪ SAGE portal, Elder Line 14567, SACRED (employment)
▪ Social isolation, elder abuse, weak long-term & palliative care
＋ Ammo — LASI (Longitudinal Ageing Study, 2020); compare Japan’s Long-Term Care Insurance (2000) ; "demographic dividend has a sunset"; NPS/APY pension gaps; Art 41 (State to secure aid in old age).
Way fwd — Strengthen PHCs/Arogya Mandirs + geriatric workforce; universal non-contributory pension; home & community elder-care; raise health spend.
Concl — A society is judged by how it treats its elders — dignity in ageing is a constitutional promise ( Art 41 ).
22. Tribal Welfare & PVTGs [covers Q35]

Intro — Despite Fifth-Schedule safeguards ( Art 244 ), India’s 10.4 cr tribals (8.6%) — and 75 PVTGs — face the country’s deepest, most- intersectional deprivation (health, education, livelihood, land).
Interventions
Persisting gaps
▪ PM-JANMAN (2023, ₹24,000 cr) for PVTGs; Dharti Aaba Janjatiya Gram Utkarsh Abhiyan (2024)
▪ Land alienation & displacement by mining/dams (rehabilitation deficits)
▪ Forest Rights Act 2006 & PESA 1996 ; Fifth/Sixth Schedule autonomy
▪ Low literacy, high IMR/MMR, sickle-cell burden; malnutrition
▪ EMRS schools; TRIFED, MSP for Minor Forest Produce , Van Dhan Kendras
▪ Weak FRA/PESA implementation ; overlap with LWE/left-wing extremism
＋ Ammo — Xaxa Committee (2014) & Bhuria Committee; Samatha (1997) & Niyamgiri/Vedanta (2013) — gram-sabha consent; "jal-jangal-zameen"; National Sickle Cell Mission (2023); Birsa Munda legacy.
Way fwd — Genuine FRA/PESA rollout; health-education-livelihood convergence; fair R&R; participatory, culture-sensitive design; sickle-cell elimination.
Concl — Tribal justice means jal-jangal-zameen rights honoured , not merely schemes announced.
23. NFSA / PDS & Food Security [covers Q36]
Intro — The National Food Security Act 2013 made food a legal right (Art 47/21) for ~two-thirds of Indians (75% rural, 50% urban); yet "hunger amidst overflowing granaries" persists.
Strengths & reforms
Weaknesses
▪ Legal entitlement 5 kg/person/month ; One Nation One Ration Card portability
▪ Leakages, exclusion/inclusion errors (Census-2011 base outdated); ~40% wrongly identified
▪ End-to-end computerisation, Aadhaar-seeding, e-PoS; PMGKAY free grains (~80 cr, extended to 2028)
▪ Cereal-centric , not nutrition-sensitive; FCI storage & carrying-cost losses
▪ Shanta Kumar Committee (2015) reforms (coverage to 40%, cash option) pending

＋ Ammo — Global Hunger Index 2024: India 105/127 (govt contests methodology); Right to Food case — PUCL v UoI (2001) ; contrast in-kind vs DBT- food; millet-fortification (IYM 2023); "food security must mean nutrition security".
Way fwd — Nutrition-sensitive PDS (millets, fortified rice, pulses, oil); update beneficiary base via Census 2027; DBT-food pilots; storage modernisation.
Concl — Food security must evolve from calorie-sufficiency to nutrition-security — the next PDS frontier.
24. Affordable Housing (PMAY-Urban) [covers Q37]
Intro — "Housing for All" underpins dignity and the right to shelter ( Art 21 , *Olga Tellis, 1985*); PMAY-U 2.0 (2024) targets 1 crore urban homes with a ₹10 lakh cr outlay.
Progress
Structural challenges
▪ Four verticals — ISSR, CLSS, AHP, BLC ; ~1.2 cr sanctioned under PMAY-U
▪ Urban land scarcity & cost ; titling & tenure insecurity
▪ Rental-housing gap — ARHC (migrant rental) under-utilised
▪ Interest subsidy; mandatory women (co-)ownership ; use of new construction tech (GHTC-India)
▪ Affordability mismatch (EWS vs market); slum in-situ redevelopment delays

＋ Ammo — Deepak Parekh Committee (affordable housing); compare Singapore HDB model; RAY (Rajiv Awas Yojana) predecessor; migrant-worker housing (post-2020 exodus); "a house is an asset, a home is dignity".
Way fwd — Land pooling & value capture; scale rental/social housing (ARHC); PPP; in-situ slum upgradation with secure tenure.
Concl — Real "Housing for All" needs affordable land and rental options , not ownership subsidies alone.
9 · LAWS & BODIES FOR PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE SECTIONS
25. Women’s Political Reservation [covers Q38]
