＋ VERIFIED 16th-FC FINALS (Feb 2026, this card + T9): share retained 41%; criteria — income distance 42.5%, population(2011) 17.5%, GDP contribution 10% (NEW), demographic performance 10%, area 10%, forest 10% (tax-effort dropped); local-body grants ₹9.47 lakh cr (RLB ₹4.4 L cr — 80% basic/20% performance; ULB ₹3.6 L cr with urbanisation premium; disaster ₹1.56 L cr); fiscal path — Centre FD 3.5% of GDP by 2030-31, States 3% GSDP; combined debt 77.3% → 73.1%. Critique intact: cess/surcharge pool-shrinkage unaddressed.
T10 · GST Council & Cooperative Federalism (covers Q19, Q20)  [15m]
Intro (concept): The GST Council (Art 279A, 101st Amdt 2016) is India's boldest experiment in “pooled sovereignty” (Vijay Kelkar) — Union 1/3, States 2/3, decisions by a 75% majority.
Intro (case): Union of India v Mohit Minerals (2022) held its recommendations persuasive, not binding — empowering both tiers yet unsettling predictability.
H1 — Cooperative-federal features:
H2 — Centralising strains:
▪ Concurrent taxing: Art 246A (dual GST); 269A (IGST) — a shared tax base.
▪ One market: “one nation, one tax” subsuming a maze of levies.
▪ Joint forum: structured Union-State bargaining; historically consensus-driven.
▪ Voting design: 75% threshold prevents unilateral Union action.
▪ Recent: GST 2.0 rate rationalisation (2025) needed renewed consensus.
▪ Binding-ness gone: Mohit Minerals frees States to legislate but weakens uniformity.
▪ Compensation-cess lapse (2022) — vertical trust deficit.
▪ Revenue autonomy loss: States surrendered buoyant taxes (VAT/octroi).
▪ Cess dependence shrinks the shareable pool feeding States.
▪ Dispute-resolution [279A(11)] mechanism remains un-operationalised.
Way Forward: operationalise the dispute-resolution mechanism · honour compensation commitments · decide by consensus, not majority steamroll · empower States on rate design.
Conclusion: A genuine federal innovation drifting toward Union leverage — reviving consensus keeps GST cooperative, not coercive.
T11 · Centre–State Coordination Mechanisms (covers Q21)  [15m]
Intro (concept): Cooperative federalism is a constitutional aspiration — Art 1's “Union of States” balances a strong Centre with genuine collaboration (Granville Austin).
Intro (fact): Rising friction over cesses, central agencies and the Governor's office has revived demand for standing coordination forums.
H1 — Institutional mechanisms:
H2 — Friction & strengthening:
▪ Inter-State Council (Art 263): set up 1990 on Sarkaria; a constitutional dispute-cum-dialogue body.
▪ Zonal Councils: statutory (SR Act 1956) — regional coordination.
▪ NITI Aayog: Governing Council; competitive federalism via rankings.
▪ Fiscal fora: Finance & GST Commissions/Councils.
▪ Judicial route: Art 131 original jurisdiction for Centre-State disputes.
▪ Fiscal: cess/surcharge, tied grants, delayed transfers.
▪ Agencies: ED/CBI federalism concerns; consent withdrawals.
▪ Governor's office: assent/President's-Rule flashpoints.
▪ Legislative: Concurrent-List encroachment; “freebies” debate.
▪ Fixes: revive ISC as a standing body (Punchhi); pre-legislative consultation; institutional fiscal dialogue.
Way Forward: make the Inter-State Council meet regularly with teeth · move from “competitive” slogans to “collaborative” forums · consult States before Concurrent-List laws.
Conclusion: Federal trust needs standing institutions, not photo-ops — cooperative federalism must be practised, not merely proclaimed.
T12 · Inter-State River Water Disputes (covers Q22)  [15m]
Intro (fact): Article 262 and the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act, 1956 sought to depoliticise water-sharing, yet Cauvery, Krishna and Ravi-Beas reveal chronic delay and defiance.
Intro (concept): Water — a State-List subject with inter-State spillovers — is a recurring “federal fault-line.”
H1 — Federal architecture:
H2 — Why tribunals falter + reform:
▪ Bar on courts: Art 262 ousts ordinary jurisdiction; Parliament legislates.
▪ Ad-hoc tribunals: one per dispute under the 1956 Act.
▪ Basin bodies: e.g., Cauvery Water Management Authority (2018).
▪ Residual SC role: appeals persist despite Art 262.
▪ Constitutional entries: Union List 56 (inter-State rivers) vs State List 17 (water).
▪ No timelines: Cauvery took ~28 years; awards delayed and defied.
▪ Data disputes and politicisation of sharing.
▪ Enforcement gap: weak compliance with awards.
▪ 2019 Bill: a single permanent tribunal + Disputes Resolution Committee.
▪ Systemic fix: basin authorities, river-linking caution, a national water framework law.
Way Forward: one permanent, expert tribunal with fixed timelines · basin-level governance · fill data gaps via a national register · link to Art 21 (water) and inter-generational equity.
Conclusion: Water federalism needs adjudication that is timely, technical and enforceable — a permanent tribunal is the missing keystone.
3 · SEPARATION OF POWERS, DISPUTE REDRESSAL & COMPARISON
T13 · Judicial Review — India vs UK (covers Q23)  [15m]
Intro (thinker): India's written Constitution with a basic-structure ceiling breaks from A.V. Dicey's parliamentary sovereignty (“Parliament can make or unmake any law”).
Intro (concept): Judicial review is India's guarantor of constitutional supremacy; in the UK it operates within, not above, Parliament.
H1 — INDIA (constitutional supremacy):
H2 — UNITED KINGDOM (parliamentary sovereignty):
▪ Basis: Arts 13, 32, 226; Constitution is the supreme law.
▪ Power: courts strike down unconstitutional statutes.
▪ Amendment: limited — basic structure inviolable (Kesavananda).
▪ Due process: procedural + substantive review (Maneka, 1978).
▪ Illustration: Minerva Mills (1980); Puttaswamy (2017).
▪ Basis: common law + HRA 1998, Constitutional Reform Act 2005 (UKSC).
▪ Power: only a “declaration of incompatibility” — no strike-down.
▪ No entrenchment: Parliament is legally sovereign.
▪ Evolving: “constitutional statutes” (Thoburn); Miller (2017) on prerogative.
▪ Origin of review: US Marbury v Madison (1803) — the comparative pole.
Way Forward: borrow the UK's deference on policy while keeping India's rights-review · use proportionality (common to both) · guard against both judicial overreach and abdication.
Conclusion: India entrusts the last word to the Constitution, Britain to Parliament — two routes to the same rule-of-law destination.
T14 · India vs UK Parliamentary System (covers Q24)  [15m]
Intro (concept): India adopted the Westminster model but adapted it to a written, federal, republican frame — “a Westminster with Indian characteristics.”
Intro (thinker): The pivot is sovereignty of Parliament (UK) vs supremacy of the Constitution (India).
H1 — Shared Westminster DNA:
H2 — Indian departures & rationale:
▪ Responsible government: collective responsibility (Arts 75(3)/164).
▪ Dual executive: nominal head + real PM-led Cabinet.
▪ Bicameralism and a lower-house-driven mandate.
▪ Accountability tools: Question Hour, motions, no-confidence.
▪ Cabinet secrecy and party discipline.
▪ Written Constitution + judicial review — supremacy of law.
▪ Federal upper house: Rajya Sabha (elected States' chamber) vs the Lords.
▪ Republic: an elected Head of State, not a hereditary monarch.
▪ Single citizenship, codified FRs — unity in diversity.
▪ Anti-defection (10th Sch) — a uniquely Indian graft; integrated judiciary.
Way Forward: retain responsible-government accountability · strengthen committees and debate (Westminster's spine) · resist executive dominance that erodes both models.
Conclusion: India kept Westminster's spirit of responsible government while anchoring it to constitutional, not parliamentary, supremacy.
T14A · Comparison Bank — India vs USA vs France (syllabus: comparison with other constitutional schemes) [10m]

Intro (concept): Beyond Westminster (T13/T14), UPSC’s comparison syllabus rewards a three-way contrast — India’s parliamentary-federal hybrid against the US presidential and French semi-presidential models.
H1 — USA vs India:
H2 — France vs India:
▪ Presidential vs parliamentary: fixed-term executive, strict separation of powers vs fusion with collective responsibility (Art 75(3)).
▪ Semi-presidential: directly-elected President + PM answerable to legislature — "cohabitation" possible; India’s President is a constitutional head.
▪ Judicial review origin: Marbury v Madison (1803) — implied; India’s is express (Arts 13/32/226) but bounded by basic structure.
▪ A-priori review: Conseil Constitutionnel reviews Bills BEFORE promulgation; India reviews only enacted law (save Presidential reference, Art 143).
▪ Federalism: indestructible union of indestructible states, equal Senate vs India — "indestructible Union of destructible states" (Art 3), unequal Rajya Sabha.
▪ Laïcité vs principled distance: France walls religion out of the public sphere (headscarf bans); India engages all faiths equally & reforms them (T30).
▪ Rights style: near-absolute First Amendment vs Art 19’s reasonable restrictions; due process (5th/14th) vs Maneka’s fair-just-reasonable.
▪ Emergency (Art 16, French Const.): sweeping presidential powers; India’s Art 352 post-44th is far more fenced — a comparative safeguard point.
Conclusion: India borrowed eclectically and fenced deliberately — parliamentary form, federal frame, express review, engaged secularism — a scheme comparative only in parts and original in synthesis.
T15 · PIL & Judicial Activism / Legislation (covers Q25, Q26, Q27)  [15m]
Intro (thinker): Architected by Justices P.N. Bhagwati and V.R. Krishna Iyer, PIL relaxed locus standi to make the Court “the last resort for the bewildered and the oppressed” (S.P. Gupta, 1981).
Intro (concept): It embodies the tension between access-to-justice and separation of powers — activism vs adventurism.
H1 — Evolution & achievements:
H2 — Excesses, critique & reform:
▪ Origins: Hussainara Khatoon (1979, undertrials); S.P. Gupta (1981, standing).
▪ Rights & labour: Bandhua Mukti Morcha (1984); Vishaka (1997) guidelines.
▪ Environment: M.C. Mehta — absolute liability, green jurisprudence.
▪ Innovations: epistolary jurisdiction, continuing mandamus, amicus.
▪ Empowerment: a voice for those outside the adversarial system.
▪ Judicial legislation: guidelines entering executive/legislative turf.
▪ Docket clog & frivolity: “publicity/paisa” PILs (Balco, 2002 urged caution).
▪ Policy by decree: courts adjudicating polycentric questions.
▪ Reform: screen frivolous PILs, impose costs, self-restraint.
▪ Institutional cure: legislature/executive must fill the gaps courts step into.
Way Forward: screen and prioritise genuine public-interest cases · exercise self-restraint on policy · pair activism with legislative diligence · protect the poor's access.
Conclusion: PIL is a gap-filler, not a governance substitute — indispensable when other organs sleep, illegitimate as routine rule.
T16 · Article 142 — Complete Justice or Overreach? (covers Q28)  [15m]
Intro (concept): Article 142 empowers the Court to do “complete justice” — a constitutional safety-valve critics fear has hardened into judicial legislation.
Intro (case): Its recent use to declare “deemed assent” was struck down (16th Presidential Reference, 2025) as supplanting a constitutional authority.
H1 — Enabling, gap-filling uses:
H2 — Overreach concerns & limits:
▪ Mass-tort settlement: Union Carbide (1989, Bhopal).
▪ Closure of disputes: Ayodhya (2019) title resolution.
▪ Liberty: Perarivalan release (2022) invoking 142.
▪ Environment/governance: binding directions where law is silent.
▪ Purpose: deliver justice the ordinary process cannot.
▪ Supplant test: SCBA v UoI (1998) — 142 supplements, cannot supplant statute.
▪ No definition: open-ended → an “unruly horse.”
▪ Separation of powers: bypasses the legislative route (deemed assent struck, 2025).
▪ Unpredictability: case-by-case equity erodes certainty.
▪ Self-restraint urged even by the Court itself.
Way Forward: use 142 as a last-resort remedy, not a parallel legislature · articulate reasons and limits · defer to statute where it speaks.
Conclusion: Complete justice must remain a remedy of last resort — power exercised with self-imposed limits, not a standing legislative shortcut.
T17 · Administrative Tribunals (covers Q29)  [10m]
Intro (fact): Introduced by the 42nd Amendment (Arts 323A/323B, on the Swaran Singh Committee), tribunals promised specialised, speedy justice.
Intro (case): L. Chandra Kumar (1997) held judicial review under Arts 32/226 a basic feature — tribunals cannot oust the High Courts.
H1 — Constitutional basis & role:
H2 — Challenges & fixes:
▪ Twin heads: 323A (service — CAT/SAT); 323B (other — NGT, NCLT, AFT).
▪ Rationale: reduce court pendency; domain expertise.
▪ Supervisory line: HCs retain writ jurisdiction over tribunals.
▪ Framework: Tribunal Reforms Act 2021 rationalised tribunals.
▪ Executive control: appointments/tenure (Rojer Mathew 2019; Madras Bar Assn).
▪ Pendency & vacancies: thin infrastructure, delayed appointments.
▪ Appeal overload back onto the Supreme Court.
▪ Independence deficit: parent-ministry as litigant and paymaster.
▪ Reform: an independent National Tribunals Commission for appointments and oversight.
Way Forward: create a National Tribunals Commission · secure tenure and funding autonomy · locate appeals in High Courts, not only the SC · fill vacancies promptly.
Conclusion: Tribunals must supplement, not supplant, the constitutional courts — independence is the price of their legitimacy.
4 · PARLIAMENT & STATE LEGISLATURES
＋ 2026 ammo (T17 tribunals / courts): pendency ~5+ crore; add Mediation Act 2023 & e-Courts Phase III (₹7,210 cr) as docket-relief reforms — pair with NJDG data in any judicial-administration answer.
T18 · Anti-Defection Law — Tenth Schedule (covers Q30, Q31, Q32)  [15m]
Intro (concept): The 52nd Amendment (1985) inserted the Tenth Schedule to cure “Aya Ram, Gaya Ram” defections — stability over horse-trading.
Intro (case): Kihoto Hollohan (1992) made the Speaker a tribunal whose decision is subject to judicial review — the design's central fault-line.
H1 — Scheme & judicial guardrails:
H2 — Defects & reform:
▪ Grounds (para 2): voluntarily giving up membership / voting against the whip.
▪ 91st Amdt (2003): only 2/3 merger exempt; the 1/3 “split” loophole removed.
▪ Adjudicator: Speaker/Chairman decides (para 6).
▪ Review: Kihoto — decision reviewable for mala fides/perversity.
▪ Timelines: Keisham Meghachandra (2020) urged a 3-month norm; Subhash Desai (2023) exposed delay.
▪ Partisan adjudicator: Speaker owes the ruling party — natural-justice deficit.
▪ Strategic delay: indefinite pendency defeats the law's purpose.
▪ Free-speech chill: blanket whip stifles legislative deliberation.
▪ Reform bodies: Dinesh Goswami, Law Commission 170th, 2nd ARC — shift to ECI/President/independent tribunal.
▪ Scope: limit the whip to confidence/money votes.
Way Forward: transfer adjudication to an independent tribunal or the ECI · impose statutory timelines · confine the whip to survival votes · protect intra-party dissent.
Conclusion: A partisan office wielding a quasi-judicial axe strains natural justice — neutral, time-bound adjudication restores the law's original intent.
T19 · Parliamentary Committees (covers Q33, Q34)  [15m]
Intro (concept): Called “mini-Parliaments” and “Parliament's eyes, ears and hands,” committees sustain scrutiny beyond the noisy floor.
Intro (data): Yet only ~16% of Bills were referred to committees in the 17th Lok Sabha, against 71% in the 15th (PRS) — a scrutiny collapse.
H1 — Structure & value:
H2 — Decline & reform:
▪ Financial: PAC (1921, opposition-chaired, audits CAG), Estimates, COPU.
▪ DRSCs: 24 department committees (1993) — year-round oversight.
▪ Ad-hoc/JPC: issue-specific deep dives (Bofors, 2G).
▪ Depth: clause-by-clause, non-partisan scrutiny away from media glare.
▪ Financial control: PAC enforces post-audit accountability.
▪ Non-binding: recommendations can be ignored by the executive.
▪ Falling referrals: many major Bills pass without committee study.
▪ Capacity: thin research support, low attendance.
▪ Reform: mandatory referral + tabling of Action-Taken Reports (Nachiappan).
▪ Strengthen DRSC secretariats and expert inputs.
Way Forward: make committee referral the default for significant Bills · publish action-taken reports · resource committees with research staff · ensure quorum and continuity.
Conclusion: Committees are only as strong as the government's willingness to heed them — institutionalising referral is the reform that matters most.
T20 · Declining Parliament — Sitting Days & Ordinances (covers Q35)  [10m]
Intro (data): Sittings have fallen from ~135 days/year (1st LS) to ~55 (PRS); the ordinance route (Art 123) increasingly bypasses deliberation.
Intro (concept): Parliament risks slipping from law-maker to law-ratifier — eroding its deliberative sovereignty.
H1 — Symptoms:
H2 — Restorative measures:
▪ Fewer sittings and frequent disruptions/adjournments.
▪ Guillotine: ~80% of Budget demands voted without discussion.
▪ Ordinance raj (Arts 123/213): governance by executive fiat.
▪ Thin scrutiny: Bills passed amid din, low committee referral.
▪ Question Hour often washed out.
▪ Minimum sitting days: NCRWC suggested ~100–120.
▪ Curb re-promulgation: Krishna Kumar Singh (2017) — “fraud on the Constitution.”
▪ Precedent: D.C. Wadhwa (1987) flagged Bihar's ordinance abuse.
▪ Deliberation: protect private-members' and opposition time.
▪ Discipline: productivity meter, anti-disruption rules, live-streaming.
Way Forward: legislate a minimum-sittings calendar · penalise disruption and ordinance overuse · mandate committee scrutiny · empower the opposition's agenda space.
Conclusion: Deliberation, not the division lobby alone, legitimises law — reviving debate revives Parliament's primacy.
T21 · Rajya Sabha as Council of States (covers Q36)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Conceived as the federal chamber and a house of “sober second thought,” the Rajya Sabha (Art 80) checks hasty legislation and voices the States.
Intro (fact): Its distinct powers (Arts 249, 312) make it a co-equal reviser, save on money Bills.
H1 — Role & special powers:
H2 — Reality check & reform:
▪ Federal voice: represents States (Art 80); permanent house (Art 83, 1/3 retire biennially).
▪ Art 249: empowers Parliament to legislate on a State-List subject (national interest, 2/3).
▪ Art 312: creation of new All-India Services.
▪ Revising role: delays/reviews Bills; cannot be dissolved.
▪ Deadlock: joint sitting (Art 108) except money Bills.
▪ Money-bill bypass: Aadhaar routed as money Bill (Rojer Mathew → 7-judge reference).
▪ Domicile diluted (2003): upheld in Kuldip Nayar (2006) — federal character weakened.
▪ Party over State: members often vote on party, not State, lines.
▪ Fix: restore domicile; curb money-bill misuse; strengthen its federal mandate.
Way Forward: restore the domicile requirement · limit money-bill certification abuse · use RS committees for federal scrutiny.
Conclusion: The Rajya Sabha shines as a deliberative check but under-delivers as a federal guardian — a gap the Constitution intends to be bridged.
T22 · Parliamentary Privileges (covers Q37)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Article 105 (and 194 for States) vests privileges — freedom of speech in the House and immunity from court proceedings — essential to fearless legislative functioning.
Intro (case): Sita Soren (2024, 7-judge) overruled P.V. Narasimha Rao (1998): a legislator has no immunity for a vote-for-bribe.
H1 — Nature & scope:
H2 — Tension with rights & reform:
▪ Individual: free speech in House; freedom from arrest (civil).
▪ Collective: publish proceedings, exclude strangers, punish for contempt.
▪ Uncodified: Art 105(3) — “as of the British Commons” until codified.
▪ Enforcement: the House's penal power for breach of privilege.
▪ Ambit: limited to what is essential to legislative functioning.
▪ Clash with FRs: potential conflict with Arts 19, 21, 32 (Keshav Singh, 1965).
▪ Bribery not immune: Sita Soren (2024) restores accountability.
▪ Press freedom: privilege vs the citizen's right to know.
▪ Uncertainty: absence of codification breeds arbitrariness.
▪ Reform: codify privileges to balance autonomy with rights.
Way Forward: codify privileges with clear limits · reconcile with fundamental rights and press freedom · use contempt power sparingly and transparently.
Conclusion: Privileges must protect the institution, not shield misconduct — codification is the unfinished reform.
T23 · Leader of the Opposition (covers Q38)  [15m]
Intro (concept): The Leader of the Opposition — a statutory office under the 1977 Act — is the linchpin of accountability: the “shadow Prime Minister.”
Intro (fact): The LoP co-selects the heads of key watchdogs, making the office structurally central to institutional neutrality.
H1 — Constitutional & statutory significance:
H2 — Contemporary concern:
▪ Watchdog panels: member for CBI Director, CVC, CIC, Lokpal, NHRC, CEC appointments.
▪ Voice of dissent: institutionalises scrutiny of the executive.
▪ Government-in-waiting: continuity and alternative policy.
▪ Statutory status: cabinet-rank recognition under the 1977 Act.
▪ Bipartisan balance: checks majoritarian capture of appointments.
▪ Mavalankar convention: 10% (55 seats) threshold denies LoP status.
▪ Vacant office: 16th/17th LS had no recognised LoP.
▪ Skewed panels: absence tilts watchdog appointments toward the executive.
▪ Democratic health: weakens the “loyal opposition” function.
▪ Fix: recognise the single-largest opposition party's leader as LoP.
Way Forward: recognise the largest opposition party's leader as LoP · keep watchdog selection panels balanced · empower the opposition's oversight role.
Conclusion: A democracy is judged by the space it gives dissent — the Leader of the Opposition institutionalises that space.
T24 · One Nation, One Election — 129th Amendment (covers Q39, Q40)  [10m]
Intro (event): The Constitution (129th Amendment) Bill, 2024 (new Article 82A) is before a JPC (P.P. Chaudhary; report due Monsoon 2026) — reviving India's pre-1967 simultaneous polls.
Intro (concept): It pits governance efficiency and cost-saving against federal autonomy and continuous accountability.
H1 — Constitutional feasibility:
H2 — Federal & accountability costs:
▪ New Art 82A + amends Arts 83, 85, 172, 174, 356 and the RPA.
▪ Sync mechanism: an “appointed date”; Assembly terms curtailed to align.
▪ Mid-term polls: only for the unexpired term.
▪ ECI role: power to defer a poll [82A(5)] — flagged by ex-CJI Gogoi.
▪ Ratification likely, given federal impact.
▪ Federal cost: curtailing State terms without consent dents autonomy.
▪ Nationalisation: local issues eclipsed (~77% back the same party in synced polls).
▪ Accountability: fixed terms dilute mid-term answerability.
▪ Regional parties disadvantaged vs national campaigns.
▪ Hung-House risk: may invite President's Rule, centralising power.
Way Forward: phase in with State consensus · safeguard hung-House outcomes · protect federal cycles · weigh Kovind-HLC (2024) gains against democratic costs.
Conclusion: Efficiency must not override federalism — a phased, consensus-based rollout suits a “Union of States.”
5 · EXECUTIVE & ACCOUNTABILITY
T25 · Section 17A PC Act — Accountability vs Protection (covers Q41)  [15m]

Intro (event): Section 17A of the Prevention of Corruption Act (2018) requires prior sanction to even investigate a public servant — a shield the Supreme Court's 2026 split verdict left constitutionally contested.
Intro (concept): It embodies the classic tension between shielding honest officers and enabling swift anti-corruption action.
H1 — The case FOR (protection):
H2 — The case AGAINST (accountability):
▪ Bona fide cover: protects risk-laden official decisions — curbs the chilling effect.
▪ Filter: screens vexatious, motivated complaints early.
▪ Morale: encourages decisive administration (T.S.R. Subramanian reforms).
▪ Judicial view: J. Viswanathan upheld it in principle.
▪ Comparative: sanction regimes exist to prevent harassment of civil servants.
▪ Shields the corrupt: pre-investigation sanction can stall probes.
▪ Executive gatekeeping: same-government sanction dilutes independence.
▪ Judicial view: J. Nagarathna held it unconstitutional → larger Bench.
▪ Access-to-justice: delay erodes deterrence.
▪ Precedent: Vineet Narain (1998) insisted on insulated investigation.
Way Forward: vest sanction in an independent body (Lokpal/Lokayukta) · impose a statutory time-limit · separate investigation from the parent department.
Conclusion: Locate sanction in an independent body with strict timelines — accountability without deterring honest decisions.
6 · JUDICIARY
T26 · Collegium System & Judicial Appointments (covers Q42, Q43)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Evolved through the Three Judges Cases (1981/1993/1998), the collegium vests appointment primacy in the judiciary — its opacity the central reform debate.
Intro (case): SC Advocates-on-Record v Union (2015) struck down the NJAC (99th Amdt) 4:1 — judicial independence is basic structure.
H1 — The case FOR transparency:
H2 — The independence caveat:
▪ Public reasoning: publish criteria and resolutions to curb arbitrariness.
▪ Codified MoP: objective Memorandum of Procedure for merit and diversity.
▪ End perceptions of insularity (“you scratch my back”).
▪ Secretariat: a permanent body to vet candidates (2nd ARC).
▪ Diversity: widen social and gender representation.
▪ No executive veto: transparency must not become a government lever (NJAC lesson).
▪ Deliberative candour: some confidentiality aids frank assessment.
▪ MoP deadlock between judiciary and executive persists.
▪ Primacy preserved: judicial primacy is the constitutional safeguard.
▪ Balance: accountability of process, autonomy of decision.
Way Forward: adopt a criteria-based MoP with reasoned outcomes · create an appointments secretariat · widen diversity without ceding primacy.
Conclusion: The goal is accountable independence — transparency in standards with autonomy in decision, legitimacy without surrendering primacy.
T27 · Writs — Article 32 vs Article 226 (covers Q44)  [10m]
Intro (thinker): Ambedkar called Article 32 the “heart and soul of the Constitution” — the guaranteed right to constitutional remedies that animates every other right.
Intro (concept): Together, Arts 32 and 226 make fundamental rights enforceable, not ornamental.
H1 — Article 32 (Supreme Court):
H2 — Article 226 (High Courts):
▪ Scope: only for Fundamental Rights.
▪ Status: a fundamental right itself; suspendable only under Art 359 (emergency).
▪ Reach: pan-India; SC is guarantor of Part III.
▪ Basic feature: Art 32 itself part of basic structure (Coelho).
▪ Direct access to the apex court for rights violations.
▪ Scope: FRs + other legal rights — wider than Art 32.
▪ Nature: discretionary, not a guaranteed right.
▪ Reach: broad territorial jurisdiction (cause of action).
▪ Five writs: habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari, quo warranto.
▪ Workhorse: the everyday engine of administrative-law review.
Way Forward: preserve Art 32's guaranteed access · use Art 226's breadth for legal-rights review · resist dilution of writ jurisdiction (basic feature).
Conclusion: The two writs are the twin engines of the rule of law — making rights real, not rhetorical.
7 · FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS, DPSP & INTERPLAY
T28 · FR–DPSP Interplay & Harmonious Construction (covers Q45)  [15m]
Intro (thinker): Granville Austin called the DPSP the “conscience of the Constitution”; FR and DPSP are the “two wheels of the chariot” of social justice.
Intro (case): Minerva Mills (1980) held the harmony and balance between FR and DPSP to be basic structure.
H1 — Evolution of the balance:
H2 — The settled doctrine:
▪ Primacy fights: Champakam Dorairajan (1951) → 1st Amdt (Art 15(4)).
▪ Amend-ability: Golaknath → Kesavananda → 25th Amdt (Art 31C).
▪ Overreach struck: 42nd Amdt extended 31C to all DPSP — voided in Minerva Mills.
▪ Rights from directives: Unni Krishnan (1993) — RTE from Arts 21+45.
▪ Status: DPSP non-justiciable yet “fundamental in governance” (Art 37).
▪ Harmonious construction: read FR and DPSP together, neither subordinate.
▪ Welfare state: DPSP give purpose; FRs give liberty.
▪ Interpretive aid: DPSP inform the meaning of FRs (esp. Art 21).
▪ Instrument of instructions: a moral compass binding on governance.
▪ Balance is basic structure: amendments cannot destroy it.
Way Forward: advance DPSP goals through rights-respecting means · legislate welfare within FR limits · use harmonious construction to reconcile liberty and justice.
Conclusion: The Constitution's conscience lies in their synthesis — liberty without justice is hollow, justice without liberty is tyranny.
T29 · Expanding Horizons of Article 21 (covers Q46)  [10m]
Intro (case): Since Maneka Gandhi (1978) — which forged the “golden triangle” (Arts 14-19-21) and a “fair, just and reasonable” test — Article 21 has become a fount of dignity-based rights.
Intro (concept): The Court treats the Constitution as a “living instrument,” reading new rights into “life and personal liberty.”
H1 — Landmark expansions:
H2 — Mechanism & caveat:
▪ Privacy: K.S. Puttaswamy (2017, 9-judge) — intrinsic to Art 21.
▪ Right to walk: Maniyar Iliyaz (2026) — safe footpaths (Arts 19(1)(d), 21).
▪ Livelihood & shelter: Olga Tellis (1985).
▪ Dignity in death: Common Cause (2018) — passive euthanasia/living will.
▪ Clean environment, health, speedy trial read in over decades.
▪ Purposive reading: liberty construed expansively post-Maneka.
▪ Positive obligations: State must create enabling conditions, not merely refrain.
▪ Remedies: compensation and mandamus for breach.
▪ From Gopalan to Maneka: silos → integrated rights.
▪ Caveat: expansion needs executive delivery to avoid paper rights.
Way Forward: match expansive rights with delivery mechanisms · budget for positive obligations · guard against over-judicialisation of policy.
Conclusion: Article 21 has grown from a shield of life into a charter of dignified existence — jurisprudence that must be matched by implementation.
T30 · Secularism, Principled Distance & Endowments (covers Q47)  [15m]
Intro (case): Affirmed as basic structure in S.R. Bommai (1994) and re-anchored by the Preamble in Balram Singh v Union (2024), Indian secularism is “principled distance” (Rajeev Bhargava).
Intro (concept): Sarva dharma sambhava — equal respect, not a wall of separation — distinguishes India's positive secularism from the US model.
H1 — Constitutional scheme:
H2 — Reconciling regulation & autonomy:
▪ Freedom: Arts 25–28; 25(2)(a) regulate secular activity; 25(2)(b) reform + temple-entry.
▪ Denominational autonomy: Art 26 — manage own affairs in religion.
▪ No religion tax (Art 27); no religious instruction (Art 28).
▪ Minority institutions: Art 30 — establish and administer.
▪ Basic structure: secularism inviolable (Bommai).
▪ Line-drawing: the essential-practices test (Shirur Mutt, 1954; Sabarimala, 2018).
▪ State regulates administration, not doctrine.
▪ Live tests: Waqf (Amdt) Act 2025 (board composition) tests Art 26 limits.
▪ Places of Worship Act 1991 guards the secular equilibrium.
▪ Comparative: positive engagement vs US non-establishment.
Way Forward: regulate secular/administrative aspects, never doctrine · hold the line between reform and interference · protect minority autonomy (Arts 26, 30).
Conclusion: India reforms faith without exiling it — the line between regulation and interference is the true test of secular fidelity.
T31 · Essential Religious Practices Doctrine (covers Q48)  [15m]
Intro (case): The “essential religious practices” (ERP) test, born in Shirur Mutt (1954), lets courts decide which practices merit constitutional protection.
Intro (concept): It casts judges, controversially, as arbiters of theology — protecting faith's core while enabling reform.
H1 — How the doctrine works:
H2 — The critique & alternative:
▪ Test: only essential practices immune under Arts 25/26.
▪ Applications: Ananda Marga (tandava); Ismail Faruqui (mosque not essential).
▪ Reform enabler: Shayara Bano (2017) — triple talaq not essential, hence void.
▪ Access: Sabarimala (2018) — exclusion fails the test.
▪ Secular acts remain regulable by the State.
▪ Competence: courts as theologians — institutional overreach?
▪ Inconsistency: Hijab split (2022) → larger bench.
▪ Freezing faith: essentiality may ossify evolving belief.
▪ Alternative test: shift to anti-exclusion + constitutional morality (Chandrachud J.).
▪ Rights-first framing over doctrinal adjudication.
Way Forward: move from “essentiality” toward an anti-exclusion, constitutional-morality test · defer to communities on doctrine · intervene only against exclusion/harm.
Conclusion: ERP protects faith's core but overreaches into doctrine — a rights-based, less theological test would serve constitutional secularism better.
T32 · Uniform Civil Code — Article 44 (covers Q49)  [10m]

Intro (fact): Article 44 (DPSP) directs the State toward a Uniform Civil Code; Uttarakhand's UCC (2025) — the first by a State (Goa retains its Portuguese code) — moved the debate from directive to statute.
Intro (concept): The UCC embodies gender justice and national integration vs pluralism and personal-law autonomy.
H1 — Constitutional vision:
H2 — Why uniformity is complex:
▪ Equality goal: common rules delinking civil law from religion (Arts 14/15).
▪ Judicial nudges: Shah Bano (1985), Sarla Mudgal (1995), Shayara Bano (2017).
▪ Praise for Goa: Jose Paulo Coutinho (2019).
▪ Integration: Ambedkar/Munshi saw it as a unifier.
▪ Religious freedom: Art 25 apprehension among minorities.
▪ Diversity: tribal/customary law — Uttarakhand exempts STs.
▪ Federal patchwork: Concurrent-List → State-by-State codes risk fragmentation.
▪ Expert caution: Law Commission (2018) — “not necessary or desirable” then; reform within personal laws.
▪ Drafting which practices to standardise is contentious.
Way Forward: reform within personal laws first · build consensus, not imposition · protect tribal/customary exemptions · pursue an optional, phased common code.
Conclusion: A constitutional aspiration best realised incrementally — “unity in diversity,” uniformity earned, not enforced.

＋ 2026 ammo (T32 UCC): Uttarakhand UCC operational since Jan 2025 — first-year learnings: live-in registration controversy (privacy vs protection), tribal exemption held; other States watching — argue "laboratory federalism".
T33 · DPDP Act 2023 & the Right to Privacy (covers Q50, Q51)  [10m]
Intro (case): The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 operationalises the privacy right in K.S. Puttaswamy (2017, 9-judge) — a consent-based architecture (B.N. Srikrishna lineage).
Intro (concept): It must balance individual privacy against national security and digital governance via proportionality.
H1 — Key provisions:
H2 — Concerns:
▪ Consent + fiduciary duties: notice, purpose limitation, data minimisation.
▪ Regulator: the Data Protection Board; Significant Data Fiduciaries.
▪ Rights: access, correction, erasure, grievance, nomination.
▪ Penalties: up to ₹250 crore for breaches.
▪ Cross-border transfer on a negative-list basis.
▪ State exemptions: wide Sec 17 carve-outs for government agencies.
▪ RTI dilution: amends Sec 8(1)(j) to bar all personal information.
▪ Board independence: executive-appointed, autonomy questioned.
▪ Surveillance risk absent a data-protection-by-design mandate.
▪ Deemed consent ambiguities.
Way Forward: restore the RTI public-interest override · insulate the Board · narrow state exemptions · enforce proportionality (Puttaswamy) and purpose limitation.
Conclusion: A landmark first step whose credibility hinges on an independent Board and a genuine privacy-transparency-security balance.
T34 · Free Speech & Social-Media Regulation (covers Q52, Q53)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Article 19(1)(a) is “the ark of the covenant of democracy,” but bows to the reasonable restrictions of Art 19(2) — a balance tested by algorithmic amplification.
Intro (case): Shreya Singhal (2015) struck §66A for vagueness and chilling effect — the touchstone of digital-speech jurisprudence.
H1 — Constitutional limits:
H2 — Digital-age flashpoints:
▪ Eight grounds (Art 19(2)): security, public order, decency, defamation, incitement, etc.
▪ Proportionality: restrictions must be narrowly tailored.
▪ §66A struck: Shreya Singhal (2015).
▪ Shutdowns reviewable: Anuradha Bhasin (2020).
▪ Blocking (§69A) read down with safeguards.
▪ IT Rules 2021: traceability + intermediary due-diligence — free-speech concerns.
▪ Fact-check unit struck: Kunal Kamra v Union (2024).
▪ Takedown portals litigated (2025) — executive overreach worries.
▪ Hate speech & misinformation amplified by algorithms.
▪ Intermediary liability (§79) safe-harbour debates.
Way Forward: keep restrictions narrow and reviewable · self-regulation + due process for takedowns · counter falsehood with more speech, not blanket bans.
Conclusion: Free speech is the rule, restriction the narrow exception — vagueness and overbreadth are constitutionally fatal.
T35 · Sedition — §124A IPC to §152 BNS (covers Q54)  [15m]
Intro (case): From Kedar Nath Singh (1962) — which saved §124A only for incitement to violence — sedition law has travelled to its BNS §152 successor.
Intro (concept): The debate is whether renaming a colonial offence amounts to reform or merely re-arms it.
H1 — Old regime & judicial curbs:
H2 — The BNS successor:
▪ Origin: §124A (Macaulay, added 1870) — “disaffection.”
▪ Narrowed: Kedar Nath (1962) — only incitement to violence/public disorder.
▪ Protective rulings: Balwant Singh (1995); Vinod Dua (2021).
▪ Suspended: §124A in abeyance (S.G. Vombatkere, 2022).
▪ Misuse data: low conviction, high pendency (NCRB).
▪ §152 BNS: acts endangering sovereignty, unity, integrity.
▪ Word dropped: “sedition” gone — critics say the offence is wider, vaguer.
▪ “Subversive activities” left undefined.
▪ Chilling effect on dissent and journalism persists.
▪ Safeguard: must meet the Kedar Nath incitement standard (Law Commission 279th).
Way Forward: read down §152 to genuine incitement · add procedural safeguards (prior sanction, judicial scrutiny) · protect dissent as constitutionally valuable.
Conclusion: Renaming is not reform — the successor must be confined to incitement, or it revives the very colonial impulse it replaced.
T36 · Preventive Detention & Article 21 (covers Q55)  [15m]
Intro (concept): India is among the few democracies to constitutionalise preventive detention in peacetime (Art 22(3)–(7)) — a “necessary evil” Ambedkar accepted reluctantly.
Intro (case): A.K. Gopalan (1950)'s narrow reading gave way to Maneka Gandhi (1978)'s due-process standard.
H1 — Constitutional framework:
H2 — Compatibility concerns:
▪ Safeguards: Art 22(4) — max 3 months without an advisory board; 22(5) — grounds furnished.
▪ Statutes: NSA, UAPA, PMLA, PSA (J&K), COFEPOSA, NDPS.
▪ 44th Amdt: 2-month cap — not notified.
▪ Advisory board: the core procedural check.
▪ Judicial review of detention orders.
▪ Due process: procedure must be “just, fair and reasonable” (Rekha, 2011).
▪ Executive misuse: “bail as exception” inverts liberty.
▪ Low conviction rates — pre-emptive punishment risk.
▪ Rights erosion: strains Arts 21 and 14.
▪ Exceptional nature: must be time-bound and reasoned.
Way Forward: narrow grounds and shorten detention windows · strengthen advisory-board scrutiny · ensure prompt communication of grounds · judicial vigilance.
Conclusion: Preventive detention must remain exceptional and time-bound — liberty is the rule, detention the narrow exception.
T37 · Custodial Violence & Institutional Reform (covers Q56)  [10m]
Intro (case): Custodial violence is “the worst crime in a civilised society” (D.K. Basu, 1997) — a direct assault on Arts 20(3), 21 and 22.
Intro (data): NHRC records thousands of custodial deaths annually — an accountability and human-rights failure.
H1 — Constitutional safeguards:
H2 — Reform agenda:
▪ Arrest guidelines: D.K. Basu (1997) — arrest memo, medical exam, information to kin.
▪ Compensation: Nilabati Behera (1993) — public-law remedy.
▪ Against self-incrimination: Art 20(3); Selvi (2010) — no narco-coercion.
▪ Mandatory CCTV: Paramvir Singh Saini (2020).
▪ Right to legal aid at arrest (Art 22(1)).
▪ Ratify UNCAT (signed 1997, not ratified) + a standalone anti-torture law (Law Commission 273rd).
▪ Police reforms: Prakash Singh (2006) — separate investigation from law-and-order.
▪ Forensic autonomy and body-cams reduce “third-degree.”
▪ Independent oversight of custodial deaths.
▪ Sensitisation & accountability of personnel.
Way Forward: enact an anti-torture statute and ratify UNCAT · implement Prakash Singh reforms · mandate CCTV/forensics · ensure swift, independent inquiry.
Conclusion: A state that tortures forfeits its moral authority — accountability, not impunity, upholds the rule of law.
T38 · Death Penalty & Rarest-of-Rare Doctrine (covers Q57)  [15m]
Intro (case): Upheld as constitutional in Bachan Singh (1980, 4:1) but confined to the “rarest of rare,” the death penalty is a contested exception to Article 21.
Intro (concept): It frames the clash between retributive and reformative theories of justice.
H1 — Constitutional validity:
H2 — Concerns & reform:
▪ Doctrine: Bachan Singh (1980); Machhi Singh (1983) — five categories.
▪ No mandatory death: Mithu (1983) struck it down.
▪ Mercy: Arts 72/161; Shatrughan Chauhan (2014) — delay/mental illness commute.
▪ Mitigation mandatory: Manoj (2022) — hear mitigating circumstances.
▪ Rarest-of-rare as the constitutional filter.
▪ Arbitrariness: judge-centric sentencing; socio-economic bias (Project 39A).
▪ Law Commission 262nd (2015): abolish except terrorism.
▪ Irreversibility amid wrongful-conviction risk.
▪ Global trend: 140+ states abolitionist.
▪ Deterrence empirically doubtful.
Way Forward: standardise sentencing with mandatory mitigation hearings · reduce the death-eligible offences · strengthen legal aid on death row · move toward abolition-with-exceptions debate.
Conclusion: Retained in law yet retreating in practice — the “rarest of rare” filter must be principled, not impressionistic.
8 · PRESSURE GROUPS & CIVIL SOCIETY
T39 · Pressure Groups & Civil Society (FCRA) (covers Q59, Q60)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Pressure groups and civil society are the “third pillar of democracy” — articulating interests and holding power to account without seeking office (Almond & Powell).
Intro (event): The SKM farmers' movement forcing the repeal of the farm laws (2021) showed organised interests reshaping State-society relations.
H1 — Role & methods:
H2 — Contemporary challenges:
▪ Types: institutional, associational (FICCI/CII/SEWA), non-associational, anomic.
▪ Methods: lobbying, agitation, litigation/PIL, electoral pressure.
▪ Policy wins: MKSS → RTI (2005); Narmada Bachao Andolan.
▪ Watchdog role: transparency, advocacy, service delivery.
▪ Mobilisation: media and social-media campaigns.
▪ FCRA 2020: SBI-Delhi account, no sub-granting, 20% admin cap (Noel Harper, 2022 upheld).
▪ “Shrinking civic space”: ~16,000 FCRA registrations cancelled.
▪ Funding chokepoints and compliance burdens.
▪ Fragmentation & elite capture dilute influence.
▪ “Street veto” vs institutional channels tension.
Way Forward: institutionalise pre-legislative consultation · calibrate FCRA to genuine security needs · protect legitimate advocacy and dissent · fund grassroots capacity.
Conclusion: A vibrant civil society deepens participatory democracy — over-regulation risks silencing democracy's early-warning system.
9 · REPRESENTATION OF THE PEOPLE ACT & ELECTORAL PROCESS
T40 · Special Intensive Revision (SIR) & ECI's Plenary Power (covers Q61)  [15m]  ★ cross-confirmed
Intro (quote): “The power under Article 324 is plenary, but not unlimited.” The Supreme Court's 2026 SIR verdict validated roll-purification while fencing it with due process.
Intro (concept): The case tests the frontier between electoral integrity and the citizen's protection from arbitrary disenfranchisement.
H1 — Source & scope of power:
H2 — Judicially recognised limits:
▪ Constitutional: Art 324 superintendence, direction and control.
▪ Statutory: §21, RPA 1950 — special revision “at any time.”
▪ Objective test: SIR not “manifestly excessive or disproportionate.”
▪ Purpose: clean, accurate rolls as a pillar of free elections.
▪ Scale: ~47 lakh Bihar deletions survived scrutiny.
▪ No citizenship ruling: the ECI cannot adjudicate citizenship (Union's domain).
▪ Presumption of inclusion: rebuttable (Lal Babu Hussein, 1995; Md. Rahim Ali, 2024).
▪ Due process: publish exclusion lists; real notice and objection windows.
▪ No burden-flip wholesale onto voters.
▪ Accessibility: gram-panchayat/website publication mandated.
Way Forward: revise rolls transparently and rule-bound · separate roll-cleaning from citizenship checks · guarantee notice, appeal and accessibility · protect Art 326 suffrage.
Conclusion: Roll integrity is essential to free elections, but must never become disenfranchisement by executive fiat.
T41 · Nature of the Right to Vote (covers Q62)  [10m]
Intro (concept): Though Article 326 guarantees adult suffrage, courts have generally held the right to vote a statutory/constitutional right — not a Part-III fundamental right.
Intro (case): N.P. Ponnuswami (1952) and Jyoti Basu (1982) route electoral disputes through the statutory scheme.
H1 — Doctrinal position:
H2 — Safeguards vs exclusion:
▪ Statutory view: the vote flows from the RPA; disputes are statutory.
▪ Constitutional core: Art 326; NOTA recognised (PUCL, 2013).
▪ Toward higher status: obiter in Anoop Baranwal (2023).
▪ Disqualifications: §8 RPA (Lily Thomas, 2013).
▪ Adult suffrage as a founding constitutional value.
▪ Presumption of inclusion (Lal Babu Hussein).
▪ Due process: notice, hearing, appeal before deletion.
▪ No citizenship adjudication by the ECI.
▪ Transparency: published rolls and objection windows.
▪ Judicial review of arbitrary exclusion.
Way Forward: codify robust due-process before deletion · consider elevating suffrage's protection · guarantee accessible remedies for the excluded.
Conclusion: However classified, the vote is democracy's foundational act — deserving of the highest procedural protection.
T41A · RPA 1951 — Corrupt Practices, Disqualification & Electoral Reform — NEW gap card [15m]

Intro (concept): The Representation of the People Act, 1951 is the operating system of Indian elections — its disqualification (§8) and corrupt-practices (§123) chapters are where the purity of the ballot is actually litigated.

Intro (case): Lily Thomas (2013) struck §8(4) — convicted legislators lose the seat at once; Abhiram Singh (2017, 7-judge) held any appeal to religion/caste/community/language of ANY candidate or voter a corrupt practice under §123(3) — the "secular ballot" ruling.
H1 — The statutory architecture:
H2 — Reform agenda (committee-anchored):
▪ §8 disqualification: conviction-based; 2-yr+ sentence → 6-yr bar (Lily Thomas, 2013); §8A — corrupt practice; §9 — dismissal for corruption.
▪ Criminalisation: ~46% of MPs elected 2024 face criminal cases (ADR) — SC: speedy trial of MP/MLA cases, candidates must publicise antecedents (Rambabu Singh Thakur, 2020).
▪ §123 corrupt practices: bribery, undue influence, religion/community appeal (Abhiram Singh), false statements (§123(4)); election void if proved.
▪ Money power: electoral bonds struck down (ADR v UoI, 2024 — Art 19(1)(a) voter’s right to know); state-funding debate (Indrajit Gupta, 1998); National Electoral Fund idea.
▪ §125A: false affidavit — but weak penalty; §126: 48-hour silence period — social media a grey zone.
▪ Law Commission 244th/255th: bar candidates once charges framed (heinous offences); cap party expenditure; totaliser for booth-secrecy.
▪ §29A: party registration — but ECI cannot de-register (Indian National Congress v Institute of Social Welfare, 2002) — a known gap.
▪ Process reforms: one-candidate-one-seat (§33(7) amendment demand); remote/migrant voting (RVM pilot debate); Form-17C transparency & roll-purity (link T40 SIR).

Way Forward: empower ECI to de-register defunct/errant parties · decide framed-charges disqualification legislatively · transparent political finance (post-bonds architecture) · fast-track MP/MLA courts.
Conclusion: Free elections die not by one blow but by a thousand statutory gaps — closing RPA’s enforcement deficits is as constitutional a project as any amendment.
10 · CONSTITUTIONAL & STATUTORY BODIES
T42 · Election Commission — Appointments & Independence (covers Q63, Q64)  [15m]
Intro (quote): “The independence of the Election Commission is the surest guarantee of free and fair elections.” The CEC & Other ECs Act, 2023 recast appointments after Anoop Baranwal (2023).
Intro (concept): ECI autonomy (Art 324) is foundational to the credibility of Indian democracy — the Seshan legacy of assertive neutrality.
H1 — Appointment framework:
H2 — Independence debate:
▪ Constitutional base: Art 324; multi-member since 1993.
▪ Selection Committee: PM + a Union Cabinet Minister + LoP.
▪ Change: the CJI was dropped from the court-mandated interim panel.
▪ Search-cum-selection shortlist precedes the committee.
▪ Tenure: 6 years or 65; CEC removable only like an SC judge.
▪ Executive tilt: 2 of 3 members from government — SC challenge pending.
▪ ECs weaker: removable on the CEC's recommendation.
▪ Neutrality tests: MCC enforcement, poll scheduling.
▪ Reform: restore an independent member/CJI to the panel.
▪ Funding & security of conditions bolster autonomy.
Way Forward: compose the selection panel neutrally · insulate ECs' removal · secure independent funding · state funding of elections (Goswami/Tarkunde) to curb money power.
Conclusion: Free elections rest on a fearless ECI — composition, not just tenure, must guarantee its neutrality.
T43 · Comptroller & Auditor General (covers Q65, Q66)  [10m]
Intro (thinker): Ambedkar deemed the CAG “the most important officer under the Constitution” (Arts 148–151) — the “guardian of the public purse.”
Intro (concept): As the auditor of all government spending, the CAG converts financial accountability from slogan to scrutiny.
H1 — Powers & functions:
H2 — Challenges & reform:
▪ Mandate (DPC Act 1971): audits Consolidated Fund, Contingency, Public Account.
▪ Audit types: financial, compliance and performance audits.
▪ Reporting line: reports → President/Governor → PAC.
▪ Landmark reports: 2G, coal-block allocations.
▪ “Friend, philosopher, guide” to the PAC.
▪ Post-facto & advisory: “watchdog, not bloodhound.”
▪ Coverage gap: PPPs, off-budget/SPV financing outside the net.
▪ Single-member: 2nd ARC urged a multi-member Board.
▪ Capacity: staffing, timeliness, data access.
▪ Local audit of PRIs/ULBs weak.
Way Forward: extend audit to PPPs and off-budget borrowings · move to a multi-member CAG · real-time/IT-enabled auditing · strengthen local-body audit.
Conclusion: The CAG makes accountability real, not rhetorical — its reach must keep pace with new financing modes.
T44 · Lokpal & Anti-Corruption Architecture (covers Q67)  [15m]
Intro (event): Born of the 2011 anti-corruption movement, the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 created an ombudsman to probe graft at the highest levels.
Intro (concept): Its lineage runs from Santhanam (1962) and the First ARC — its promise still outrunning its performance.
H1 — Design:
H2 — Performance & reform:
▪ Composition: Chair + 8 members (half judicial).
▪ Jurisdiction: PM (with safeguards), ministers, MPs, Group A–D, foreign-funded trusts.
▪ First Lokpal: Justice P.C. Ghose (2019).
▪ Wings: Inquiry + Prosecution.
▪ Asset disclosure and complaint mechanism.
▪ Delayed operationalisation; few prosecutions.
▪ No suo motu; depends on the CBI for investigation.
▪ LoP-vacancy complicated selection.
▪ Weak whistle-blower cover: WBP Act 2014 not operational.
▪ Understaffing and procedural delays.
Way Forward: empower independent investigation and suo-motu powers · operationalise whistle-blower protection · impose timelines · fully staff the institution.
Conclusion: An institution's credibility lies in its bite — the Lokpal must move from symbolism to sanction.
T45 · National Human Rights Commission (covers Q68)  [15m]
Intro (fact): Established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 and guided by the Paris Principles, the NHRC is the “watchdog of human rights.”
Intro (concept): But a watchdog that can bark, not bite — its recommendatory powers limit its efficacy.
H1 — Functioning:
H2 — Reform imperatives:
▪ Composition: Chair (ex-CJI/SC judge) + members; SHRCs mirror it.
▪ Functions: inquiry, intervention, awareness, research.
▪ Reach: custodial deaths, human-rights violations.
▪ Advisory role: recommends action to governments.
▪ Autonomy: statutory, not constitutional, body.
▪ Non-binding: recommendations lack enforcement.
▪ Armed forces: cannot directly investigate — only seeks a report.
▪ One-year limitation bars older complaints.
▪ GANHRI 'A' status deferred — pluralism/independence concerns.
▪ Funding/staffing dependence on the executive.
Way Forward: grant enforceable orders and armed-forces jurisdiction · diversify membership · secure financial autonomy per the Paris Principles · lift the limitation bar.
Conclusion: Human-rights protection needs teeth, not testimonials — empowering the NHRC is overdue.
T46 · Right to Information Act (covers Q69)  [15m]
Intro (case): The RTI Act, 2005 made “information the currency of democracy,” rooting the right to know in Art 19(1)(a) (Raj Narain, 1975).
Intro (concept): It turned the citizen from petitioner into auditor of the State — the 2nd ARC's “master key to good governance.”
H1 — Empowerment:
H2 — Institutional erosion:
▪ Architecture: PIO → first appeal → CIC/SIC.
▪ Proactive disclosure: §4 — suo-motu publication.
▪ Impact: exposed scams; empowered the marginalised.
▪ Accountability tool across all public authorities.
▪ Citizen oversight of governance.
▪ 2019 Amendment: IC tenure/salary set by the Centre — independence diluted.
▪ DPDP 2023: guts §8(1)(j) — bars all personal information.
▪ Vacancies & pendency cripple Commissions.
▪ Attacks on RTI activists deter use.
▪ §8 exemptions misused to deny information.
Way Forward: restore §8(1)(j)'s public-interest override · insulate IC appointments · fill vacancies · enact whistle-blower protection for activists.
Conclusion: Transparency is the oxygen of accountability — diluting RTI dims the citizen's lamp on power.
11 · LOCAL GOVERNMENT
T47 · Panchayati Raj — Mandate vs Reality (covers Q70, Q71, Q72, Q73)  [15m]

Intro (thinker): The 73rd Amendment (1992, Part IX) constitutionalised rural self-government as the “third tier” (Balwantrai Mehta → L.M. Singhvi), resting on the “3 Fs” — Functions, Functionaries, Funds.
Intro (concept): Gandhi's “Gram Swaraj” ideal now faces the challenge of Functionality — devolution vs delegation.
H1 — Constitutional mandate:
H2 — Ground reality & way forward:
▪ Framework: Arts 243–243O; 11th Schedule (29 subjects).
▪ Institutions: Gram Sabha (243A), SEC (243K), SFC (243I).
▪ Reservations: SC/ST + 1/3 women (50% in many States).
▪ Tribal areas: PESA 1996 empowers Gram Sabhas.
▪ Fiscal link: SFC + Finance-Commission local grants.
▪ Weak devolution: funds/functionaries withheld by States.
▪ Proxy representation: “sarpanch-pati”; parallel bodies bypass PRIs.
▪ Capacity deficit: delayed SFCs and elections.
▪ Tied grants limit local discretion.
▪ Fixes: activity-mapping, genuine fiscal devolution, 16th-FC conditional grants (audits/SFCs), e-Gram Swaraj.
Way Forward: devolve real functions and untied funds · constitute SFCs on time · build local capacity and audit · curb parallel bodies · empower Gram Sabhas (PESA).
Conclusion: Grassroots democracy deepens only when the third tier commands real functions, funds and functionaries — devolution, not delegation.
T48 · Urban Local Bodies — 74th Amendment (covers Q74)  [10m]
Intro (concept): The 74th Amendment (1992, Part IXA) envisaged empowered cities, yet ULBs remain “functionally and financially dependent” — even as cities become India's growth engines.
Intro (fact): GST subsumed buoyant local taxes (octroi/LBT), shrinking municipal own-revenue.
H1 — Causes of weak autonomy:
H2 — Strengthening measures:
▪ Reluctant devolution: States withhold 12th-Schedule (18 subjects) and taxes (243W/243X).
▪ Revenue erosion: GST subsumed octroi/LBT.
▪ Parastatals (development authorities) dilute ULB powers.
▪ Weak SFCs and under-exploited property tax.
▪ Low creditworthiness limits borrowing.
▪ Property-tax reform: GIS mapping, revised valuation, better collection.
▪ Municipal bonds: Ahmedabad/Indore/Pune models; pooled finance.
▪ Value capture: betterment levies, land-value capture.
▪ Empowered SFCs; activate MPC (243ZE)/DPC (243ZD).
▪ 16th-FC grants tied to audited accounts; AMRUT/Smart-City support.
Way Forward: reform property tax and issue municipal bonds · devolve 12th-Schedule functions/taxes · empower SFCs and metropolitan planning · build ULB capacity (HPEC 2011).
Conclusion: Cities drive growth but govern on borrowed money — fiscal empowerment is the unfinished 74th-Amendment agenda.
12 · OTHER CONSTITUTIONAL ISSUES — Schedules, Citizenship
T49 · Fifth & Sixth Schedules — Tribal Governance (covers Q75)  [10m]
Intro (concept): The Fifth (Art 244(1)) and Sixth (Art 244(2)) Schedules create asymmetric governance for tribal areas — a constitutional promise of self-rule and protection.
Intro (fact): The Xaxa Committee (2014) flagged the gap between constitutional design and tribal realities.
H1 — Fifth Schedule:
H2 — Sixth Schedule:
▪ Coverage: Scheduled Areas in 10 mainland States.
▪ Machinery: Governor's report + Tribes Advisory Council; power to modify laws.
▪ PESA 1996 extends Panchayats and empowers Gram Sabhas.
▪ Protection: land-alienation and resource safeguards.
▪ Advisory autonomy rather than legislative.
▪ Coverage: Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram.
▪ ADCs: Autonomous District Councils — law-making (land/forest/marriage), own courts, taxes.
▪ Fiscal base: Art 275 grants; e.g., Bodoland, Karbi Anglong.
▪ Friction: ADC-vs-State tensions; left-out tribes.
▪ Demand: extend the Sixth Schedule to Ladakh.
Way Forward: empower Gram Sabhas under PESA · make ADCs accountable and adequately funded · include excluded tribes · reconcile autonomy with State coordination.
Conclusion: True tribal self-governance needs devolution with accountability — protection without paternalism.
T50 · Citizenship — Framework, CAA & NRC (covers Q58, Q76)  [10m]
Intro (concept): The Constitution establishes single citizenship (Arts 5–11) as a unifier; the CAA 2019 and NRC debates have made acquisition-and-loss a live constitutional question.
Intro (case): Sanmita Assam (2024) upheld §6A (Assam Accord cut-off), while the CAA's Art-14 challenge remains pending.
H1 — Constitutional/statutory frame:
H2 — Recent flashpoints:
▪ Provisions: Arts 5–11; Citizenship Act 1955 (birth, descent, registration, naturalisation).
▪ Shift: from jus soli toward jus sanguinis (post-1987 amendments).
▪ OCI and modes of loss (renunciation, termination, deprivation).
▪ Single citizenship for national unity.
▪ Assam Accord 1985 — §6A cut-off (1971).
▪ CAA 2019: fast-tracks 6 non-Muslim minorities (pre-2014); rules 2024.
▪ Equality challenge: religion-based classification vs Art 14 (pending).
▪ NRC: Assam exercise (~19 lakh excluded) — documentation and due-process concerns.
▪ §6A upheld: Sanmita Assam (2024).
▪ Federal sensitivities in the Northeast.
Way Forward: balance humanitarian intent with secular equality (Art 14) · ensure due process in any NRC · respect Assam-Accord and federal concerns · avoid statelessness.
Conclusion: Citizenship defines the “we” of “We, the People” — its reform must honour both compassion and constitutional equality.
FLOW-MEMORY SPINE (recite in order to trigger all 50 cards)

Constitution: Basic Structure → Const. Morality → Art 368/131st → Delimitation → Women's Reservation → Emergency/44th → Article 370 →  Federalism: Governor/16th Ref → 16th FC → GST Council → Coordination(ISC) → Water disputes →  Sep. of Powers: JR India-UK → Parl. India-UK → PIL → Art 142 → Tribunals →  Parliament: Anti-defection → Committees → Sitting-days/Ordinance → Rajya Sabha → Privileges → LoP → ONOE →  Executive: §17A PC Act →  Judiciary: Collegium → Writs 32/226 →  Rights/DPSP: FR-DPSP → Art 21 → Secularism → ERP → UCC → DPDP → Free speech → Sedition → Preventive detention → Custodial → Death penalty →  Pressure groups: Civil society/FCRA →  Elections: SIR/Art 324 → Right to vote →  Bodies: ECI → CAG → Lokpal → NHRC → RTI →  Local: Panchayat → ULB →  Other: 5th/6th Schedule → Citizenship/CAA.
CONCEPT / DOCTRINE BANK (sprinkle for concept clarity)

Basic structure · constitutional morality · principled distance / sarva dharma sambhava · cooperative & competitive federalism · pooled sovereignty · golden triangle (Arts 14-19-21) · due process (Maneka) · harmonious construction · essential religious practices · rarest of rare · separation of powers / checks-and-balances · parliamentary sovereignty vs constitutional supremacy · judicial review / activism / overreach · asymmetric federalism · living constitution · fiscal federalism (vertical/horizontal equity) · grassroots democracy / 3 Fs · rule of law · proportionality.
UNIVERSAL LINKERS (drop into any intro/conclusion)

Ambedkar: “Constitution can be as good or bad as those who work it.” · Granville Austin: Constitution as a “seamless web” / DPSP the “conscience.” · Preamble anchor: Justice-Liberty-Equality-Fraternity + “We, the People.” · Cooperative federalism: Art 1 — a “Union of States.” · Constitutional morality as the fulcrum checking high functionaries · living document with “capabilities of enormous dynamism” (Supreme Court).
VERIFY-BEFORE-EXAM (these drift — confirm close to the date)
RESOLVED — 16th FC
Final report tabled Feb 2026 — see T9/T10 ammo box for the full numbers; quote at least the 41% + new GDP-contribution criterion.
RESOLVED — Census/delimitation clock
Census 2027 notified (caste enumeration; ref. 1 Mar 2027) — T4/T5 sequencing is now live.
STILL OPEN (re-verify near exam)
131st Bill revival status · ONOE JPC report (due Monsoon 2026) · SIR nationwide rollout orders · CAA Art-14 verdict · §17A larger Bench · Waqf Act interim orders.

16th-FC final tabled numbers · status of the 131st Bill/delimitation · ONOE JPC report outcome · SIR nationwide rollout & any fresh SC order · pending CAA Art-14 verdict · §17A PC Act larger-Bench ruling · latest Waqf Act interim orders · newest Art-21 expansions.

title: PubAd · Paper 1
lines: PUBLIC ADMINISTRATION PAPER I (ADMINISTRATIVE THEORY) — MEMORISATION CHEAT SHEET
(ENRICHED · FLOW) · Mains 2026
READ THE BOXES: ▪ Intro (2 options) ▪ H1 / H2 side-by-side (table) ▪ Way-Forward ▪ Conclusion. Bold = write-this keyword; italics = thinker/committee/case.

How to use: each theme = ready-made Intro → H1/H2 headings that mirror the demand → ▪ points, each a keyword lead-in : mechanism + Ex: thinker/committee/scheme/data → Way-Forward → Conclusion . Syllabus order; theory (Paper I) is bridged to an Indian example (Paper II).

Scoring spine (every point): keyword → mechanism → named thinker/committee/report/scheme/data. Weave one doctrine + one committee/report + one current initiative (12–18 mo) into every answer. ★ = cross-confirmed high-probability theme.
1 · INTRODUCTION
T1 · Nature, Scope & Identity of Public Administration (covers Q1, Q5) [10m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (concept): Public Administration has oscillated between a managerial view (POSDCORB, generic management) and an integral/political view (Waldo) — never settling whether its focus (principles of management) or its locus (the state) defines it.

Intro (thinker): Nicholas Henry's five paradigms (dichotomy → principles → political science → management science → PA as PA) show the discipline redrawing its focus-locus boundary at each crisis, each time enriched not enfeebled.
H1 — The focus–locus oscillation:
H2 — Why the 'identity crisis' enriches:
▪ Focus (what): administrative techniques, POSDCORB principles. Ex: Gulick's science-of-administration claim.
▪ Each crisis borrows a new identity: behavioural, public-choice, NPM, governance — cumulative not corrosive.
▪ Locus (where): the governmental setting, public interest. Ex: Waldo's 'Administrative State' (1948).
▪ Public-interest anchor: the constant that distinguishes it from business. Ex: Appleby — 'government is different'.
▪ Paradigm 1–2: Wilson–Goodnow dichotomy, then Willoughby's principles era. Ex: 'Papers on the Science of Administration', 1937.
▪ Interdisciplinary strength: draws on economics, sociology, law without dissolving.
▪ Paradigm 3–4: retreat into political science, then management science (1950s–70s).
▪ Indian relevance: discipline widened from regulation to development and now digital governance . Ex: NITI Aayog, DPI.
▪ Paradigm 5: PA as PA — an autonomous field with its own public ethos.
▪ Perennial tension productive: keeps PA responsive to a changing state.
Way Forward: anchor identity in public value not technique · treat crises as course-corrections · retain a normative core amid method-borrowing.
Conclusion: PA's want of a fixed identity is not weakness but plasticity — every crisis has left it a larger, more relevant discipline of the democratic state.
T2 · Public vs Private Administration / Distinctiveness (covers Q4) [20m]

Intro (thinker): Paul Appleby held government differs in kind, not merely in degree — its breadth of scope, public accountability and political character set it apart from any business ('Big Democracy', 1945).

Intro (comparative): Sir Josiah Stamp and Herbert Simon frame the debate: Stamp's four principles of difference against Simon's claim that the public– private line is one of degree, blurred by the modern regulatory-corporate state.
H1 — The case for distinctiveness:
H2 — The blurring & the counter-view:
▪ Political direction & sovereignty: administration serves a public will, wields coercive authority. Ex: Appleby — 'political character'.
▪ Generic-management school: Simon — administration is administration; POSDCORB is common ground.
▪ Public accountability: answerable to legislature, courts, citizens, media — a fishbowl. Ex: RTI 2005, CAG audit.
▪ Convergence: NPM imports market tools; PPP fuses sectors. Ex: viability- gap funding, outsourcing.
▪ Public interest, not profit: service over surplus; equity over efficiency.
▪ Corporatisation of public sector: Ex: Navratna autonomy, MoUs.
▪ Uniformity & rule of law: consistency, non-arbitrariness bind the public official. Ex: Art 14; natural justice.
▪ Regulatory state: private actors perform public functions. Ex: SEBI, TRAI, RERA.
▪ Scope & impact: vast, monopolistic, touching every citizen cradle-to- grave.
▪ Verdict: ends and accountability differ even where techniques converge.
Way Forward: borrow business techniques selectively · never surrender public accountability or equity · a hybrid that manages like business but answers like government.
Conclusion: Public administration may manage like business but must answer like government — its distinctiveness lies not in tools but in its public, political and accountable soul .
T3 · Woodrow Wilson & the Politics–Administration Dichotomy (covers Q2) [15m]

Intro (historical): Woodrow Wilson's 'The Study of Administration' (1887) founded the discipline, urging a science of administration insulated from the 'hurry and strife' of politics — 'administration lies outside the proper sphere of politics'.
Intro (quote): Wilson: 'It is getting to be harder to run a constitution than to frame one' — the plea that spawned a dichotomy his own presidency would belie.
H1 — Wilson's enduring contribution:
H2 — Critique & the dichotomy's collapse:
▪ Autonomy of the field: administration as a distinct study, borrowing European science. Ex: Goodnow's 'Politics and Administration', 1900.
▪ Policy–administration continuum: Appleby — the two are 'seamlessly' interwoven.
▪ Merit & efficiency: a professional service above spoils. Ex: Pendleton Act,
▪ Administrative discretion: officials make value choices. Ex: Simon's fact- value entanglement.
▪ New Public Administration: Waldo — administration is inescapably political and normative.
▪ Business-like methods: economy and efficiency in the run of government.
▪ Indian reality: bureaucracy shapes policy. Ex: NITI Aayog drafting; committed-bureaucracy debate.
▪ Comparative method: learn administration even from unlike regimes.
▪ Wilson's own career: a reformist President who legislated — belying strict separation.
▪ Instrumental neutrality: administrators execute the will of the state faithfully.
Way Forward: read the dichotomy as an analytic ideal , not a description · defend administrative neutrality while conceding policy influence · pair discretion with accountability.
Conclusion: Wilson's dichotomy is honoured in the breach , yet his deeper legacy — an autonomous, merit-based, efficient administration — remains the discipline's foundation stone.
T4 · New Public Administration / Minnowbrook (covers Q3) [20m]

Intro (event): The Minnowbrook Conference (1968) , convened by Dwight Waldo , birthed New Public Administration — a revolt of young scholars demanding relevance, values, social equity and change against behaviouralist value-neutrality.
Intro (concept): NPA added social equity as the 'third pillar' beside economy and efficiency — Frederickson's claim that a service fair to all is the test of good administration.
H1 — Contribution across three Minnowbrooks:
H2 — Limitations & Indian resonance:
▪ Minnowbrook I (1968): relevance, values, social equity, client-centred administration. Ex: H.G. Frederickson.
▪ Vague on method: rich in values, thin on operational tools.
▪ Anti-positivism: rejects fact-value dichotomy; embraces normative purpose.
▪ Equity–efficiency tension: may strain scarce-resource administration.
▪ Client-focus & participation: decentralisation, debureaucratisation for the disadvantaged.
▪ Anti-management drift: risks romanticising participation.
▪ Minnowbrook II (1988): consolidation amid the conservative turn.
▪ Indian echo: social justice built into the state. Ex: DPSP; reservation; Antyodaya.
▪ Minnowbrook III (2008): networks, globalisation, collaborative & digital governance.
▪ Living legacy: revived as social equity in inclusive governance. Ex: Aspirational Districts, DBT targeting.
Way Forward: institutionalise social-equity audits · marry NPA values with NPM's delivery discipline · localise SDGs as the new equity frontier.
Conclusion: From social equity to networked globalisation, NPA kept asking administration the normative question — for whom? — and that conscience remains its permanent gift.
T5 · NPM → New Public Governance (paradigm shift) (covers Q7) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (thinker): Hood (1991, 'A Public Management for All Seasons') and Osborne & Gaebler ('Reinventing Government', 1992) recast the state to 'steer, not row' , importing market-managerialism into a Weberian bureaucracy.

Intro (concept): NPM privileged the 3 Es (economy, efficiency, effectiveness) and the citizen-as-customer; New Public Governance ( Stephen Osborne , 2006) answers its fragmentation with collaboration and networks .
H1 — NPM: promise & its fragmentation:
H2 — NPG: restoring coherence:
▪ Let managers manage: agencification, output controls. Ex: UK Next Steps agencies, 1988.
▪ Plural & networked delivery: many interdependent actors co-produce services.
▪ Competition & contracting-out: market discipline. Ex: PPP, outsourcing in India.
▪ Relational, trust-based: contracts give way to collaboration.
▪ Performance measurement: SMART targets. Ex: Outcome Budget 2005; RFD.
▪ Whole-of-government / joined-up: coordination over disaggregation. Ex: DigiLocker–UMANG integration.
▪ Citizen as customer: service standards. Ex: Citizen's Charters 1997; Sevottam.
▪ Co-production with citizens: Ex: MyGov, participatory budgeting.
▪ Fragmentation cost: 'hollow state', silos, accountability gaps. Ex: 2nd ARC on fragmentation.
▪ Indian hybrid: 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance' + DBT/JAM adopt efficiency selectively.
Way Forward: pair outcome accountability with process accountability · adopt market tools context-sensitively · use DPI to make networked governance coherent, not chaotic.
Conclusion: The pendulum has swung from managerial NPM to collaborative public-value governance — India needs a hybrid marrying managerial rigour with constitutional coherence.
T5A · Public Choice Approach — NEW gap card (syllabus-named) [20m] ★ theory behind NPM

Intro (thinker): Public choice applies economics to politics — Buchanan & Tullock ("The Calculus of Consent", 1962) model politicians, voters and bureaucrats as self-interested utility-maximisers, dethroning the "benevolent state" assumption.

Intro (concept): Its bureaucratic core: Niskanen’s budget-maximising bureaucrat and Downs’ officials ("Inside Bureaucracy", 1967) — bureaus over-supply because budgets, not profits, measure success; the remedy prescribed is competition and choice, the intellectual fuel of NPM (T5).
H1 — Core claims & apparatus:
H2 — Critique & Indian resonance:
▪ Methodological individualism: collective outcomes explained from individual incentives, not "public interest".
▪ Vincent Ostrom: "The Intellectual Crisis in American Public Administration" (1973) — used public choice FOR democratic administration: polycentricity over monocentric bureaucracy.
▪ Rent-seeking (Tullock, Krueger): actors lobby to capture state-created rents — licences, quotas, tariffs.
▪ Critique: Peter Self ("Government by the Market") — cynical view corrodes public ethos; empirically bureaucrats also mission-driven (public-service motivation).
▪ Government failure as the mirror of market failure: vote-motive politics, fiscal illusion, log-rolling.
▪ Indian bridge: licence-permit raj as textbook rent-seeking → 1991 LPG; competition in delivery (DBT choice, portability — ONORC); Niskanen logic behind zero-based scheme reviews.
▪ Prescriptions: privatise, contract out, voucherise, constitutional limits on fiscal power.
▪ Balance line: public choice diagnoses incentives brilliantly but must be paired with public-value (Moore) and NPS (T6) correctives.
Way Forward: use public choice as diagnostic (incentive audit of any scheme) · design competition & exit options into delivery · guard the ethic of service against pure-market cynicism.

Conclusion: Public choice stripped administration of its innocence — by assuming the worst of incentives it taught reformers to design for them; NPM is its child, and public value its necessary conscience.
T6 · Good Governance, New Public Service & Networked Governance (covers Q6, Q8, Q9) [10m] ★ cross-confirmed
Intro (definition): Good governance (World Bank, 1992) fuses legitimacy of process with efficiency of outcome — captured in India as SMART (Simple, Moral, Accountable, Responsive, Transparent).
Intro (thinker): Denhardt & Denhardt's New Public Service (2000) restores the citizen, not the customer , to the centre — administrators 'serve rather than steer' a democratic community.
H1 — Good governance & public-value process:
H2 — NPS & governance as networks:
▪ Process legitimacy: participation, rule of law, consensus. Ex: UNDP 8 attributes.
▪ Citizen not customer: democratic citizenship over market exchange. Ex: Denhardt.
▪ Outcome efficiency: effectiveness, responsiveness, results.
▪ Serve, don't steer: public interest is the goal, not a by-product.
▪ Accountability + transparency: Ex: RTI 2005, social audit, CPGRAMS.
▪ Governance = government + networks + markets + communities ( Rhodes ).
▪ Equity & inclusiveness: leave no one behind. Ex: Antyodaya, Aspirational Districts.
▪ Enabling not rowing state: capacity to convene and regulate plural actors. Ex: ONDC, India Stack.
▪ Both/and, not either/or: a fair process legitimises an efficient outcome.
▪ Risk — hollowing of the state: networks can dilute accountability; steering capacity must remain.
Way Forward: build state convening capacity for networks · keep citizens as owners not consumers · anchor efficiency in democratic legitimacy.
Conclusion: Whether framed as good governance, New Public Service or networked governance, the lodestar is the empowered citizen — the state serving a democratic community, not merely a market.
FLOW — paradigm ladder (recite before any theory answer):
Wilson dichotomy 1887 → Taylor/Fayol science 1911-16 → Weber ideal-type → Hawthorne/HR 1930s
→ Simon behavioural 1947 → Development Admin 1960s → NPA/Minnowbrook 1968 → Public Choice 1960s-70s
→ NPM 1991 (steer-not-row) → Good Governance 1992 → NPS 2000 (serve-not-steer) → NPG networks 2006
→ Neo-Weberian State → Digital-era governance / DPI (India Stack) — each crisis enlarged the field (T1)
2 · ADMINISTRATIVE THOUGHT

＋ 2026 ammo (T5/T6): tie NPM’s intellectual root to the new Public Choice card (T5A); add "digital-era governance" (Dunleavy) — reintegration + needs-based holism + digitisation — as the post-NPM label for DPI-age India.
T7 · Taylor & Fayol: Scientific & Administrative Management (covers Q14, Q18) [15m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (thinker): F.W. Taylor's Scientific Management ('Principles', 1911) sought the 'one best way' through time-motion study — efficiency purchased, critics said, at the cost of the worker's humanity.
Intro (comparative): Henri Fayol ('General & Industrial Management', 1916) theorised management top-down — 14 principles and POCCC — where Taylor worked bottom-up from the shop floor.
H1 — Taylor: technique of work:
H2 — Fayol: principles of administration:
▪ One best way: scientific task analysis, time-motion study. Ex: pig-iron & shovelling experiments.
▪ 14 principles: unity of command, scalar chain, gangplank , esprit de corps.
▪ Functional foremanship & standardisation of tools.
▪ POCCC: Plan, Organise, Command, Coordinate, Control — a universal executive function.
▪ Differential piece-rate: incentive tied to output.
▪ General applicability: administration is teachable and universal.
▪ Mental revolution: cooperation of worker and manager.
▪ Why Fayol weathered better: principles are adaptable; Taylor's techniques were context-bound to the factory.
▪ Critique — dehumanisation: 'machine model' ignores the social worker; yet survives in every performance dashboard of the digital state.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: scalar hierarchy of the Secretariat; O&M as scientific- management heir.
Way Forward: retain measurement without mechanising the worker · treat principles as flexible guides not laws · fuse efficiency with human-relations insight.
Conclusion: Taylor gave administration its stopwatch and Fayol its compass — technique dates faster than principle, but a humane synthesis of both still governs the modern office.
T8 · Classical Principles / POSDCORB (Gulick–Urwick) as 'Proverbs' (covers Q10, Q11) [15m]

Intro (concept): Luther Gulick's POSDCORB (Planning, Organising, Staffing, Directing, Coordinating, Reporting, Budgeting) codified what a chief executive does — but tells us little of what administration is for .

Intro (thinker): L. Urwick sought in the management of men 'the precision of an engineer'; Herbert Simon's 'Proverbs of Administration' (1946) exposed the principles as mutually contradictory maxims .
H1 — The classical edifice:
H2 — Simon's 'proverbs' critique:
▪ POSDCORB: the executive's seven functions. Ex: Gulick–Urwick, 'Papers',
▪ Contradictory maxims: specialisation vs unity of command pull opposite ways.
▪ Span of control vs fewer levels: the two principles conflict.
▪ Span of control: limits on subordinates directly supervised.
▪ No empirical basis: proverbs, not scientific laws.
▪ Unity of command: one boss per employee.
▪ Ignores the human & informal: Barnard, Mayo correctives.
