CONCEPT / DOCTRINE BANK (sprinkle for concept clarity) Politics-administration dichotomy · focus vs locus · bounded rationality / satisficing · fact-value dichotomy · POSDCORB · span of control · unity of command · line-staff-auxiliary · ideal-type bureaucracy · goal displacement / trained incapacity / bureaupathology · Peter Principle · Parkinson's Law · iron law of oligarchy · zone of indifference · acceptance theory of authority · informal organisation · Hawthorne effect · law of the situation · integration / power-with · Theory X/Y/Z · hygiene-motivators · expectancy · linking-pin · immaturity-maturity · incrementalism · mixed-scanning · normative-optimum · multiple- streams / policy window · elite vs pluralist · authoritative allocation of values · public choice · NPM (3 Es) · New Public Service · New Public Governance · Good Governance (SMART) · Neo-Weberian State · prismatic-sala (formalism, overlapping, heterogeneity) · ecology of administration · development administration vs administration of development · embedded autonomy · state capacity · capability approach · committed-vs-neutral bureaucracy · generalist-vs-specialist · representative bureaucracy · proportionality · legitimate expectation · natural justice · ultra vires · delegated legislation · citizen charter · social audit · whole-of-government · matrix · regulatory state · DPI / India Stack · JAM · nudge · incremental budgeting · ZBB · outcome/gender budgeting · performance (VFM) audit.

UNIVERSAL LINKERS / QUOTES (droppable into any intro/conclusion) Wilson: 'It is getting to be harder to run a constitution than to frame one.' · Appleby: 'Government is different.' · Weber: bureaucracy as an 'iron cage' of rationality · Simon: the 'administrative man' satisfices where the 'economic man' maximises · Follett: the 'law of the situation' and power-with · Barnard: authority rests in the subordinate's zone of indifference · Sen: development as freedom · Wildavsky: the budget is incremental and political · Waldo: the 'Administrative State' · Good governance = SMART (Simple, Moral, Accountable, Responsive, Transparent) · 'steer, not row' (Osborne & Gaebler) · 'serve, not steer' (Denhardt) · 'Minimum Government, Maximum Governance.'

Cross-check every year, Article, committee name and datum before the exam.

Tabled Feb 2026: 41% devolution; GDP-contribution criterion 10% (new); local grants ₹9.47 L cr with entry conditions — quote in financial-administration answers.

Lateral-entry next tranche · Digital India Act draft · DPDP-RTI §44(3) litigation · CBC/iGOT coverage numbers — re-verify near exam.

title: PubAd · Paper 2
lines: PUBLIC ADMINISTRATION PAPER II (INDIAN ADMINISTRATION) — MEMORISATION CHEAT SHEET
(ENRICHED · FLOW) · Mains 2026
READ THE BOXES: ▪ Intro (2 options) ▪ H1 / H2 side-by-side (table) ▪ Way-Forward ▪ Conclusion. Bold = write-this keyword; italics = thinker/committee/case.

How to use: each theme = ready-made Intro → H1/H2 headings that mirror the demand → ▪ points, each a keyword lead-in : mechanism + Ex: institution/committee/scheme/data → Way-Forward → Conclusion . Syllabus order; every Indian-practice card (Paper II) is bridged to a theory/thinker (Paper I).

Scoring spine (every point): keyword → mechanism → named institution/committee/report/scheme/data. Weave one doctrine/thinker + one committee/report + one current initiative (12–18 mo) into every answer. ★ = cross-confirmed high-probability theme.
1 · EVOLUTION OF INDIAN ADMINISTRATION
T1 · Kautilya's Arthashastra & Ancient Statecraft (covers Q5, Q7) [10m]

Intro (thinker): Kautilya's Arthashastra is a manual of statecraft and administrative ethics — its Saptanga (seven-limbed state) anticipates the modern idea of an interdependent administrative state .
Intro (quote): Kautilya: 'In the happiness of his subjects lies the king's happiness' — an early doctrine of Yogakshema (welfare) and raja-dharma that prefigures the welfare state.
H1 — Saptanga & administrative ideas:
H2 — Ethics, relevance & theory-bridge:
▪ Saptanga (7 limbs): Swami, Amatya, Janapada, Durga, Kosha, Danda, Mitra — an organic theory of the state.
▪ Yogakshema: welfare and security of subjects as the king's duty. Ex: proto-welfare-state.
▪ Amatya & Adhyakshas: a graded, specialised bureaucracy of superintendents.
▪ Administrative ethics: raja-dharma , integrity, transparency of accounts.
▪ Danda-niti: the science of governance and punishment; rule of law.
▪ Mandala theory: realist statecraft, foreign-policy circles.
▪ Espionage & accountability: checks on officials, anti-corruption vigilance.
▪ Theory-bridge: organic Saptanga parallels systems theory ; welfare parallels Sen's capability idea.
▪ Fiscal administration: Kosha (treasury) as the sinew of the state.
▪ Contemporary echo: Ex: probity codes, vigilance (CVC), citizen-welfare governance.
Way Forward: revive Kautilyan probity and welfare-first ethic · anchor modern anti-corruption in raja-dharma · read the state as an interdependent organism.
Conclusion: The Arthashastra shows Indian administrative thought is millennia old — its Saptanga state and Yogakshema ethic still speak to a welfare democracy's quest for probity and public good.
T2 · Mughal Administration (covers Q9, Q11) [15m]
Intro (institution): The Mansabdari system ( Akbar ) organised the nobility into a graded zat–sawar hierarchy, fusing civil rank and military obligation under a centralised, paper-based bureaucracy.
Intro (comparative): Todar Mal's zabti/dahsala land-revenue settlement systematised assessment — a continuity the British would inherit and formalise into their own revenue administration.
H1 — Structure of Mughal administration:
H2 — Continuities & departures:
▪ Mansabdari: zat (personal rank) + sawar (cavalry) — a numerically graded service.
▪ Continuity: revenue-centric field administration, district as fulcrum.
▪ Jagirdari: revenue assignments in lieu of salary; transferable, non- hereditary.
▪ Departure: modern service is merit-recruited, rule-bound, non-personal.
▪ Territorial tiers: Subah → Sarkar → Pargana, with Subedar, Diwan, Faujdar.
▪ Persian idiom: vocabulary and record-keeping carried into British era.
▪ Land revenue: Todar Mal's zabti/dahsala; measurement and average yields.
▪ Weakness: personalised loyalty, no permanent civil service. (→ contrast Weber's legal-rational).
▪ Central departments: Diwan (revenue), Mir Bakshi (military), Sadr (religious/charity).
▪ Theory-bridge: patrimonial-prebendal type ( Weber ) vs modern legal- rational bureaucracy.
Way Forward: read continuity in revenue-and-district administration · note the shift from patrimonial to legal-rational service · value systematised assessment over personalised tenure.
Conclusion: Mughal administration bequeathed India a centralised revenue-and-district template , but its patrimonial, personalised core is precisely what the modern merit bureaucracy replaced.
T3 · British Legacy — ICS, Cornwallis & the Steel Frame (covers Q1, Q10, Q2) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (historical): Cornwallis founded a civil service that was honest but alien — the covenanted service, purged of private trade and Indianisation alike, laid the base of the 'steel frame' designed for control rather than development .

Intro (concept): Indian administration carries the layered imprint of its Mughal-Persian and British inheritances — a district-revenue continuity overlaid with an impersonal, law-and-order colonial superstructure.
H1 — Building the colonial service:
H2 — Control-bias & its legacy:
▪ Cornwallis reforms (1793): covenanted service, higher pay, no private trade — 'honest but alien'.
▪ Regulation over development: revenue and order, not welfare, were the goals.
▪ Europeanisation: Indians barred from higher posts; a service of rulers, not representatives.
▪ Generalist Collector as omnibus authority — enduring model.
▪ Macaulay & open competition (1853/1854): merit recruitment. (→ Northcote–Trevelyan).
▪ Rule-bound, hierarchical, impersonal — Weberian in form.
▪ ICS 'steel frame': elite, generalist, district-centred. Ex: Lloyd George ; Patel later reclaimed it.
▪ Continuity into free India: Ex: AIS, district administration, secretariat.
▪ Separation of powers in revenue and judiciary partially attempted.
▪ Theory-bridge: a Weberian legal-rational frame put to developmental uses post-1947.
Way Forward: repurpose the steel frame from control to development · democratise and specialise the generalist legacy · retain merit and integrity, shed colonial distance.
Conclusion: The British left a service honest, impersonal and control-oriented — independent India's project has been to keep its steel while reforging it for development and democracy .
T4 · Colonial Land-Revenue Settlements (covers Q8) [15m]

Intro (concept): The colonial land-revenue settlements left a lasting imprint on Indian field administration — the Permanent, Ryotwari and Mahalwari systems shaped landholding, revenue and rural power for a century.
Intro (comparative): Each settlement located revenue liability differently — with the zamindar, the ryot, or the village community — embedding distinct patterns of agrarian administration.
H1 — The three settlements:
H2 — Lasting administrative imprint:
▪ Permanent Settlement ( Cornwallis , 1793): Bengal; zamindar as proprietor; fixed revenue.
▪ Agrarian structure: absentee landlordism (Bengal) vs peasant proprietorship (Ryotwari).
▪ Ryotwari ( Munro/Read ): Madras & Bombay; direct state–cultivator settlement.
▪ Revenue-centric district administration persists.
▪ Mahalwari ( Holt Mackenzie , 1822): NW Provinces/Punjab; village/mahal as revenue unit.
▪ Land records & tenure disputes legacy. Ex: DILRMP/SVAMITVA digitisation today.
▪ Revenue liability located with zamindar / ryot / community respectively.
▪ Post-independence land reforms responded to these patterns.
▪ Field machinery: Collector–revenue hierarchy institutionalised.
▪ Theory-bridge: Riggs' ecology — administration shaped by agrarian society.
Way Forward: digitise and update the colonial revenue record base · complete land reforms unfinished since these settlements · reorient revenue field-staff toward service.

Conclusion: The Permanent, Ryotwari and Mahalwari settlements wrote the grammar of Indian field administration — its revenue spine and land-record burdens are colonial inheritances still being reformed.
T5 · Indianisation & Constitutional Evolution (covers Q3, Q4, Q6) [15m]

Intro (historical): Indianisation of the public services was conceded as a political necessity, never an administrative conviction — the Islington and Lee Commissions rationed it grudgingly against nationalist pressure.

Intro (event): Dyarchy under the Montagu–Chelmsford reforms (1919) was an unworkable halfway house , yet the training ground of Indian self- government — one stage in the march from Charter Acts to the GoI Act, 1935.
H1 — Indianisation of the services:
H2 — Constitutional evolution to 1935:
▪ Aitchison (1886): graded Indianisation of services.
▪ Charter Acts (1793–1853): Law Member ( Macaulay ), open competition (1853).
▪ Islington Commission (1912): limited reservation of higher posts for Indians.
▪ Morley–Minto (1909) and Montagu–Chelmsford (1919): dyarchy in provinces.
▪ Lee Commission (1924): 50:50 direct recruitment; recommended a Public Service Commission (est. 1926).
▪ GoI Act 1935: provincial autonomy, federal scheme, Public Service Commissions.
▪ Political necessity: conceded under nationalist and wartime pressure.
▪ Continuities into the Constitution: Ex: services, federal-fiscal design, emergency provisions.
▪ Legacy: the constitutional guarantee of an independent UPSC (Art 315).
▪ Theory-bridge: incrementalism — self-government built stage by stage.
Way Forward: read Indianisation as won, not gifted · trace constitutional continuities honestly · value the independent PSC as the durable institutional gain.

Conclusion: From grudging Indianisation to the flawed schooling of dyarchy, the colonial constitutional journey seeded the institutions — merit services, federalism, PSCs — that independent India inherited and transformed.
2 · PHILOSOPHICAL & CONSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK
T6 · Value Premises & Constitutionalism (covers Q12, Q13, Q15, Q17) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (concept): Independent India placed the goals of a welfare state upon the shoulders of a colonial steel frame — a value-transformation from control to service that the inherited machinery was never designed for.

Intro (quote): The Preamble is the 'identity card' of the Constitution and the moral compass of administration; the Directive Principles are its conscience- keeper, and constitutionalism its promise of limited government.
H1 — The value premises:
H2 — From colonial frame to democratic ethos:
▪ Welfare-state goals on a control-era frame — the founding paradox.
▪ Steel frame repurposed: regulation → development → rights-based delivery.
▪ Preamble values: justice, liberty, equality, fraternity as administrative lodestars.
▪ Bureaucracy–democracy–development triad in tension.
▪ DPSP (Part IV, Art 37): conscience-keeper; non-justiciable yet 'fundamental in governance'.
▪ Fundamental Rights + DPSP as the twin engines (harmony doctrine).
▪ Constitutionalism = limited government: power bound by law and rights.
▪ Theory-bridge: Appleby's 'government is different'; Waldo's value-laden administration.
▪ Rule of law & accountability as the frame's moral core.
▪ Contemporary test: Ex: rights-based laws — RTI, RTE, MGNREGA, NFSA.
Way Forward: align administrative culture with constitutional values · treat DPSP as a delivery mandate · keep power limited and accountable.

Conclusion: The Indian administrative story is the effort to infuse a colonial steel frame with a democratic, welfare soul — constitutionalism is the tether that keeps power the servant of the Constitution.
T7 · Quasi-Federalism & Administrative Structure (covers Q14) [15m]
Intro (thinker): India is, in K.C. Wheare's phrase, 'quasi-federal' — a federation designed with a strong unitary bias , a single Constitution, integrated services and Union primacy in crisis.
Intro (concept): The quasi-federal design shapes administration: a common civil service, a single judiciary and Union override , balanced against States' operational autonomy.
H1 — Features of the unitary bias:
H2 — Administrative implications:
▪ Single Constitution & citizenship; integrated judiciary.
▪ Cooperative federalism in normal times. Ex: GST Council, ISC, NITI Aayog.
▪ All-India Services (Art 312): shared cadres bind Centre and States.
▪ Coordination machinery for a large diverse polity.
▪ Union override: Ex: Arts 200/201, 256/257, 356.
▪ Tensions: cadre-deputation, Governor's role, central schemes' rigidity.
▪ Emergency provisions convert federal to unitary.
▪ Competitive federalism: Ex: SDG/DoingBusiness rankings.
▪ Divisible-pool & FC transfers structure fiscal dependence.
▪ Theory-bridge: Riggs' ecology — federal form fitted to a plural society.
Way Forward: deepen cooperative and competitive federalism · use coordination fora over unilateralism · reserve unitary levers for genuine crises.
Conclusion: India's quasi-federalism is unitary in bias but federal in spirit — good administration works its cooperative institutions rather than leaning on its centralising levers.
T8 · Political Culture & Bureaucracy–Democracy (covers Q16, Q18, Q19) [15m]

Intro (thinker): The Indian bureaucracy functions within a political culture that prizes hierarchy, patronage and personalised authority ( Almond & Verba's culture framework), shaping administrative behaviour and performance.
Intro (concept): Bureaucracy and democracy are uneasy but indispensable partners — the permanent expert executive and the transient popular will held in productive tension.
H1 — Political culture & administrative behaviour:
H2 — Bureaucracy–democracy tension:
▪ Hierarchy & deference: upward-looking, risk-averse decision-making.
▪ Permanence vs accountability: expert continuity vs popular control.
▪ Patron–client networks and personalised authority.
▪ Political neutrality vs responsiveness balance.
▪ Formalism: gap between rule and practice. (→ Riggs' prismatic society).
▪ Committed-vs-neutral debate (Indira era).
▪ Mixed civic culture ( Almond & Verba ): participant + subject + parochial.
▪ Democratic control instruments: Ex: RTI, PIL, legislative committees.
▪ Impact: delay, deference, but also stability and continuity.
▪ Theory-bridge: Weber (rational bureaucracy) meets democratic accountability.
Way Forward: cultivate a participative, outcome-oriented administrative culture · balance neutrality with democratic responsiveness · reduce formalism and deference.
Conclusion: Bureaucracy and democracy are partners under strain — India's task is to keep the expert steel responsive to the democratic will without dissolving either into the other.
3 · PUBLIC SECTOR UNDERTAKINGS
T9 · PSUs — Forms, Autonomy & Accountability (covers Q20, Q24) [10m]
Intro (concept): The Memorandum of Understanding promised autonomy without surrendering accountability — the Navratna framework delegated commercial freedom while retaining public answerability.
Intro (concept): The autonomy–accountability–control trilemma is the perennial governance problem of the PSU — freedom to compete against answerability for public funds.
H1 — Forms & the autonomy framework:
H2 — Accountability & control problems:
▪ Forms: departmental undertaking, statutory corporation, government company.
▪ Multiple principals: ministry, Parliament, CAG, COPU.
▪ MoU system ( Arjun Sengupta , 1986): performance contract with targets.
▪ Political & bureaucratic interference erodes commercial autonomy.
▪ Navratna (1997), Maharatna, Miniratna: graded financial autonomy.
▪ CAG audit + PUC scrutiny vs managerial freedom.
▪ Board governance and professional management.
▪ Performance measurement via MoU/KPIs.
▪ Theory-bridge: the corporation was to marry business autonomy with public accountability.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: COPU reports; disinvestment/DIPAM oversight.
Way Forward: insulate boards from micromanagement · deepen MoU-based performance accountability · professionalise boards and appointments.
Conclusion: The PSU forever chases the balance of freedom to compete and duty to answer — the MoU-Navratna design remains the best template for autonomy that stays publicly accountable.
T10 · Disinvestment, Privatisation & the Changing Role of PSUs (covers Q21, Q22, Q23, Q25) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (concept): From the commanding heights of the economy to a strategic minimum , the very rationale of the public sector has been reversed — LPG recast the PSU from nation-builder to portfolio asset.
Intro (event): Strategic disinvestment and a 'bare minimum' presence in strategic sectors (2021 PSE Policy) redefine the public sector; asset monetisation is disinvestment by another name .
H1 — The reversal of rationale:
H2 — Monetisation, privatisation & impact:
▪ Nehruvian commanding heights: PSUs as engines of self-reliant industrialisation.
▪ National Monetisation Pipeline (2021): lease brownfield assets for capital.
▪ LPG (1991): dereservation, delicensing, disinvestment begin.
▪ Asset monetisation ≈ disinvestment of usage rights, not ownership.
▪ New PSE Policy (2021): 'bare minimum' PSUs in 4 strategic sectors; privatise the rest.
▪ DIPAM manages disinvestment and asset value.
▪ Strategic disinvestment: Ex: Air India (2021); LIC IPO (2022).
▪ Impact: efficiency and fiscal gains vs equity, employment, strategic concerns.
▪ Theory-bridge: NPM/'steer-not-row' — state exits production.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: minimum-government-maximum-governance philosophy.
Way Forward: privatise transparently with competition and worker safeguards · retain genuine strategic and public-good sectors · use monetisation for capital formation, not deficit-financing.
Conclusion: The public sector has travelled from commanding heights to strategic minimum — the reform test is to unlock efficiency and capital without surrendering equity and strategic autonomy.
4 · UNION GOVERNMENT & ADMINISTRATION
T11 · Cabinet System — Secretariat, Committees & Collective Responsibility (covers Q28, Q30, Q35) [15m]

Intro (institution): The Cabinet Secretariat is the apex instrument of administrative coordination — servicing the Cabinet, its committees and inter- ministerial consensus under the Cabinet Secretary, the seniormost civil servant.

Intro (concept): Cabinet Committees quietly redistribute the collective authority of the Cabinet , even as the character of cabinet government and collective responsibility shifts toward prime-ministerial dominance.
H1 — Cabinet Secretariat & committees:
H2 — Collective responsibility & its drift:
▪ Cabinet Secretariat: coordination, agenda, minutes, follow-up. Ex: crisis- management, monitoring.
▪ Collective responsibility (Art 75(3)): Council answerable as a body to Lok Sabha.
▪ Cabinet Secretary: head of civil services, chief coordinator, ex-officio to many bodies.
▪ Prime-ministerial government: PM as 'first among unequals'.
▪ Cabinet Committees: CCS, CCEA, CCPA — decentralise decision. Ex: Rules of Business under Art 77.
▪ Coalition vs single-party dynamics reshape collegiality.
▪ Inter-ministerial coordination and dispute resolution.
▪ PMO ascendancy concentrates coordination (→ next card).
▪ Theory-bridge: coordination as Gulick's POSDCORB 'Co'.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: shift from cabinet to core-group/committee decision- making.
Way Forward: strengthen the Cabinet Secretariat's coordinating neutrality · use committees for collegial decision, not bypass · preserve genuine collective responsibility.

Conclusion: The Cabinet Secretariat remains the keystone of Union coordination , but as authority migrates to the PMO and committees, the collegiality of cabinet government needs conscious protection.
T12 · PMO & Central Secretariat (covers Q29, Q32) [20m]

Intro (institution): The Prime Minister's Office has emerged as the nerve-centre of Union administration — an extra-constitutional but pivotal instrument of prime-ministerial coordination and monitoring.

Intro (concept): The Central Secretariat , conceived as the 'brain of the Union Government' , is faulted for a generalist–specialist imbalance — the case for domain expertise against the generalist tradition.
H1 — The rise of the PMO:
H2 — Central Secretariat & the expertise debate:
▪ Nerve-centre: policy coordination, monitoring, crisis-management. Ex: PRAGATI platform.
▪ Brain of government: policy formulation, inter-ministerial coordination.
▪ Principal Secretary & advisers to the PM.
▪ Generalist tradition: tenure system, rotation of IAS. (→ Fulton critique of the amateur).
▪ Concentration of coordination across ministries.
▪ Specialist deficit: thin domain expertise for complex policy.
▪ Concerns: over-centralisation, bypassing of ministries.
▪ Reforms: Ex: lateral entry, Mission Karmayogi competencies, specialist wings.
▪ Theory-bridge: staff agency to the chief executive ( Pfiffner's keystone).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: 2nd ARC & Surinder Nath on domain competence.
Way Forward: institutionalise domain expertise via lateral entry and specialisation · keep the PMO a coordinator, not a parallel government · blend generalist breadth with specialist depth.
Conclusion: The PMO has become the command node and the Central Secretariat the brain — both need reform: one toward restraint, the other toward the domain expertise a modern state demands.
T13 · Ministries, Departments & Field Organisations (covers Q27, Q31) [20m]

Intro (concept): The Ministry is a political creation; the Department is its administrative reality — the Allocation of Business and Transaction of Business Rules (Art 77) map who does what and how business is disposed.
Intro (concept): Below them, attached, subordinate and field organisations translate policy into execution — the line of implementation that turns secretariat decisions into services.
H1 — Ministries & departments:
H2 — Attached, subordinate & field offices:
▪ Ministry (political) vs Department (administrative) distinction.
▪ Attached offices: technical advice + execution. Ex: DGHS, DGFT.
▪ Allocation of Business Rules: distribute subjects among ministries.
▪ Subordinate offices: field-level implementation under attached/ministry.
▪ Transaction of Business Rules: how decisions are taken and channelled.
▪ Field organisations: the delivery frontline.
▪ Secretariat vs executive functions demarcated.
▪ HQ–field coordination and delegation issues. (→ Fesler area-function).
▪ Theory-bridge: Gulick's departmentalisation by purpose/process/persons/place.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: policy-implementation gap; last-mile delivery reform.
Way Forward: clarify secretariat–executive demarcation · delegate operational authority to field offices · align attached/subordinate structures with delivery outcomes.
Conclusion: The Union executive runs on a ladder from political ministry to field office — coherent administration depends on clear business rules at the top and empowered execution at the bottom.
T14 · Parliamentary Oversight & Executive–Legislature–Judiciary (covers Q26, Q33, Q34) [20m]
Intro (concept): The real work of parliamentary scrutiny has migrated from the floor of the House to the committee room — even as parliamentary oversight of the executive has steadily weakened .
Intro (concept): Meanwhile judicial review has expanded into the judicial governance of administration — the court supervising administrative action ever more intrusively.
H1 — Parliamentary oversight & committees:
H2 — Executive–legislature–judiciary balance:
▪ DRSCs (1993): 24 departmentally-related standing committees; detailed, non-partisan scrutiny.
▪ Executive dominance via majority and anti-defection.
▪ Financial committees: PAC, Estimates, PUC.
▪ Judicial review expansion: PIL, proportionality, basic structure.
▪ Floor tools: questions, debates, motions, budget.
▪ Judicial governance risk: court prescribing administrative content.
▪ Erosion: declining sittings, guillotine, fewer bills referred.
▪ Separation of powers & checks-and-balances under strain.
▪ Theory-bridge: legislative control ( accountability & control ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: bills passed without committee reference; judicial monitoring of agencies.
Way Forward: refer bills to committees by default and strengthen post-legislative scrutiny · curb the guillotine · balance judicial review with self-restraint.

Conclusion: As legislative oversight recedes and judicial review advances, India's inter-institutional balance tilts — reviving committee scrutiny is the surest way to restore the legislature's check on the executive.
T14A · Census 2027 — Administrative Machinery of the Count — NEW mini-card (live 2026 topic) [10m]

Intro (event): Census 2027 — India’s first digital census and first caste enumeration since 1931 — is the largest peacetime administrative mobilisation: ~30 lakh enumerators, mobile-app capture, a self-enumeration portal, reference dates 1 Oct 2026 (snow-bound) / 1 Mar 2027.
H1 — Administrative design:
H2 — Challenges & stakes:
▪ Machinery: RGI & Census Commissioner (MHA) → State/District Census Officers → charge officers → enumerators (teachers as ex-officio) — classic HQ-field cascade (Fesler, T-cards).
▪ Caste-column design: "open-column" method field-tested; classification chaos risk (SECC-2011 lesson: 46 lakh caste names); privacy under Census Act 1948 (confidential, non-justiciable data).
▪ Digital stack: app-based capture, geo-tagged houselisting, management portal — from paper schedules to real-time MIS.
▪ Sequencing stakes: census → delimitation → women’s reservation (106th CAA) — the most consequential administrative timetable of the decade; North-South federal anxiety rides on it.
▪ Two phases: houselisting (2026) → population enumeration (Feb–Mar 2027).
▪ Capacity & trust: enumerator training at scale, digital-divide fallback, misinformation management; delayed-census cost — 2011-base welfare exclusions (NFSA).
Way Forward: transparent caste-classification protocol · statutory data-protection assurance · publish quickly (avoid SECC limbo) · update welfare bases (NFSA, delimitation inputs) on release.
Conclusion: The census is administration counting itself into legitimacy — done digitally and honestly, 2027 will re-base representation, welfare and social justice for a generation.
5 · PLANS AND PRIORITIES
T15 · Planning Commission → NITI Aayog & Cooperative-Competitive Federalism (covers Q36, Q40) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (event): The shift from the Planning Commission to NITI Aayog (2015) replaced directive planning with cooperative and competitive federalism — a think-tank and convener in place of a fund-allocating commission.
Intro (concept): NITI Aayog governs the States by ranking them rather than by directing them — competitive federalism through indices in place of Plan transfers.
H1 — From directive planning to convening:
H2 — Competitive federalism & its limits:
▪ Planning Commission (1950): centralised Five-Year Plans, fund allocation.
▪ Ranking as governance: Ex: SDG India Index, Aspirational Districts, Health/Education indices.
▪ NITI Aayog (2015): think-tank, no fund-allocation power; cooperative federalism.
▪ Competitive federalism spurs reform through comparison.
▪ Governing Council of CMs — States as partners.
▪ Limits: no financial teeth; advisory only.
▪ Bottom-up, sectoral, and outcome focus over indicative targets.
▪ Data-quality & comparability concerns.
▪ Theory-bridge: networked/enabling governance over command planning.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: transfer of resource role to Finance Ministry/FC.
Way Forward: give NITI convening-plus-monitoring strength without reviving command planning · improve index data quality · pair competition with capacity-support for laggards.
Conclusion: NITI Aayog replaced the commanding planner with a cooperative convener — governing by ranking and persuasion, its influence rests on ideas and indices rather than the purse.
T16 · Aspirational Districts/Blocks & Programme Monitoring (covers Q37, Q38, Q41) [15m]
Intro (concept): A plan is only as good as its last-mile evaluation — India's machinery for implementation, monitoring and outcome evaluation is the real test of planning.

Intro (event): The Aspirational Districts (2018) and Aspirational Blocks (2023) Programmes carry the logic of competitive-and-cooperative federalism below the district , driven by real-time data and rankings.
H1 — Aspirational programmes as delivery model:
H2 — Monitoring & outcome evaluation:
▪ Aspirational Districts (2018): 112 districts; convergence, competition, collaboration.
▪ DMEO (NITI Aayog): evaluation of schemes.
▪ Aspirational Blocks (2023): deepens the model below the district.
▪ Outcome Budget & output-outcome frameworks.
▪ KPIs & Champions of Change dashboard: real-time ranking.
▪ Digital dashboards: Ex: PRAGATI, DBT, PM Gati Shakti.
▪ 3 Cs: Convergence of schemes, Collaboration of centre-state-district, Competition.
▪ Third-party & concurrent evaluation for credibility.
▪ Theory-bridge: development administration + evidence-based governance ( Simon ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: attribution problem; weak feedback into design.
Way Forward: institutionalise independent outcome evaluation · scale the aspirational model with genuine flexibility · close the monitoring-to-redesign loop.
Conclusion: The Aspirational model shows planning's future is granular, data-driven and competitive — but its promise is realised only when last-mile monitoring feeds honestly back into design.
T17 · State & Decentralised Planning (covers Q39, Q42) [15m]
Intro (concept): With the Planning Commission gone, State Planning Boards have lost their interlocutor without finding a new role — the machinery of planning at the State level adrift after 2015.
Intro (concept): Decentralised planning was to be the cornerstone of participatory development, yet District Planning Committees remain moribund — the constitutional vision unfulfilled.
H1 — State-level planning machinery:
H2 — Decentralised & participatory planning:
▪ State Planning Boards weakened post-Planning-Commission.
▪ DPCs (Art 243ZD) to consolidate panchayat + municipal plans — largely moribund.
▪ Loss of interlocutor: no central counterpart for plan dialogue.
▪ MPCs (Art 243ZE) for metropolitan planning.
▪ State Finance Commissions for local-body devolution (Art 243-I/Y).
▪ People's-plan campaigns: Ex: Kerala's Kudumbashree/People's Plan model.
▪ Scheme-driven state planning displaces comprehensive plans.
▪ Constraints: capacity, funds, functionary devolution gaps.
▪ Theory-bridge: bottom-up planning ( subsidiarity ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: Gram Panchayat Development Plans (GPDP).
Way Forward: reinvent State Planning Boards as outcome-and-convergence bodies · operationalise DPCs and MPCs · empower participatory GPDP planning with funds and staff.
Conclusion: Planning has decentralised in design but not in practice — reviving State Boards and the dormant DPCs is essential to make participatory, bottom-up planning real.
6 · STATE GOVERNMENT & ADMINISTRATION
T18 · Union–State Relations & Coordination Machinery (covers Q43, Q45) [20m]

Intro (concept): Fiscal transfers have received far more attention than the institutional machinery of Centre–State coordination — the Inter-State Council and Zonal Councils under-used in Indian cooperative federalism.
Intro (event): Simultaneous elections ('One Nation, One Election') promise administrative efficiency but test the federal architecture — a coordination question with deep federal implications.
H1 — Coordination machinery:
H2 — ONOE & federal implications:
▪ Inter-State Council (Art 263, 1990): recommended by Sarkaria ; under- convened.
▪ Efficiency claim: reduced cost, model-code disruption, governance continuity.
▪ Zonal Councils (SRA, 1956): advisory regional coordination.
▪ Federal concerns: synchronisation, premature dissolution, Art 356 misuse risk.
▪ GST Council (Art 279A): the most effective cooperative body. Ex: consensus voting.
▪ Administrative demands: EC capacity, EVMs, security.
▪ NITI Aayog Governing Council as a coordination forum.
▪ Constitutional amendments and ratification needed.
▪ Theory-bridge: Follett's integration; cooperative federalism.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: Ram Nath Kovind Committee (2024) recommendations.
Way Forward: convene the ISC regularly as the apex coordination forum · strengthen Zonal Councils · weigh ONOE's efficiency against federal and administrative costs.
Conclusion: Indian federalism has invested in fiscal transfers over coordination institutions — reviving the Inter-State Council matters more than any single reform like simultaneous elections.
T19 · Fiscal Federalism & the 16th Finance Commission (covers Q44, Q48, Q49) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (event): The Sixteenth Finance Commission ( Arvind Panagariya ) signals a possible shift from entitlement-based transfers to compliance-driven fiscal federalism — devolution criteria, local-body grants and fiscal discipline under review for 2026–31.
Intro (concept): The GST and successive Finance Commissions are recasting Union–State financial relations — pooling sovereignty in GST while the divisible pool and cesses remain contested.
H1 — The 16th FC and devolution:

▪ Cess & surcharge shrink the divisible pool — States' grievance.

▪ Vertical fiscal imbalance: States spend more, Centre taxes more.
▪ Local-body grants with entry-conditions & performance grants.
▪ Conditional Centrally-Sponsored Schemes constrain State autonomy.
▪ Fiscal-discipline incentives and debt-path guidance.
▪ Theory-bridge: cooperative vs coercive federalism ( Riker ).
Way Forward: widen the divisible pool by curbing cesses · balance conditionality with State fiscal autonomy · strengthen State Finance Commissions for local devolution.
Conclusion: Fiscal federalism is India's most contested frontier — the 16th FC and GST must balance Union discipline with State autonomy , or cooperative federalism hardens into a coercive one.

＋ VERIFIED 16th-FC FINALS (T19): devolution retained 41%; criteria — income distance 42.5%, population 17.5%, GDP contribution 10% (NEW), demographic performance 10%, area 10%, forest 10%; local grants ₹9.47 lakh cr (RLB 4.4 + ULB 3.6 + disaster 1.56, with entry conditions); Centre FD → 3.5% GDP by 2030-31, States 3% GSDP; combined debt 77.3%→73.1%. Frame: compliance-driven fiscal federalism — conditionality vs autonomy is the 20-marker.

Conclusion: India's rural safety net rests on rights-based employment and SHG-led livelihoods — their promise is real, but convergence and last-mile delivery are the persistent gaps to close.

＋ CRITICAL 2026 UPDATE (T37): MGNREGA replaced by VB-G RAM G Act, 2025 (in force 1 Jul 2026) — 125-day guarantee; four priority areas (water security, rural infra, livelihood infra, extreme-weather mitigation); ≤60-day agricultural pause; 60:40 Centre-State funding (90:10 NE); ~₹1.51 lakh cr. PubAd angles: Weidner goal-change (guarantee → mission-mode); subsidiarity vs centralised allocation; transition management (job-card migration, social-audit continuity); Kerala/TN federal objections + UN experts’ rights-dilution warning.
12 · URBAN LOCAL GOVERNMENT
T38 · 74th Amendment, Municipal Governance & the Commissioner–Council (covers Q100, Q103) [15m]

Intro (concept): In Indian city government, the executive belongs to the State and the deliberative to the city — the Municipal Commissioner–elected council relationship the crux of accountable urban management.
Intro (concept): Municipal finance is the Achilles' heel of urban governance — the 74th Amendment's promise unfulfilled without fiscal empowerment.
H1 — 74th Amendment & the Commissioner–council divide:
H2 — Missions & municipal capacity:
▪ 74th Amendment (1992): constitutional status; 12th Schedule (18 functions); Wards Committees.
▪ AMRUT & Smart Cities Mission (2015): infrastructure and area-based development.
▪ Municipal Commissioner (executive): State-appointed, real administrative power.
▪ Special Purpose Vehicles in Smart Cities bypass elected bodies — accountability concern.
▪ Elected council & Mayor (deliberative): weak, often ceremonial Mayor.
▪ Capacity deficit: staff, planning, technical skills.
▪ Accountability gap: executive answers to State, not city.
▪ Parastatals fragment urban governance.
▪ Theory-bridge: staff–line/political–permanent executive tension.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: directly-elected empowered Mayor debate.
Way Forward: empower the elected council and Mayor with executive accountability · integrate parastatals under municipal government · build municipal technical capacity.
Conclusion: Indian cities are run by State-appointed executives and represented by weak councils — accountable city management needs an empowered elected leadership over the municipal executive.

＋ VERIFIED (T38/T39): 16th FC ULB grants ₹3.6 lakh cr — entry conditions (online audited accounts, property-tax floor growth) + urbanisation premium; strongest-ever FC nudge on own-source revenue — quote against the "<1% of GDP municipal revenue" datum.
T39 · Municipal Finance & Own-Source Revenue (covers Q101) [20m]
Intro (concept): Local bodies suffer less from a shortage of funds than from a shortage of the will to raise them — the own-source-revenue problem at the heart of municipal finance.
Intro (event): The Sixteenth Finance Commission's entry-level conditions and performance grants seek to incentivise cities to raise their own revenue, not merely receive transfers.
H1 — The own-source revenue problem:
H2 — FC grants & reform levers:
▪ Weak OSR: under-collected property tax, user charges, low buoyancy.
▪ 16th FC local grants: entry conditions (accounts, audit) + performance grants.
▪ Political reluctance to tax local constituents.
▪ Property-tax reform: GIS mapping, unit-area assessment.
▪ Dependence on transfers and grants erodes autonomy.
▪ Municipal bonds & pooled finance: Ex: Ahmedabad, Pune bonds.
▪ Untapped bases: land-value capture, betterment levy, professional tax.
▪ Land-value capture & TDR for infrastructure.
▪ Theory-bridge: fiscal decentralisation; subsidiarity.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: State Finance Commission neglect; 15th FC audited- accounts condition.
Way Forward: reform property tax with GIS and unit-area methods · tie grants to OSR performance · unlock municipal bonds and land-value capture; empower SFCs.
Conclusion: Municipal finance fails on will more than want — conditioning grants on own-revenue effort and reforming property tax is the route to fiscally autonomous, capable cities.
T40 · Metropolitan Governance, New Localism & Urban Resilience (covers Q98, Q99, Q102) [15m]
Intro (concept): The metropolis is planned by everyone and governed by no one — institutional fragmentation leaves the Metropolitan Planning Committee a paper body in most cities.

Intro (concept): Globalisation has made cities the new theatres of governance , straining the 'local' against the 'global' — even as recurrent urban flooding exposes governance failure more than nature's fury .
H1 — Metropolitan governance & fragmentation:
H2 — Urban resilience & climate:
▪ MPCs (Art 243ZE): to integrate metropolitan planning — largely non- functional.
▪ Urban flooding: encroached drains, unplanned growth, weak SWM. Ex: Bengaluru, Chennai, Mumbai.
▪ Multiplicity of agencies: development authorities, parastatals, utilities — no single governor.
▪ Governance failure > natural fury: fragmented planning, poor enforcement.
▪ Governed by no one: overlapping jurisdictions, no metropolitan authority.
▪ Climate-resilient cities: Ex: heat action plans, sponge-city concepts.
▪ Global-local strain: world-class-city aspirations vs local accountability.
▪ Disaster-resilient infrastructure and land-use control.
▪ New localism: empowered, networked local governance. (→ networked governance).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: CDRI; NDMA urban-flood guidelines.
Way Forward: operationalise MPCs as real metropolitan authorities · integrate fragmented agencies · mainstream climate-resilience and drainage in city planning.
Conclusion: The metropolis is everyone's plan and no one's government — new localism and functioning MPCs are needed to govern, and to make Indian cities resilient to a changing climate.
13 · LAW AND ORDER ADMINISTRATION
T41 · Colonial Legacy & Police Reform (covers Q104, Q105, Q109) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed

Intro (concept): Indian policing still wears a uniform stitched in 1861 — the Police Act of 1861 was written to secure the ruler, not to serve the citizen , and its unfinished replacement is the story of police reform.
Intro (concept): Police reform in India has become a matter of litigation rather than legislation — the courts, not the legislature, driving change since Prakash Singh .
H1 — The 1861 legacy & its problems:
H2 — Reform: litigation over legislation:
▪ Police Act 1861: post-1857 control instrument; ruler-serving, not citizen- serving.
▪ National Police Commission (1977–81): 8 reports, Model Police Act — largely un-adopted.
▪ Colonial features: politically subservient, coercive, non-accountable.
▪ Prakash Singh (2006): 7 SC directives — SSC, DGP tenure, PEB, PCA, separation of investigation.
▪ Ills: politicisation, poor investigation, custodial abuse, understaffing.
▪ Litigation-driven: courts fill the legislative vacuum.
▪ Order vs service orientation persists.
▪ State inaction: partial, diluted compliance.
▪ Theory-bridge: Weberian legal-rational ideal vs colonial reality.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: Model Police Act 2006; state police-act reforms.
Way Forward: enact modern state Police Acts implementing Prakash Singh directives · separate investigation from law-and-order · insulate postings via Security Commissions.
Conclusion: Indian policing is governed by a colonial statute and reformed only by litigation — genuine reform needs the legislature to finally deliver what the courts have long directed.
T42 · Prakash Singh, New Criminal Laws & Community Policing (covers Q106, Q107, Q108) [15m]

Intro (event): Police reform now runs on two tracks — judicially mandated structural reform ( Prakash Singh ) and legislatively mandated procedural reform (the new criminal laws) — reshaping policing around statutory timelines and technology.
Intro (concept): Police–public relations and community policing are the softer, trust-building complement to structural and procedural reform.
H1 — Two-track reform:
H2 — Community policing & trust:
▪ Structural ( Prakash Singh , 2006): SSC, fixed DGP tenure, PEB, Police Complaints Authority.
▪ Community policing: citizen partnership, beat-level engagement. Ex: Janamaithri (Kerala), friends-of-police.
▪ Procedural (new criminal laws, 2023): Ex: BNS, BNSS, BSA — effective July
▪ Police–public relations: trust, accessibility, sensitivity.
▪ SMART policing (Strict-Sensitive, Modern-Mobile, Alert-Accountable, Reliable-Responsive, Tech-Trained).
▪ Technology & timelines: e-FIR, forensic mandates, videography, statutory deadlines.
▪ Grievance & transparency mechanisms.
▪ Victim-centric & tech-driven investigation.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: women's help-desks, cyber-crime units.
▪ Theory-bridge: rule of law + due process ( administrative law ).
Way Forward: implement structural directives alongside the new procedural codes · build forensic and tech capacity · institutionalise community policing and trust.
Conclusion: Police reform now advances on judicial-structural and legislative-procedural tracks — but technology and codes deliver justice only when paired with community trust and accountability.
T43 · Investigative/Paramilitary Agencies & Criminalisation of Politics (covers Q110, Q111) [15m]

Intro (concept): Central investigative and para-military agencies are indispensable to countering insurgency and terrorism, yet raise federal concerns over autonomy and encroachment on the State's police domain.
Intro (concept): Criminalisation of politics corrodes administration — the nexus of crime, politics and bureaucracy that the Vohra Committee first mapped.
H1 — Central agencies & federal concerns:
H2 — Criminalisation of politics & administration:
▪ CBI (DSPE Act 1946): needs State consent; 'caged parrot' autonomy critique.
▪ Crime–politics–bureaucracy nexus: Ex: Vohra Committee (1993).
▪ NIA, ED, IB: counter-terror and financial-crime reach.
▪ Muscle & money power distort administration.
▪ CAPFs (CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB): insurgency, border, internal security.
▪ Judicial response: Ex: disclosure of criminal antecedents; SC directions on speedy trials of MPs/MLAs.
▪ Federal friction: general-consent withdrawal; law-and-order is a State subject.
▪ Electoral & administrative capture by tainted actors.
▪ Theory-bridge: unitary-bias federalism; accountability of agencies.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: ADR data; decriminalisation and fast-track courts.
Way Forward: guarantee agency autonomy with accountability · resolve CBI-consent friction via statute · decriminalise politics through speedy trials and disclosure.
Conclusion: Central agencies secure the nation but strain federalism , and criminalisation corrodes administration from within — both demand autonomy paired with accountability and the rule of law.
14 · SIGNIFICANT ISSUES IN INDIAN ADMINISTRATION
T44 · Anti-Corruption Architecture — CVC, CBI, Lokpal & Lokayukta (covers Q114, Q117) [20m] ★ cross-confirmed
Intro (concept): India's anti-corruption architecture suffers from overlapping jurisdiction and underlapping autonomy — many bodies, little coordinated independence.
Intro (institution): The Lokpal — an anti-corruption ombudsman long demanded — is tested by the gap between its statutory promise and operational performance .
H1 — The architecture & its fault-lines:
H2 — Lokpal promise vs performance & reform:
▪ CVC (2003 Act; Santhanam , 1964): apex vigilance; advisory, no prosecution power.
▪ Delayed operationalisation: appointments and infrastructure lagged.
▪ CBI (DSPE 1946): premier investigator; consent and autonomy issues.
▪ Prosecution dependence on CBI/agencies.
▪ Lokpal (2013 Act): ombudsman for public servants incl. PM (with safeguards).
▪ §17A PC Act (2018): prior sanction to investigate — protection vs shield debate.
▪ Lokayuktas: State-level, uneven powers and vacancies.
▪ Coordination deficit across CVC-CBI-Lokpal-ED.
▪ Overlap + underlap: jurisdictions collide; autonomy and resources thin.
▪ Theory-bridge: accountability & control; integrity institutions.
Way Forward: grant investigative autonomy and resources to integrity bodies · clarify jurisdiction and coordination · fill Lokpal/Lokayukta vacancies and empower prosecution.
Conclusion: India's anti-corruption bodies are many but fragmented — real accountability needs coordinated jurisdiction and genuine autonomy, so the Lokpal's promise is matched by performance.
T45 · National Commissions, NHRC & Vulnerable Sections (covers Q112, Q120) [20m]
Intro (concept): The proliferation of national commissions for the disadvantaged has multiplied advocacy without guaranteeing accountability — voice without enforceable teeth.
Intro (concept): The NHRC is a recommendatory body without teeth — its role in administrative accountability constrained by advisory powers and structural limits.
H1 — Commissions for vulnerable sections:
H2 — NHRC & the teeth deficit:
▪ Constitutional bodies: NCSC (338), NCST (338A), NCBC (338B, 102nd CAA).
▪ NHRC (PHRA 1993): inquiry, recommendation, not enforcement.
▪ Statutory: NCW (1990), NCPCR — advocacy and inquiry.
▪ Recommendatory limits: cannot compel; armed-forces jurisdiction curtailed.
▪ Advocacy without accountability: recommendatory, under-resourced.
▪ GANHRI 'A' status: Ex: recent deferment over autonomy/pluralism concerns.
▪ Overlap & vacancies blunt effectiveness.
▪ Appointment & independence questions.
▪ Theory-bridge: representative bureaucracy; social equity ( NPA ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: enforce recommendations, diversify membership.
Way Forward: give commissions defined powers and resources, not just advocacy · strengthen NHRC autonomy and follow-up · rationalise overlaps and fill vacancies.
Conclusion: National commissions and the NHRC give the vulnerable a voice but not yet enforceable protection — accountability requires teeth, autonomy and resources, not merely another institution.
T46 · Regulatory Commissions & Judicial Administration (covers Q118, Q113) [15m]
Intro (concept): Regulatory commissions are the new frontier of the administrative state — expert, insulated bodies whose autonomy is perennially threatened by government and captured interests.
Intro (concept): The largest arrear in Indian governance is the arrear of justice — the crisis of judicial administration a governance failure as much as a legal one.
H1 — Regulatory commissions & autonomy:
H2 — Judicial administration & arrears:
▪ Rise of the regulatory state: Ex: SEBI, TRAI, CCI, CERC, RERA.
▪ Arrears crisis: 5+ crore pending cases; delay denies justice.
▪ Fused functions: rule-making + adjudication + enforcement ('fourth branch').
▪ Causes: vacancies, low judge-population ratio, procedural delay.
▪ Threats to autonomy: government direction, appointments, funding, capture.
▪ Reforms: Ex: e-Courts, NJDG, fast-track & commercial courts, ADR/Lok Adalats.
▪ Accountability gap: unelected power needs parliamentary/judicial check.
▪ Judicial infrastructure & case-management deficits.
▪ Theory-bridge: independent regulatory authorities ( organisations ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: NALSA, mediation (2023 Act), All-India Judicial Service debate.
Way Forward: insulate regulators while subjecting them to review · fill judicial vacancies and expand ADR · digitise and reform case-management to cut arrears.
Conclusion: Regulatory commissions and courts are both frontiers of the administrative state — one needs autonomy with accountability , the other capacity to end the arrear of justice .
T47 · Disaster Management (covers Q115, Q119) [10m]

Intro (event): The amended disaster-management law consolidates planning authority at the apex, while disaster response remains an intensely local act — a centralisation–localisation tension at the heart of the framework.
Intro (institution): The Disaster Management Act, 2005 and the NDMA built a top-down institutional architecture; the challenge is to empower the local first-responder within it.
H1 — The DM framework:
H2 — Centralisation–localisation tension:
▪ DM Act 2005: NDMA (PM-chaired), SDMA, DDMA — three-tier structure.
▪ Apex planning consolidation vs local response reality.
▪ Shift: relief-centric → prevention, mitigation, preparedness, resilience.
▪ District Collector/DDMA as the operational pivot.
▪ NDRF & SDRF response forces; NIDM training.
▪ Community-based DM & early warning: Ex: cyclone preparedness, heat action plans.
▪ DM Amendment (2024/25): strengthens plans and apex authority.
▪ Climate-linked disasters rising: Ex: Wayanad landslides (2024), urban floods.
▪ Theory-bridge: disaster-management cycle; crisis coordination.
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: CDRI; mainstreaming DM into development.
Way Forward: empower districts and communities as first responders · mainstream mitigation into development · fund and localise early-warning and resilience.
Conclusion: Disaster management plans at the apex but responds at the ground — an effective framework consolidates strategy centrally while empowering the district and community to act locally.

＋ 2026 ammo (T47): DM (Amendment) Act 2025 specifics — Urban Disaster Management Authorities for State capitals & million-plus cities; statutory disaster databases; NDMA/SDMA to prepare their own plans; NCMC statutory — pair with Wayanad (2024) & heatwave-notification debate.
T48 · Civil Society, NGOs & FCRA (covers Q116) [15m]

Intro (event): The regulatory tightening of foreign contributions (FCRA) has redrawn the boundary between the state and civil society — with real implications for voluntary organisations in development administration.
Intro (concept): NGOs and civil society are partners in development and watchdogs of the state — a relationship of collaboration and contestation the FCRA regime has recalibrated.
H1 — Civil society in development administration:
H2 — FCRA & the redrawn boundary:
▪ Roles: service delivery, advocacy, watchdog, last-mile reach.
▪ FCRA (2010; 2020 amendment): tighter foreign-funding rules, SBI-Delhi account, no sub-granting.
▪ Partners & critics: collaborate on schemes, hold state accountable.
▪ Compliance burden: registrations lapsed/cancelled; funding squeezed.
▪ Social capital & participation: Ex: SHGs, MKSS/RTI origins, social audit.
▪ State–civil-society boundary redrawn toward control.
▪ Complement state capacity in remote and marginalised areas.
▪ Sovereignty vs space: national-interest concerns vs shrinking civic space.
▪ Theory-bridge: networked governance; social capital ( Putnam ).
▪ Indian bridge: Ex: NITI Aayog NGO-Darpan; self-regulation debate.
Way Forward: balance national-security scrutiny with civic space · ease compliance for genuine voluntary bodies · strengthen domestic funding and self- regulation.
Conclusion: Civil society is both partner and watchdog of development administration — FCRA's tightening must guard sovereignty without shrinking the civic space that democratic governance needs.
